faq.answer: Nc'Nean deliberately avoids chill filtration and artificial coloring in this whisky to preserve its natural aromas and original color. This approach keeps the character unadulterated and allows for a more intense sensory experience.

faq.question: How does the sherry finish influence the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: The sherry finish brings forward fruity notes like strawberries and red fruit syrup, imparting a gentle sweetness to the whisky. At the same time, it complements the floral and herbal aromas, creating a complex and well-balanced flavor profile.

faq.question: What role does sustainability play in the production of the Nc'Nean Huntress Lemon Meadow?
faq.answer: Nc'Nean places great emphasis on sustainability, reflected in environmentally friendly production methods such as the use of renewable energy and recyclable materials. This not only supports environmental protection but also shapes the philosophy behind the whisky.

item.name: Caperdonich 27 Years (2024) The Tasteful 8 - Brühler Whiskyhaus
item.description: The Caperdonich Distillery is one of the vanished legends of the Scotch whisky world. Closed in 2002 and finally demolished in 2011, its bottlings are now coveted rarities.

image.alt: Caperdonich 2024 27 Years The Tasteful 8 - Brühler Whiskyhaus

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression is soft and harmonious. Notes of honey and vanilla dominate and give the whisky a pleasant sweetness. These are joined by toffee nuances reminiscent of butter caramel and a clear malty depth that emphasises the long maturation. A subtle nutty note resonates and lends structure, but tends to remain in the background. The alcohol is well integrated and does not push itself to the fore.
tasting_notes.palate: The palate reveals a pleasant balance between sweetness and spice. Caramel and Christmas spices open up the flavours, accompanied by a tangy liveliness that is almost unexpected for a 27-years. A slightly perfumed note shimmers through - a hint of floral elegance without being overpowering. The texture is medium dense, smooth, but not exceptionally oily.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is intense and spicy, with herbal and spicy notes gaining the upper hand. A clear oak presence ensures a dry but not overloaded finish. The flavours linger for a long time, but lack the final kick that characterises a truly exceptional whisky.

conclusion.marcel: A well-rounded, sweet Speysider that’s simply enjoyable with its gentle honey notes and festive spices. For a 27-years rarity, it may lack that certain something, but I’d still be happy to pour it again on the next fireside evening—simply because it drinks smoothly and doesn’t try to be complicated.
conclusion.sascha: The 27-years Caperdonich release shows a solid balance between sweetness and spice, with a structured oak influence in the finish. Despite its elegant harmony, the whisky lacks a distinctive profile that sets it apart from other Speyside distillates, making it feel more like a reliable representative than a standout highlight.

faq.question: What makes the Caperdonich 2024 27 Years The Tasteful 8 from a closed distillery so special?
faq.answer: The Caperdonich distillery was closed in 2002 and demolished in 2011, making its whiskies rare collector’s items. This release is special because it combines the typical Speyside character with a lengthy 27-year maturation, showcasing notes of honey, vanilla, and toffee.

faq.question: How does the high drinking strength of 52.1% ABV affect the tasting experience of this whisky?
faq.answer: At 52.1% alcohol, the whisky delivers an intense aroma without harshness. The alcohol is well integrated, enhancing the sweet and spicy notes like caramel and Christmas spices, while providing a lively texture.

faq.question: What flavor notes are typical for the Caperdonich 2024 27 Years The Tasteful 8 and how do they develop on the palate?
faq.answer: On the nose, the whisky presents a harmonious blend of honey, vanilla, toffee, and malty depth. On the palate, sweetness and spice combine with caramel, floral hints, and a touch of Christmas spices. The finish is long, spicy, and marked by a pronounced oak presence.

item.name: St. Kilian Heavy Metal Edition Birmingham – Home of Metal – Unpeated
item.description: Black Sabbath. Judas Priest. Heavy riffs. Flamethrowers. And now also: whisky. With the limited Heavy Metal Edition ‘Birmingham - Home of Metal’, St. Kilian is bringing a single malt to the stage that is just as uncompromising as the music to which it is dedicated. No smoke, no age statement, but 60.6% cask strength by volume and a load of virgin oak.

image.alt: St. Kilian Heavy Metal Edition Birmingham – Home of Metal – Unpeated

tasting_notes.nose: Vanilla and caramel like a southern dessert, served on glowing wood. The typical Virgin Oak punch is unmistakable: sweet, warm, slightly burnt, like the last chord after a guitar feedback. And there is also that slightly ashy touch, without any smoke or peat.
tasting_notes.palate: Powerful, oily, sweet - but not sticky. The refined sugar takes over the solo, accompanied by subtle red fruits that reveal themselves surprisingly - above all because no pre-seasoned barrels were at work here. This is Virgin Oak in its purest form, but with a deep groove - not just sweet, but with structure, drive and a hint of floral harmony, probably thanks to the wild service tree.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium-long, round, caramelly and slightly bitter from the wood - like a final, well-placed power chord that lingers in the room. No afterburn, no chaos - simply clean, rich and strong craftsmanship.

conclusion.marcel: This St. Kilian Heavy Metal Edition is like a powerful guitar riff in a glass – direct, sweet, and with enough edges to keep it interesting. Virgin Oak sets the tone, but the subtle service tree note provides the groove that makes it exciting. For fans who don’t need smoke but love character.
conclusion.sascha: A technically impressive single malt that shines with its clear Virgin Oak note and subtle wood bitterness. The balance between sweet vanilla, delicate fruitiness, and precise wood influence creates a convincing structure that doesn’t feel overwhelmed despite the high cask strength. A solid example of powerful yet controlled wood character without artificial sherry influences.

faq.question: Why is the St. Kilian Heavy Metal Edition Birmingham unpeated, even though it feels so powerful?
faq.answer: The whisky deliberately avoids smoke and peat to highlight the Virgin Oak cask maturation and the natural aromas of vanilla, caramel, and red fruits. The power comes from the cask strength of 60.6% ABV and the intense wood character, not from smoke.

faq.question: What does Virgin Oak mean and how does it influence the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: Virgin Oak refers to the use of new, unused oak casks for maturation. These impart particularly intense aromas of wood, vanilla, and caramel, giving the whisky its distinctive, warm, and slightly toasted character.

faq.question: Why does this whisky have no age statement and how does that affect its quality?
faq.answer: Omitting an age statement allows St. Kilian to blend different vintages and maturation stages to achieve a specific flavor profile. Quality here is ensured through cask selection, maturation process, and cask strength, rather than age alone.

item.name: Allt-a-Bhainne (2008/2019) Scottish Roadsign Edition No. 11 - Flickenschild
item.description: The Allt-a-Bhainne Distillery, more of a hidden champion of the Speyside region, surprises time and again with unique bottlings. The Scottish Roadsign Edition No. 11 from independent bottler Flickenschild brings an exciting side of this distillery to light - raw, powerful and surprisingly nuanced at the same time.

image.alt: Allt-a-Bhainne 2008/2019 Scottish Roadsign Edition No. 11 - Flickenschild

tasting_notes.nose: The very first sniff reveals that this whisky is not for the faint-hearted. Lovage literally jumps into the nose and is reminiscent of a savoury kitchen aroma. This is accompanied by unusually distinctive notes of tobacco, followed by a softer sweetness reminiscent of caramel and rape honey. Delicate hints of toasted bread and a slightly cakey fragrance resonate in the background, giving the whole a warm base.
tasting_notes.palate: A veritable rollercoaster unfolds on the palate. The entry is intense and direct - fruitcake with a hint of apricot, before a wild shift to maritime notes. Dried seaweed, a hint of iodine and even something reminiscent of wall paint give the whisky an industrial, almost wild flavour. Mint and heather add a refreshing complexity that gives the character depth. Particularly exciting is the slightly chemical impression of thinner, which blends surprisingly well into the overall picture.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is rather short, but concise. A sweetish spiciness lingers on the tongue, like a fleeting moment that lingers in the memory longer than you initially expect.

conclusion.marcel: The Allt-a-Bhainne 2008/2019 Scottish Roadsign Edition No. 11 is a bold character whisky that really delights with its rough, maritime notes and sharp spiciness. Not a whisky for the faint-hearted, but a genuine pleasure for adventurers and lovers of unusual flavors. Perfect paired with caramelized nuts or smoked salmon – a dramatic tasting experience you won’t soon forget.
conclusion.sascha: The Scottish Roadsign Edition No. 11 from Allt-a-Bhainne impresses with its complex balance between maritime, industrial, and sweet elements. Its clear structure, featuring nuances of lovage, caramel, and a surprising chemical note, makes it an exciting Speyside whisky full of character. Due to its intense aromatic profile, this bottling is especially recommended alongside hearty dishes like smoked salmon or spicy rye bread.

faq.question: What makes the Scottish Roadsign Edition No. 11 from Allt-a-Bhainne special compared to other bottlings?
faq.answer: This edition stands out due to its unusual combination of intense maritime and slightly chemical notes, which are rare in the Speyside region. The whisky offers a complex aromatic spectrum ranging from lovage and tobacco to maritime seaweed and iodine notes, complemented by a high drinking strength of 59.1% ABV.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 59.1% affect the taste and drinking experience?
faq.answer: The high alcohol content delivers an intense aromatic profile and a powerful entry on the palate. It amplifies distinctive notes such as tobacco, maritime iodine, and minty herbal aromas. At the same time, the strength can make the whisky feel a bit rougher, which is why a drop of water often rounds out the tasting experience.

faq.question: Why are chemical impressions like thinner described in the tasting notes, and is this a quality defect?
faq.answer: Such unusual, chemical nuances are not quality defects but rather expressions of complex maturation and cask selection, especially in some independent bottlings and rougher, unconventional whiskies. Here, they surprisingly integrate well into the overall profile, giving the whisky a unique, almost industrial character.

item.name: St. Kilian Signature Edition Fourteen – Unpeated
item.description: With the Signature Edition FOURTEEN, the St. Kilian Distillery is launching a single malt on the market that is deliberately dedicated to the influence of fine French cask art. This limited bottling combines distillates that have matured for between three and seven years in a selection of exceptional casks - including Rhum Agricole, Armagnac, Cognac, Sauternes, Margaux red wine and Champagne Vin Clair.

image.alt: St. Kilian Signature Edition Fourteen – Unpeated

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression is clear: fruity sweetness in its purest form. Peach rings, passion fruit, lychee and juicy pear form a tropical combination of flavours reminiscent of fine dessert wines - no wonder given the variety of barrels. There is also creamy vanilla, delicate cream caramel and a hint of crème brûlée. The nose is very soft, round and inviting without appearing arbitrary - an interplay that makes you want more.
tasting_notes.palate: The first impression is confirmed on the palate: buttery and creamy, with ripe fruit flavours of flat peach and kiwi and a restrained vinosity that is particularly evident in the middle section. The different barrel types do not work against each other here, but with each other - they create depth, but not chaos. The texture is dense and smooth, the sweetness remains present but never intrusive. A perfectly balanced interplay of fruit, vanilla and subtle oak spice.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium-long, harmonious and very balanced. A soft oak flavour, accompanied by fine vanilla and a hint of caramel, remains pleasant on the palate. A slight tingle - the famous ‘St. Kilian funk’ - provides character and recognition.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that’s fun and full of character without trying too hard – the FOURTEEN invites you to relax and enjoy its fruity-sweet richness. The French casks give it a special charm that piques curiosity for more, without overwhelming the palate. For connoisseurs seeking an approachable premium single malt with personality, this is the perfect choice.
conclusion.sascha: With the FOURTEEN, St. Kilian achieves a remarkably balanced cask composition that creates depth without compromising harmony. The seamless integration of Rhum Agricole, Armagnac, and others delivers a structured complexity offering both intricacy and clarity. A technically impressive single malt that demonstrates how cask selection and maturation time can precisely shape character.

faq.question: How do the different types of French casks influence the flavor of the St. Kilian Signature Edition Fourteen – Unpeated?
faq.answer: The combination of Rhum Agricole, Armagnac, Cognac, Sauternes, Margaux red wine, and Champagne Vin Clair casks imparts complex fruit notes such as peach, passion fruit, and lychee, along with a creamy vanilla and caramel texture. This variety of casks provides depth and a harmonious interplay without any single aroma dominating.

faq.question: Why is the St. Kilian Signature Edition Fourteen labeled 'Unpeated,' and how does this affect the aroma?
faq.answer: 'Unpeated' means the whisky was made without using peated malt. As a result, it lacks the smoky or medicinal notes typically found in peated whiskies. Instead, the focus is on fruity sweetness and cask complexity, leading to a soft, rounded, and inviting aroma.

faq.question: What is the alcohol content, and how should the Signature Edition Fourteen be best enjoyed?
faq.answer: The alcohol content is 49.8% ABV. This strength allows for an intense flavor experience while remaining very drinkable. For optimal tasting, it’s recommended to enjoy the whisky neat or with a few drops of water to fully reveal the complex fruit and cask notes.

item.name: Octomore 13 Years (2024) The Tasteful 8 - Brühler Whiskyhaus
item.description: Octomore - the name is legendary. A synonym for peaty-smoky excesses and at the same time proof that smoke is not just smoke. Year after year, fans wait for new bottlings, experiments with cask maturation, ageing and intense phenol power.

image.alt: Octomore 2024 13 Years The Tasteful 8 - Brühler Whiskyhaus

tasting_notes.nose: This whisky takes no prisoners. A clear cereal note mixes with a strong ash flavour reminiscent of a campfire that has gone out. Phenolic, yes - but not overwhelming. Instead, there is a certain maturity, a depth that you don't find in every Octomore. No cutting iodine hit, but a dark, almost oily smokiness.
tasting_notes.palate: One sip and the journey begins: clear, direct and intensely smoky. Anyone who knows Octomore knows that the smoke is not hidden here - it takes centre stage. However, it is not just smoke, but also a surprising sweetness that accompanies the start. This note is almost syrupy on the tongue before being replaced by a slightly citrusy freshness. However, the real highlight is the mouthfeel: clearly astringent - as if the whisky is drawing all moisture out of the mouth and leaving its mark. The high alcohol strength is evident here, but it remains well integrated.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is, as expected, long and intense. Once again, a wave of smoke rolls over the tongue, this time with a fine, slightly peppery spiciness reminiscent of white pepper. The finish is dry but warm.

conclusion.marcel: This Octomore 13 Years is a genuine experience for anyone who loves smoky power with a touch of sweetness and character. The intense cloud of smoke is perfectly complemented by a pleasant freshness and a dry, warm finish – a whisky that leaves you wanting more without putting on airs.
conclusion.sascha: The complex balance of profound phenol intensity and delicate sweetness makes this 13-years Octomore bottling a technically impressive Islay whisky. Well-integrated 58.1% ABV and a long, peppery finish underline its structured maturity, even if the astringency doesn’t equally please every palate.

faq.question: How does the Octomore 2024 13 Years The Tasteful 8 differ from other peated whiskies?
faq.answer: The Octomore 2024 13 Years The Tasteful 8 is characterized by an intense yet mature smokiness that is less sharp and iodine-heavy than many other peated whiskies. Instead, it offers a dark, oily smoke note combined with a surprising sweetness and a complex phenol structure.

faq.question: What role does the 58.1% alcohol strength play in the tasting experience of this whisky?
faq.answer: The high alcohol strength of 58.1% provides an intense mouthfeel that is slightly astringent and leaves a certain dryness. At the same time, the alcohol is well integrated, allowing the smoky and sweet aromas to come through clearly without being overpowering.

faq.question: How does the 13-year maturation influence the character of the Octomore 2024 The Tasteful 8?
faq.answer: The 13-year maturation gives the whisky a remarkable depth and complexity often missing in younger Octomore releases. The smokiness becomes less sharp, while subtle notes of grain, sweetness, and a lightly citrusy freshness develop, giving the whisky its balance.

item.name: Nc'Nean (2017/2024) STR Cask - Best Dram
item.description: Nc'Nean is a young distillery from the Scottish Highlands that is known for its sustainable and modern approach. But what happens when an independent bottler like Best Dram takes the distillate and puts it in an STR (Shaved, Toasted, Re-charred) cask?

tasting_notes.nose: From the very first nosing, it is clear that this is a fruity and flavoursome spirit. A frozen berry mix opens the aroma - strawberries, raspberries and blueberries dance in the nose and bring a sweet and sour freshness with them. This is joined by the scent of fresh wood, reminiscent of a carpenter's workshop. A hint of sugar rounds off the whole, while spicy citrus notes and a good portion of hazelnut provide depth.
tasting_notes.palate: The intensity continues in the mouth. The oak comes through emphatically - dark, powerful and full of presence. But it is accompanied by a rich fruitiness: dark berries, plums and a fine hint of cinnamon combine harmoniously. The spices provide a pleasant warmth, while the fruit gives the whisky a juicy, almost marmalade-like depth. Despite its power, it remains surprisingly accessible, with a beautiful balance between strength and elegance.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long and impressive. The dark wood notes linger on the tongue, accompanied by a hint of spicy oak and a hint of berries. A subtle sweetness remains, allowing the whisky to finish round and pleasant.

conclusion.marcel: This Nc'Nean 2017/2024 STR Cask captivates with its lively berry notes and spicy wood character, making it a genuine pleasure to drink. The combination of freshness and powerful cask spice delivers personality without overwhelming — a dramatic dance between modernity and tradition that’s simply fun.
conclusion.sascha: With precise cask selection, this Nc'Nean demonstrates a remarkable balance between intense oak spice and juicy fruit richness. Its structure impresses with harmonious spice notes and a long, complex finish, presenting itself as a technically refined, modern Highland whisky.

faq.question: What does STR Cask mean and how does it influence the flavor of the Nc'Nean 2017/2024?
faq.answer: STR stands for Shaved, Toasted, Re-charred — a cask treatment where the wood is first shaved, then toasted, and finally re-charred. This process intensifies the wood aromas and imparts the whisky with a complex combination of spicy, smoky, and fruity notes, which are clearly evident in the Nc'Nean 2017/2024.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 54.3% affect the drinking experience?
faq.answer: At 54.3% ABV, the whisky offers a robust presence that supports the intense fruit and wood notes. Many enthusiasts recommend adding a few drops of water to open up the aromas and better perceive the balance between power and elegance.

faq.question: Why is Nc'Nean particularly known for sustainability as a distillery?
faq.answer: Nc'Nean follows an environmentally conscious approach in production, including the use of renewable energy, organically grown raw materials, and minimal packaging. This sustainable philosophy makes the brand a pioneer among the young Scottish distilleries.

item.name: Glen Elgin (2008/2020) - Sansibar
item.description: Sometimes you come across a whisky that makes you wonder: ‘Why exactly was this bottled?’ The Glen Elgin 2008/2020 from Zanzibar is such a case. With a whopping 58.7% ABV, it comes across as a powerhouse - but unfortunately there is little substance behind the impressive figure.

tasting_notes.nose: To be honest: the nose gives hope. Nougat meets vanilla, plus an interesting herbal note with sage and a hint of herbal tea. There is also a hint of pear, which adds a certain freshness. Everything has been good so far - but unfortunately it doesn't stop there.
tasting_notes.palate: Sobering. Yes, there is vanilla. Yes, it's creamy. But then? Then... Nothing. Thin on the palate, as if someone had forgotten to fill it with flavours. A little light fruity sweetness, but overall? Boring.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish? Juicy, slightly vanilla-like, a hint of oak. Sounds nice, but doesn't linger. It's gone as quickly as it comes - as if it's in a hurry to shirk its responsibilities.

conclusion.marcel: At first glance, the Glen Elgin 2008/2020 scores points with its robust alcohol strength, but unfortunately, it proves to be a disappointment in terms of flavor. Where are the exciting aromas that invite you to enjoy? For anyone looking for a full-bodied whisky, this one is rather a letdown – not even a dash of vanilla can save it.
conclusion.sascha: Despite a solid 58.7% ABV, the Glen Elgin 2008/2020 shows an unbalanced aroma profile with a weak flavor and a flat finish. The promised complexity is missing, making the whisky noticeably weaker in terms of balance and depth than expected. For lovers of well-balanced Speyside whiskies, this bottling is hardly recommendable.

faq.question: Why does the Glen Elgin 2008/2020 have little flavor depth despite its high alcohol content?
faq.answer: Although the Glen Elgin 2008/2020 is very strong at 58.7% ABV, it lacks flavor complexity. The high alcohol tends to overpower rather than enhance the aromas, resulting in a thin and rather uninspiring tasting experience.

faq.question: How does the Glen Elgin 2008/2020 differ from other Glen Elgin releases?
faq.answer: Compared to other Glen Elgin releases, this Sansibar edition stands out due to its very high alcohol content and the absence of an age statement. Flavor-wise, it is less complex and more powerful than layered, distinguishing it from the classic, matured expressions.

faq.question: What aromas can be expected from the Glen Elgin 2008/2020 and how long do they last?
faq.answer: On the nose, nougat, vanilla, herbs like sage, and a fresh pear note dominate. On the palate, vanilla and a light, bright fruit sweetness appear, but it lacks intensity. The finish is short with vanilla and woody nuances that fade quickly.

item.name: Ledaig 16 Years (2005/2022) - Signatory Vintage
item.description: Ledaig - the smoky, wild brother of the Tobermory Distillery on the Isle of Mull.Anyone who knows Ledaig knows that there are no half measures here.The whisky is often intense, maritime and dirty - a kind of Islay alternative with its very own twist. The Ledaig 2005/2022 from Signatory Vintage takes this to the extreme.16 years of maturation and a stable 66.1% ABV - this is no gentle introduction to the world of peated whisky, but a real monster.The question is: does it deliver what the figures promise?

tasting_notes.nose: It's clear from the first sniff: here comes a heavyweight.Bacon, barbecue smoke and maritime salt notes immediately dominate. It smells as if you are standing right next to a smoker grill on the coast, while the wind carries a mixture of charcoal and sea air. But there is more to this intense first impression. With a little time in the glass, BBQ sauce, honey and a hint of espresso unfold - a sweet and spicy depth that rounds off the flavour.The smoked almonds, which add a slightly nutty, oily note, are particularly exciting. And then there's that slightly earthy, dirty undertone that's so typical of Ledaig - as if you've just rolled your hands in wet peat. Fascinating and intense!
tasting_notes.palate: No surprise: at 66.1%, this whisky packs a punch. But despite the high alcohol strength, it is surprisingly complex. Voluminous, oily and powerful, it rolls over the tongue as if it were clawing its way into every corner of the mouth. The first impression is fruity and salty, almost as if you were tasting grilled peaches with a pinch of sea salt. But the BBQ wave sets in the very next moment: smoke, ham, charcoal, paired with a certain rubbery note and a slight hint of iodine. What makes it special: the flavours from the nose are perfectly reflected in the taste - nothing falls apart, nothing seems disharmonious. It does exactly what it is supposed to do, and with impressive intensity.
tasting_notes.finish: After such a brute start, one might expect a long, epic finish - but here the Ledaig surprises. Although the smoke remains noticeable, the finish is relatively short overall. What remains is a mixture of fruity and smoky notes, with a gentle echo of charcoal and salt.

conclusion.marcel: The Ledaig 2005/2022 is a wild ride for anyone who loves smoky power – the combo of BBQ, sea, and bacon really hits hard here. Despite a hefty 66.1%, it remains surprisingly approachable and just plain fun, even if the finish feels a bit too short. If you like it intense and full of character, this one will definitely deliver.
conclusion.sascha: This Ledaig impresses with its clear structure and the balance between maritime notes and smoky intensity at a proud 66.1% ABV. The aromas are precisely layered, the palate full-bodied and complex, with only the relatively short finish slightly detracting from the overall experience. A technically very well-crafted, robust single malt with a strong signature.

faq.question: What makes the Ledaig 2005/2022 16 Years from Signatory Vintage stand out compared to other peated whiskies?
faq.answer: The Ledaig 2005/2022 is distinguished by its combination of intense smokiness, maritime notes, and an unusually high alcohol strength of 66.1%. It blends the typical Islay character with a distinctive, sometimes earthy and nutty aroma profile that sets it apart from classic Islay whiskies.

faq.question: How should one drink the Ledaig 2005/2022 16 Years to best experience its aromas?
faq.answer: Due to its strong 66.1% ABV, it is recommended to dilute the whisky with a few drops of water. This helps unlock the complex aromas like BBQ sauce, honey, and smoked almonds, making the flavor more accessible without losing intensity.

faq.question: Why is the finish of the Ledaig 2005/2022 relatively short despite its powerful aromatic profile?
faq.answer: Although the Ledaig 2005/2022 scores with strong smoke and full flavor, the finish is comparatively short and crisp. This is because the whisky presents its aromas in a concentrated way without lingering in a long aftertaste, which for many fans offers an interesting contrast to other long-drawn Islay whiskies.

item.name: Craigellachie 10 Years (2012/2023) - Signatory Vintage
item.description: The Craigellachie Distillery is known for its strong, malty whiskies with plenty of structure and character. But in the hands of the independent bottler Signatory Vintage, it often becomes even more intense. At 67.4% ABV, this is a whisky that challenges connoisseurs, but also rewards them - with intense sherry flavours, dark fruits and impressive depth.

tasting_notes.nose: From the very first nosing, the sherry flavour literally leaps out at you. Sultanas and grilled bacon dates dominate the flavour, accompanied by a deep, leathery note reminiscent of fresh saddle leather. These are joined by chestnuts and a hint of cardamom, which add a spicy complexity. A malty bread crust also resonates, giving the whisky an additional savoury component. Although the high alcohol strength is noticeable, it does not burn aggressively, but carries the flavours with strength and elegance.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, Craigellachie is powerful and sweet, almost like a BBQ glaze on grilled meat. The sultana and date notes from the aroma continue, embedded in a strong leather structure. This is joined by a subtle roasted note reminiscent of slightly burnt sugar. Despite its strength, the whisky remains balanced - the cask gives the distillate a deep, savoury sweetness without overpowering it. With a little time in the glass, a slight dryness develops, rounding off the experience.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long, warming and complex. A combination of fruity sweetness and strong oak remains present for a long time, accompanied by a slightly bitter finish. Notes of red wine and a subtle spiciness provide additional depth. In the second wave, the whisky becomes somewhat drier, but always remains elegant and aromatic.

conclusion.marcel: A sherry powerhouse that really delights with its rugged edges and full blast of raisins and leather. Despite the hefty 67.4%, this Craigellachie is surprisingly approachable and develops a lovely roundness in the glass – perfect for those who enjoy their whisky intense and untamed.
conclusion.sascha: With a clear cask influence and remarkable balance between sweet sherry notes and a dry, austere character, this Craigellachie shows an impressive structure. The 67.4% ABV is well integrated, so despite its power, it never feels unbalanced but instead offers a complex and substantial profile.

faq.question: Why does the Craigellachie 2012/2023 with 67.4% alcohol not have an aggressive sharpness in taste?
faq.answer: Despite the high alcohol content of 67.4%, this whisky is carefully bottled to provide a balanced experience. The intense sherry cask maturation and complex aromas soften the sharpness, allowing the alcohol to carry the flavors without coming across as aggressive.

faq.question: How does maturation in sherry casks influence the flavor of this Craigellachie whisky?
faq.answer: The sherry cask maturation imparts robust aromas of raisins, dates, and dark fruits, along with a hearty sweetness. Additionally, complex notes such as leather, spices, and a subtle toastiness develop, giving the character depth and structure.

faq.question: What makes the Signatory Vintage bottling of this Craigellachie whisky special compared to the original release?
faq.answer: Signatory Vintage often selects more intense and characterful casks that make the whisky even more powerful and complex. For this Craigellachie, a cask strength of 67.4% was chosen, emphasizing the distillery’s typically robust style and adding extra depth through sherry cask notes.

item.name: GlenAllachie 12 Years (2009/2022) - Signatory Vintage
item.description: The Glenallachie Distillery is located in the Speyside region of Scotland and was founded in 1967. While for a long time it mainly produced blended whiskies, in recent years it has made a name for itself with independent bottlings and its own single malts. Glenallachie has gained a higher profile, particularly due to the influence of Billy Walker, and is known for its fruity, often sherried whiskies.

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression shows a clear sweetness: cream caramel, strawberries and light wine flavours characterise the bouquet. A hint of Campino sweets, cherry, caramel and raspberries give the whisky a creamy and fruity flavour. The wine character is present, but not too dominant.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the winey influence is more pronounced - albeit in a rather youthful and immature form. Strawberry sweetness meets a clear sulphur note. There is also a certain cardboardiness that makes the mouthfeel less elegant. The high alcohol content provides a strong but not optimally balanced structure.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish shows young oak notes, accompanied by a slightly tangy, almost sharp component. There is a lack of depth here, making the finish rather unbalanced and somewhat unpleasant.

conclusion.marcel: The GlenAllachie 2009/2022 certainly brings an enticing fruity sweetness to the glass, but the underdeveloped wine cask note and the unpleasant sulfur character noticeably detract from the drinking pleasure. For a dram to enjoy in good company, it simply lacks the character and depth one would expect – it leaves you wanting more.
conclusion.sascha: With its high alcohol content, the GlenAllachie 12 Years displays a youthful, unbalanced structure where the balance between fruitiness and sulfur note fails to come together. The lack of maturation depth and the sharp finish make this single malt feel underdeveloped despite some interesting elements.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 66.4% affect the taste of this GlenAllachie 12 Years?
faq.answer: The high alcohol content delivers a powerful and intense aromatic profile but can also make the mouthfeel sharp and unbalanced. It is advisable to dilute the whisky with a little water to better balance the flavors and soften the sulfur note.

faq.question: What role does the independent bottler Signatory Vintage play in this GlenAllachie release?
faq.answer: Signatory Vintage purchases casks directly from the distillery and bottles them without alteration. This approach often provides very distinctive and unadulterated insights into the whisky, as with this GlenAllachie 2009/2022, which faithfully showcases the youthful and fruity notes characteristic of the distillery.

faq.question: Why does this GlenAllachie, despite 12 years of maturation, have a rather youthful and underdeveloped flavor profile?
faq.answer: The combination of a relatively short maturation period and the strong influence of the wine cask results in a bold but not yet fully integrated flavor structure. The pronounced sulfur note and cardboard-like texture on the palate indicate that the whisky has not yet achieved full balance and depth.

item.name: Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02
item.description: With the AD/04.22:02 release, the young Highland distillery Ardnamurchan once again demonstrates its commitment to transparency. This single malt is composed of ex-bourbon, American and Spanish Oloroso, as well as American and Spanish PX casks from the 2015 and 2016 vintages. Bottled in 2022 at 46.8% ABV, it is non-chill filtered and natural in colour – in true Ardnamurchan fashion.

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression is shaped by cool smoke that doesn’t dominate but blends gently with other notes. Fresh popcorn and a subtle herbal character emerge, joined by a hint of chalk. A bright, fresh citrus note stands out prominently. A touch of vanilla adds roundness, while a faint red fruit note—reminiscent of raspberry—lingers in the background. The overall profile is pleasantly maritime.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the whisky comes across as fruity and fresh. Lime takes the lead, delivering a pronounced citrus impact. A subtle salty note adds depth, accompanied by a hint of minerality. The texture is creamy and soft. A gentle smoky layer floats through the mouth, enhancing the overall complexity without overpowering the palate.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium in length. Dark chocolate comes to the fore, paired with a pleasant spiciness. A note of bitter orange provides a mildly tangy conclusion.

conclusion.marcel: Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02 is a genuine pleasure, with its fresh citrus notes and gentle smoke immediately whetting the appetite for more. Despite its youth, it comes across as surprisingly rounded and inviting – a whisky that’s fun to drink yet still shows character.
conclusion.sascha: With clear cask documentation and precise aromatics, the Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02 impresses through its balanced harmony and structured complexity. The harmonious interplay of fruit, minerality, and delicate smoke highlights the technical maturity that you don’t always expect from a young Highland release.

faq.question: What are the special features of the cask maturation for the Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02?
faq.answer: The Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02 matured in a combination of ex-bourbon, American, as well as Spanish Oloroso and PX casks from the vintages 2015 and 2016. This variety of cask types creates a complex aroma profile with notes of vanilla, fruit, and spice.

faq.question: How does the smoke influence the flavor of the Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02?
faq.answer: The smoke in the Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02 is subtle and understated. It complements the fresh citrus and fruit notes without overpowering them, giving the whisky a pleasant maritime and lightly spicy depth.

faq.question: Why is the Ardnamurchan AD 04.22:02 bottled without coloring and chill filtration?
faq.answer: Bottling without coloring and chill filtration preserves the whisky’s natural aromas and texture. This ensures the character of the distillate remains unaltered, which is especially appreciated in high-quality single malts like this one.

item.name: HeiligenBergFeld (2020/2024) Rum Barbados Cask
item.description: German whisky is still a young discipline, but some distilleries are boldly venturing forward with creative cask maturations. HeiligenBergFeld is precisely one of these: Their latest bottling, the 2020/2024 Rum Barbados Cask, comes in at a whopping 60.2 % ABV and refuses to be pigeonholed into classic casks.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose? Let's say ... surprising. Toilet stone meets fresh pineapple - a contrast that is definitely not commonplace. This is joined by a malty base that hints at the young age of the whisky. It remains youthful and impetuous, with floral hints of jasmine blossom and a fresh acidity of Granny Smith apples. A flavour that will not appeal to everyone, but definitely arouses curiosity.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the whisky is initially sweet, almost tantalising, but then a metallic note comes into play, making the whole thing somewhat edgy. White pepper brings a slight spiciness, which is accompanied by a refreshing mint note. And then? Peach and citrus tic-tacs! Yes, exactly that. A flavour that balances between refreshing and strange - somewhere between a summer drink and a drawer full of coins.
tasting_notes.finish: This is where the HeiligenBergFeld finally turns up the heat. Crumbly lemon iced tea, a fine, crisp texture and a sweet, tropical note of papaya make for a smooth, pleasant finish. The finish is clearly the strongest side of this whisky.

conclusion.marcel: This HeiligenBergFeld 2020/2024 Rum Barbados Cask is like a lively teenager still trying to find its place – sometimes exciting, sometimes a bit rough around the edges. Especially the finish, with its sweet tropical notes, leaves you wanting more, even though the nose and palate could use a bit more harmony. Anyone up for an adventure with some quirks and character will definitely have fun here.
conclusion.sascha: The HeiligenBergFeld 2020/2024 Rum Barbados Cask showcases an interesting cask choice that imparts an unusual complexity to the young whisky, but unfortunately it falls short in terms of flavor balance. The metallic nuance and somewhat inconsistent structure weaken the overall impression, while the promising finish scores with a zesty lemon freshness. For lovers of unconventional German whiskies, it remains an intriguing, albeit still unrefined, option.

faq.question: How does the Rum Barbados Cask maturation influence the flavor of the HeiligenBergFeld 2020/2024?
faq.answer: Maturation in the Rum Barbados Cask gives the whisky a tropical sweetness expressed through aromas like pineapple, papaya, and citrus notes. At the same time, the cask adds a certain spiciness and an unusual metallic note, which lend the young whisky complexity and character.

faq.question: Why does the HeiligenBergFeld 2020/2024 have such an intense flavor despite its young age?
faq.answer: Although the whisky is young (no age statement), it is bottled at a high cask strength of 60.2% ABV. The combination of intense alcohol content and maturation in rum casks results in powerful aromas and a complex flavor profile that compensate for the youthful age.

faq.question: What does the unusual metallic note in the flavor of this whisky mean?
faq.answer: The metallic note is an unusual but characteristic flavor nuance that can appear in some young whiskies. It arises from the combination of cask character and young spirit components, giving the HeiligenBergFeld 2020/2024 an edgy, intriguing dimension.

item.name: Benrinnes 21 Years Grand Crescendo - Diageo Special Releases (2024)
item.description: Once again, the new Diageo Special Releases are here – this time under the name Grand Crescendo. As always, expectations are high, especially given the hefty price tag that accompanies these limited editions year after year. This time, the spotlight is on a 21-years Benrinnes from Speyside – a distillery cherished by insiders but rarely highlighted in such a prominent way. A good reason to take a closer look at this single malt.

image.alt: Benrinnes 21 Years Grand Crescendo - Diageo Special Releases 2024

tasting_notes.nose: The aromas are powerful from the start. A vibrant fruit basket emerges with distinct notes of peach, mango, and berries – exotic, sweet, and inviting. Marzipan and a hint of caramel add depth, while tobacco leaves introduce a spicy edge. Despite the 55.4% ABV, everything is well integrated and aromatically precise.
tasting_notes.palate: As expected: full-bodied and intense. The initial impression is bold, almost explosive – a true flavor journey. Sweet fruit pairs with elegant yet noticeable oak spice. A tangy orange note adds freshness, complemented by hints of strawberry and a nutty element reminiscent of hazelnut. A slight dryness rounds out the mouthfeel.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long, robust, and notably astringent. Menthol brings a cooling freshness, while strong oak notes and leather add structure. Walnuts leave a slightly bitter, nutty final note. A finish that lingers – and challenges.

conclusion.marcel: This Benrinnes 21 Grand Crescendo is a true Speyside rocker: powerful, multi-layered, and full of character. Despite its hefty price tag, it impresses with pure drinking pleasure and an exciting variety of aromas that leave you wanting more – but you really should savor each sip, not just stash it on the shelf.
conclusion.sascha: A technically impressive single malt with a pronounced balance between intense fruitiness, elegant oak spice, and a long, structured finish. The Benrinnes 21 demonstrates how precisely matured whisky can be crafted, though its price feels a bit ambitious for everyday enjoyment.

faq.question: What makes the Benrinnes 21 Years Grand Crescendo from the Diageo Special Releases 2024 stand out compared to other Speyside whiskies?
faq.answer: The Benrinnes 21 Years Grand Crescendo is distinguished by its intense fruitiness featuring peach, mango, and berries, combined with sweet notes like marzipan and caramel alongside spicy tobacco and oak aromas. Its high bottling strength of 55.4% ABV is well integrated, giving it a powerful yet balanced complexity.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol strength of 55.4% ABV affect the tasting experience of this whisky?
faq.answer: Despite the robust 55.4% ABV, the Benrinnes 21 Years Grand Crescendo remains aromatically clear and well integrated. The high strength amplifies the intensity of the flavors and delivers a full-bodied, almost explosive entry without the whisky feeling harsh or unbalanced.

faq.question: Which flavor notes should one pay special attention to when tasting the Benrinnes 21 Years?
faq.answer: During tasting, the exotic fruit notes of peach, mango, and berries stand out, complemented by sweet hints of marzipan and caramel. On the palate, orangey and slightly tart nuances bring freshness, while hazelnut, menthol, leather, and robust oak tones shape the long, sturdy finish.

item.name: Talisker Skye
item.description: When it comes to Scotch whisky, Talisker is synonymous with strong flavours, maritime character and a pinch of smoke. But while the well-known Talisker 10 Years is considered a classic, the Talisker Skye offers a somewhat softer, more accessible alternative - at least on paper.

tasting_notes.nose: As soon as you hold the glass to your nose, you immediately realise: Yes, this is Talisker! But a somewhat younger representative. Instead of deep, heavy smoke, you get a gentle peaty note that is more reminiscent of a distant campfire on the beach. This is joined by white pepper, classic bourbon cask notes such as vanilla, caramel and toffee as well as an interesting salty sea breeze. A slight seaweed note resonates, and on top of that there is a pinch of BBQ peanuts and a hint of honey. All in all, a round but somewhat lighter nose that makes you want to take your first sip.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip makes it clear that Talisker Skye is going its own way. Surprisingly, a clear peach note appears first, which is almost tropical. Then comes a slightly bitter spiciness, which is accompanied by oak and a salty, maritime freshness. The mouthfeel is quite creamy, which makes the whisky pleasantly smooth. Spicy accents of rye bread and a slight sharpness provide a nice counterbalance to the fruity sweetness.
tasting_notes.finish: This shows that the Talisker Skye is younger than its 10-years brother. The finish is rather short, but still remains interesting. A mineral note, paired with the typical Talisker salinity, dominates the finish. A hint of mealy apple rounds off the whole thing before the flavours quickly fade away.

conclusion.marcel: Talisker Skye delivers the typical maritime character but is noticeably more approachable and less smoky than the 10-years. For those looking to gently ease into salty, smoky whiskies, it’s a charming companion – though for the same price, you might still prefer the more complex classic.
conclusion.sascha: The Talisker Skye shows a solid balance between fruity sweetness, spicy oak, and maritime saltiness, but in terms of structure and complexity, it feels somewhat underdeveloped compared to its 10-years counterpart. For newcomers to smoky-salty whisky profiles, it’s a balanced, if not entirely convincing, option price-wise.

faq.question: How does the Talisker Skye differ in taste from the Talisker 10 Years?
faq.answer: Talisker Skye is slightly younger and features a milder smoke and peat character than the 10-years. It comes across as lighter, with fruity peach notes, a creamy texture, and a salty sea breeze, whereas the 10-years is bolder and smokier.

faq.question: Why does the Talisker Skye have no age statement on the bottle?
faq.answer: Talisker Skye is a NAS whisky (No Age Statement), meaning it has no fixed minimum age. This allows the distillery to blend different casks to achieve the desired flavor and style.

faq.question: Is the Talisker Skye suitable for whisky beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Talisker Skye’s softer, more accessible aroma profile with less intense smokiness makes it well-suited for beginners. Its fruity and mildly spicy notes provide a pleasant introduction to Talisker’s characteristic maritime flavor profiles.

item.name: Bruichladdich 30 Years (1993/2023) - Malts of Scotland
item.description: Islay without peat, but full of depth. Those familiar with older Bruichladdich expressions know this distillery offers not smoke, but structure, elegance, and a quiet strength. Independent bottler Malts of Scotland has secured a venerable bourbon barrel from 1993 – and with it, shows what time can do to a fine single malt. Matured for 30 years on the Isle of Islay, bottled at 45.8% ABV, non-chill filtered and natural in colour.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose unfolds with a beautifully layered complexity. Tropical fruits glide gently into the scene – mango, pineapple, dragon fruit – accompanied by soft cotton candy and delicate honey. A subtle oak note lingers in the background, tied together with cereal, Bircher muesli and almond milk. There’s even a light nuttiness reminiscent of trail mix. This is old-school Bruichladdich at its best: multifaceted, elegant, never overpowering.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip confirms the promise: a textbook mature bourbon cask. Again, tropical fruit – mango and papaya now – paired with oats, cereals, and subtle chia or psyllium seed notes, evoking a modern breakfast bowl. It may sound unusual, but it works perfectly with the soft sweetness and malty core. With the second sip, the spice arrives: robust, mature oak, dry and assertive but never bitter. A persistent sweetness remains – somewhere between a fruity granola bar and sun-ripened fruit.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium in length, oaky and spicy, with a hint of vanilla and a flash of apple. Compared to the beautifully expressive nose and palate, the finish feels a touch simpler, more on the dry side. Not weak – far from it – but more restrained. In a whisky of this quality, that makes it the “least strong” part, which still means “very good.”

conclusion.marcel: This Bruichladdich 1993/2023 is like a quiet friend, inviting you to relax with tropical sweetness and mature oak. Not every dram needs to make a grand entrance; here, it’s the subtle, enjoyable depth that counts – a whisky that rewards patience with character. Only the finish could use a bit more pep, but that just makes the whole experience charmingly human.
conclusion.sascha: An impressively structured single malt that clearly reflects its maturation in a high-quality bourbon barrel over 30 years. The balance between tropical fruit sweetness, a malty base, and dry oak is top-notch, although the slightly restrained finish slightly limits the overall impression. For lovers of refined cask maturation and harmonious complexity, this is a first-class example of independent bottling craftsmanship.

faq.question: Why isn’t the Bruichladdich 1993/2023, aged 30 years, smoky even though it’s from Islay?
faq.answer: Bruichladdich is known for maintaining an unpeated style on Islay. Unlike other distilleries on the island, the focus here is on a clear, elegant structure and fruity aromas, rather than smoke or peat.

faq.question: What does it mean that the whisky is non-chill filtered and natural color?
faq.answer: Non-chill filtered means the whisky wasn’t filtered at low temperatures, preserving more natural flavors and oils. Natural color indicates that no artificial coloring was added, emphasizing the whisky’s authenticity.

faq.question: How does the 30-year maturation in a bourbon barrel influence the taste of this Bruichladdich?
faq.answer: The long maturation in a bourbon barrel imparts complex depth with pronounced tropical fruit notes like mango and papaya, along with a gentle sweetness. At the same time, the matured oak adds spice and a pleasant dryness without overpowering the flavor.

item.name: Benromach 40 Years Batch 2 (2022)
item.description: Whiskies of this age are rare. Whiskies of this calibre are even rarer. The Benromach 40 Years Batch 2, bottled in 2022 at a bold 56.5% ABV, hails from Scotland's Speyside region and spent four decades in first fill sherry casks. This is not an everyday dram – it’s a sensorial time capsule, meticulously composed and highly limited.

tasting_notes.nose: What strikes first is a noble aged character – no dust, just maturity. Cognac-like notes rise gently, followed by ginger, maple syrup and a touch of honey. There’s an underlying richness of sweet aged leather, ripe tobacco, and a whiff of coffee grounds. Add to that deeply overripe red fruits, baked apple and sugar beet – it’s an aromatic tapestry that unfolds slowly and rewards patience.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, this whisky is surprisingly vivid. Despite its age, it’s oily, full-bodied and layered. The entry is sweet, with orange and grapefruit bringing a light tang. Then the cask takes over: curry, Indian spices, and dry tannic structure. Toward the end, ginger makes a comeback, followed by a flash of sour cherries, adding drama and contrast.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium in length, but full of character. Notes of old oak, some bordering on burnt wood, dominate the aftertaste. Intense spice lingers, balanced by a subtle return of fruity undertones – the sherry cask’s final whisper.

conclusion.marcel: This Benromach 40 Years is like a well-told novel: profound, full of surprises, and with a spice that lingers long after. It demonstrates just how majestic well-aged whisky can be without shouting for attention – a true experience for connoisseurs seeking something extraordinary.
conclusion.sascha: The harmonious balance of sweet fruit, spicy depth, and structured oak maturity makes the Benromach 40 Years a technically impressive whisky. Despite its power, it remains remarkably well-balanced and impressively showcases how complexity and approachability can go hand in hand.

faq.question: Why was the Benromach 40 Years Batch 2 matured in first fill sherry casks?
faq.answer: First fill sherry casks impart intense aromas of dried fruits, spices, and sweet oak to the whisky. Over such a long maturation period of 40 years, they create a complex, profound flavor profile that shapes the character of the whisky.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 56.5% ABV affect the tasting experience?
faq.answer: The high alcohol content provides a powerful and lively texture that remains intact despite the whisky’s advanced age. It enhances the range of aromas and allows the complex spice and fruit notes to be perceived more intensely. Adding a little water can help to further unlock the finer nuances of the flavors.

faq.question: How does the Benromach 40 Years Batch 2 differ from younger Benromach releases?
faq.answer: The 40-years Benromach reveals a significantly deeper, more mature aroma profile with dominant notes of leather, tobacco, and aged wood that are less pronounced in younger releases. The extended maturation in sherry casks also brings exceptional complexity and spiciness that younger expressions have yet to fully develop.

item.name: St. Kilian Exceptional Range Mizunara – Peated
item.description: Attention whisky nerds: If you think you've tasted everything - stop for a moment. The St. Kilian Exceptional Range Mizunara - Peated is not a standard bottling, but an aromatic border crosser that is anything but predictable. This limited single cask bottling from the renowned St. Kilian Distillery combines Japanese elegance with German innovative spirit.

image.alt: St. Kilian Exceptional Range Mizunara – Peated

tasting_notes.nose: The first moment? Surprisingly restrained. A hint of cold smoke, almost shy, lies over a warm, dark fruity centre. Plums, sultanas, a touch of caramel, light but present. And then there is this ‘funky’ element - elusive, slightly fermented, perhaps a greeting from the Mizunara oak? It remains exciting.
tasting_notes.palate: Boom. On the palate, the whisky hits like a spice merchant from Kyoto. Black pepper, clove, sandalwood and tart herbs - complex, powerful, almost impossible to categorise. The smoke remains in the background, allowing the spices to take centre stage. The texture: extremely full-bodied, almost creamy, but with an edgy, wildly natural depth. Not beginner material - this is a sensory labyrinth.
tasting_notes.finish: The smoke only returns in the finish - cold, pebbly, slightly ashy, a bit like a campfire at dawn. Then fruit again, this time red berries, subtly embedded. The finish is long, voluminous, complex - and this is where the whisky becomes an experience.

conclusion.marcel: Not a whisky for the faint-hearted, but that’s exactly what makes the St. Kilian Exceptional Range Mizunara – Peated so thrilling. The surprising spice notes and the mysterious Mizunara oak invite you to dive in and marvel – an experience you won’t forget anytime soon.
conclusion.sascha: With a complex balance of spicy wood notes, bitter herbs, and restrained smoke, this Mizunara-Peated impresses through its layered structure and powerful finish. Technically flawless, it challenges the connoisseur with its dense aromatic profile and the unusual interplay of Mizunara oak and peat.

faq.question: What makes the Mizunara oak in the St. Kilian Exceptional Range Mizunara – Peated special?
faq.answer: Mizunara oak is a rare Japanese oak species that imparts unique aromas to the whisky. In the St. Kilian Mizunara – Peated, it provides a slightly fermentative, “funky” aroma that sets it apart from classic oak cask notes and creates an exciting, complex depth.

faq.question: How does the peat smoke influence the taste of this whisky compared to other smoky whiskies?
faq.answer: In the St. Kilian Mizunara – Peated, the smoke takes a more subtle backseat. It is cold, mineral-like, and ashy, especially present in the finish, complementing the dominant spice and fruit notes rather than overpowering them, which distinguishes it from heavily smoky whiskies.

faq.question: Why does the St. Kilian Exceptional Range Mizunara – Peated have no age statement (No Age Statement)?
faq.answer: Omitting an age statement allows the distillery to blend casks of varying maturities to achieve a particularly complex and balanced flavor profile. This way, the focus can be on quality and character rather than age alone.

item.name: Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky
item.description: The Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky is a real all-rounder that not only shines on its own, but also in cocktails. With an ABV of 45.0 % and an innovative production process that combines 80 % corn and 20 % barley, this whisky offers a pleasantly smooth texture - just the thing for anyone looking for a balanced and versatile whisky.

tasting_notes.nose: As soon as the bottle is opened, a light floral fragrance hits the nose, accompanied by a tantalising honey note and a hint of coconut. This sweet freshness is quickly replaced by caramel, nuts and a light hint of hay and straw. A pleasant blend of bright fruits, such as a hint of apple or pear, and a mild coconut note create an inviting, almost tropical atmosphere.
tasting_notes.palate: ﻿The Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky shows its true strength on the palate: sweet, but not too overpowering, with caramel, vanilla and a mild, pleasant spiciness. You can taste gummy bears and a hint of rye. The texture is soft and almost creamy, with a subtle ‘vapour’ that does not overpower the whole.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the Nikka Coffey Grain is not long, but fresh and cereal-like.

conclusion.marcel: The Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky is a charmingly straightforward companion for relaxed evenings, offering pure enjoyment with its sweet honey notes and creamy texture. Those looking for a soft, approachable whisky without any fuss will definitely find what they’re after here – though a bit more depth would have done this dram some good.
conclusion.sascha: With its balanced structure of sweet caramel notes and subtle spice, the Nikka Coffey Grain demonstrates solid harmony, making it versatile both neat and in cocktails. However, compared to more complex grain whiskies, it lacks a bit of pronounced complexity and a longer finish.

faq.question: How does Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky differ from traditional single malt whiskies?
faq.answer: Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky is made predominantly from corn (80%) with a portion of barley (20%) and distilled in a Coffey still, which gives it a softer, sweeter texture. Unlike single malts, which are made exclusively from malted barley, this grain whisky often exhibits fruitier and milder aromas.

faq.question: What does the use of a Coffey still mean in the production of this whisky?
faq.answer: A Coffey still is a continuous distillation apparatus originally developed for grain whisky. It allows for a gentler and more efficient distillation process, which imparts the Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky with its characteristic smoothness and the complex aromas of corn and barley.

faq.question: What is the best way to enjoy Nikka Coffey Grain Whisky?
faq.answer: This whisky is well suited both for sipping neat and as a versatile base for cocktails. Its soft texture and balanced sweetness pair well with fruity or spicy cocktail ingredients, while neat with a splash of water or on the rocks, its floral and caramel notes really shine.

item.name: Cap a Pie (2018) - Michel Couvreur
item.description: With the Cap à Pie, Michel Couvreur expands his portfolio with a bold new blend. This assemblage combines whiskies aged between 4 and 12 years, all of which spent an additional four years finishing in ex-sherry casks. Bottled at 45% ABV and without an age statement, this whisky keeps its origins a secret—but the influence of its maturation is unmistakable.

image.alt: Michel Couvreur Cap a Pie Release 2018

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression is surprising—espresso? For a moment, you might think someone mixed up whisky and coffee. Intense roasted aromas dominate, followed by a slightly musty cellar note, nutty tones, and a touch of dried dates. Leather, dark chocolate, and a hint of licorice complete this complex nose. It’s not designed to please—this one challenges the drinker.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, Cap à Pie continues its dry and earthy journey. Oak is front and center, joined by date-like sweetness and a hint of dark berry fruitiness. Particularly striking is the impression of fermented grapes, which lends the whisky a wine-like character. The sherry cask influence is undeniable—but expressed more through depth and spice than sweetness.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish stays true to the overall profile: dry, spicy, and oaky. A touch of pepper brings a subtle heat, before a faint mustiness echoes back—like a final glance into an old cellar filled with wine casks.

conclusion.marcel: This is not a whisky for collectors, but for connoisseurs who crave an unusual, robust tasting experience. Cap à Pie surprises with its dry, almost rustic character and challenges the brave to embrace its dark cellar aromas. If you’re into sherry bombs, you’d better look elsewhere – this dram is honest, distinctive, and definitely a conversation starter at your next tasting.
conclusion.sascha: The Michel Couvreur Cap à Pie impresses with its complex structure and the successful interplay of dry sherry notes and earthy oak. Despite its quirky mustiness, it remains balanced and reveals an intriguing depth that is well supported at 45% ABV. Not an easy introduction, but a fascinating blend for lovers of sophisticated whiskies with character.

faq.question: Why does Michel Couvreur not state an age on the Cap à Pie 2018 release?
faq.answer: Cap à Pie is a blend of whiskies aged between 4 and 12 years, all of which have undergone a four-year sherry finish. Michel Couvreur deliberately omits an age statement (NAS) to highlight the complexity of the blend without being confined to a single age.

faq.question: What role does the sherry finish play in this whisky?
faq.answer: The minimum four-year finish in ex-sherry casks is a defining factor for Cap à Pie. It imparts spicy depth, dry sweetness, and complex aromas of dark berries, leather, and spices, without becoming overly sweet.

faq.question: Why does the flavor sometimes remind one of fermented grapes and wine?
faq.answer: The wine-like character comes from the sherry finish, which gives the whisky a fruity note reminiscent of dark berries and fermented grapes. This nuance adds a special depth and complexity that sets Cap à Pie apart from classic whiskies.

item.name: Glen Moray Port Cask Finish
item.description: Located in the heart of Scotland’s Speyside region, Glen Moray is known for approachable, fruit-driven whiskies. Since the grand opening of its visitor centre on July 6th, 2004, the distillery has continued to evolve while staying true to its roots. Interestingly, Glen Moray played a key role in the early days of wood finishing experiments — a technique that would later make Glenmorangie world-famous. With the Port Cask Finish, Glen Moray presents a no-age-statement single malt matured in ex-bourbon barrels before receiving a finishing period in port wine casks. Bottled at a friendly 40% ABV, it's an ideal choice for those new to whisky.

tasting_notes.nose: Right from the first sniff, the whisky offers a warm and inviting aroma. Notes of white chocolate, a subtle wine-like character, and a gentle touch of leather emerge. Black cherries add fruity depth, supported by a pleasantly malty sweetness. The nose invites you to take that first sip.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, sweetness takes the lead — clearly showcasing the influence of the port cask. A light bitterness provides a nice balance. Hints of caramel and vanilla mingle with soft oak, and there’s a touch of youthful spirit that gives it a charming character rather than being a drawback.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is short but harmonious. Grainy and bready notes linger, accompanied by a touch of spice and a final trace of fruity sweetness.

conclusion.marcel: Lighthearted and charming, the Glen Moray Port Cask Finish brings plenty of drinking pleasure to the glass without being overly complicated – a genuine Speyside treat with a sweet, fruity port wine touch that leaves you wanting more. For anyone seeking an uncomplicated whisky with character, this is a fantastic companion for relaxed evenings.
conclusion.sascha: The Glen Moray Port Cask Finish impresses with its well-balanced interplay between malty sweetness and a refined port wine finish, even though the short finish lacks a bit of lasting persistence. A technically solid Speyside single malt that offers an interesting, structured alternative, especially for newcomers to cask finishes.

faq.question: How does the port cask finish influence the taste of the Glen Moray Port Cask Finish?
faq.answer: The port cask finish imparts a pronounced fruity sweetness to the whisky, expressed through notes of Morello cherries and a wine-like aroma. At the same time, it creates a pleasant balance between sweetness and a slight bitterness, complemented by caramel and vanilla flavors.

faq.question: Who is the Glen Moray Port Cask Finish particularly suited for?
faq.answer: With its moderate 40% ABV and accessible, fruity profile, the Glen Moray Port Cask Finish is ideal for beginners looking for a gentle single malt with an interesting cask maturation.

faq.question: Why did Glen Moray choose not to include an age statement for this whisky, and what does that mean for the flavor?
faq.answer: Omitting an age statement (No Age Statement) allows the distillery to blend different vintages to achieve a consistent flavor profile. The result is a whisky with a youthful character that gains additional complexity and sweetness from the port cask finish.

item.name: Woodford Reserve Kentucky Derby 148
item.description: Every year, Woodford Reserve celebrates the Kentucky Derby with a limited edition release. The Kentucky Derby 148 bottle is not only visually striking—designed by renowned equine artist Jaime Corum—but also aims to capture the elegance and power of the iconic horse race in its flavor profile. But does this bourbon live up to the promise of its exclusive design?

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, the Woodford Reserve Kentucky Derby 148 stays true to its roots: caramel and vanilla form the foundation, while honey and a hint of orange add a pleasant sweetness. Spicy rye notes and a nutty depth follow, with brown sugar and oak rounding out the experience.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, this bourbon is full-bodied and well-rounded. Caramel and toasted nuts dominate the first impression, accompanied by a pleasant oak spice. The rye spice is more pronounced than in the classic Woodford Reserve, adding extra character to this Derby release. A subtle dryness provides structure, while delicate notes of coffee beans and cocoa add depth.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-length finish continues the more intense notes: dark cocoa and a touch of cinnamon linger before fading into an elegant oak note. A worthy ending that doesn't overwhelm but remains memorable.

conclusion.marcel: Anyone who enjoys a solid bourbon with a bit more spice and character will be well served here – the Kentucky Derby 148 is charming, but not a showstopper. For collectors and Woodford fans, it’s a nice, stylish addition that scores points with its story and attractive design. No drama, but a pleasant companion for relaxed evenings.
conclusion.sascha: The Woodford Reserve Kentucky Derby 148 impresses with its balanced interplay between sweet caramel notes and a spicy rye backbone, yet it remains rather conservative. Technically clean, with a medium-length, structured finish, it represents a successful, if not revolutionary, evolution within the portfolio. For connoisseurs who value harmony and subtle complexity, it comes highly recommended.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Woodford Reserve Kentucky Derby 148 in taste from the classic Woodford Reserve Bourbon?
faq.answer: The Kentucky Derby 148 exhibits a more pronounced rye spiciness and a more intense oak spice than the classic Woodford Reserve. Additionally, notes of coffee beans and cocoa add extra depth, while the finish, with dark cocoa and cinnamon, comes across as spicier and more complex.

faq.question: How is the Kentucky Derby reflected in the design and flavor of this special edition?
faq.answer: The bottle’s design was created by artist Jaime Corum and visually captures the elegance of the Kentucky Derby. In terms of flavor, the bourbon aims to reflect the atmosphere and tradition of the horse race through its combination of power, spice, and elegance.

faq.question: Is the Woodford Reserve Kentucky Derby 148 suitable for beginners or more for experienced bourbon lovers?
faq.answer: With its complex flavor profile, which includes classic caramel and vanilla notes alongside robust rye spice and bitter cocoa aromas, this bourbon is geared more toward experienced drinkers who appreciate a profound and balanced bourbon variant.

item.name: Woodford Reserve Distiller's Select
item.description: Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select is a Kentucky bourbon known for its classic yet full-bodied style. The Woodford Reserve Distillery produces a series of four straight whiskeys, each highlighting a primary grain. The Distiller’s Select is a bourbon, meaning it contains at least 51% corn, complemented by rye and malted barley. One standout feature is its pot still distillation – a rarity among Kentucky bourbons, adding depth and complexity to the final product.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, this bourbon presents a rich bouquet of traditional notes. Sweet caramel blends with vanilla and honey, while oak undertones add depth. A fresh orange note introduces a fruity brightness, while a hint of solvent and subtle rye spice highlight its grain composition.
tasting_notes.palate: The palate is well-balanced and harmonious. Honey sweetness meets fresh citrus and juicy nectarines. A generous amount of rye spice gives the bourbon an appealing sharpness and depth. Caramel and oak provide a classic bourbon character that is both rich and satisfying.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium in length and pleasantly warming. Tobacco notes take center stage, accompanied by the distinctive spiciness of rye and oak, creating a well-rounded and lasting impression.

conclusion.marcel: Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select brings fun to the glass and impresses with its sweet caramel and honey notes – a bourbon that delivers uncomplicated enjoyment and is perfect for a relaxed evening. Not overly complex, but with enough character to keep it interesting.
conclusion.sascha: With a balanced harmony of sweetness, rye spice, and oak, Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select presents itself as a structured bourbon that, despite its classic style, offers a pleasant depth. The medium-length tobacco and spice notes on the finish provide a coherent, lasting conclusion.

faq.question: What makes the pot still distillation of Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select special?
faq.answer: Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select is distilled in pot stills, which is unusual among Kentucky bourbons. This traditional distillation method allows for a slower and gentler separation of the alcohols, resulting in a deeper range of aromas and greater complexity in the bourbon.

faq.question: How does the grain bill influence the flavor of Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select?
faq.answer: The bourbon is made from at least 51% corn, complemented by rye and malted barley. The corn provides the characteristic sweetness, the rye adds a spicy sharpness, and the malted barley contributes to the flavor depth. This combination creates a balanced and complex flavor profile.

faq.question: Why does Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select have no age statement?
faq.answer: Woodford Reserve Distiller’s Select is bottled without an explicit age statement, meaning it is blended from various barrels to ensure a consistent flavor profile. The maturation period typically ranges between four and seven years but varies depending on barrel selection.

item.name: Tomatin (2009/2021) - Gordon & MacPhail
item.description: Approachable but not boring – this Highland single malt shows what independent bottlers like Gordon & MacPhail can draw out of a distillery like Tomatin. The Discovery range is designed as an accessible entry point into different whisky styles – smoky, sherried, or fruity. This 2009 Tomatin clearly belongs to the fruity corner – and delivers just that.

tasting_notes.nose: A friendly and inviting nose with ripe apples, floral notes, and soft vanilla. Beneath that lie hints of fresh hay and raw cookie dough. Nothing too complex, but clean and pleasant.
tasting_notes.palate: Surprisingly spicy for 43% ABV, with malty sweetness, honey, a slight waxy texture, and a noticeable oak presence. The mouthfeel is soft but substantial – this isn’t a thin whisky.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium in length, slightly bitter and sweet, with a final echo of gentle fruitiness. The oak lingers but doesn’t overwhelm.

conclusion.marcel: An uncomplicated Highland Single Malt that’s a joy to drink, pleasantly surprising with its fruity sweetness combined with robust oak. A great introduction for beginners, and for connoisseurs, a solid companion without any frills – honest and straightforward, just like a good friend for an after-work drink.
conclusion.sascha: The Tomatin 2009/2021 impresses with a clear structure and a balanced interplay between malty sweetness and dominant oak. Despite its simple aromatic profile, it offers a surprisingly substantial texture, though it remains limited in complexity – a reliable Highland malt for those who appreciate clean lines and approachability.

faq.question: What makes the Tomatin 2009/2021 from Gordon & MacPhail stand out compared to other Highland malts?
faq.answer: The Tomatin 2009/2021 is distinguished by its fruity style, which Gordon & MacPhail, as an independent bottler, highlights. Unlike typical Highland malts, this whisky focuses on ripe apple notes, vanilla, and a pleasant spiciness, making it beginner-friendly yet still interesting in flavor.

faq.question: How does the bottling by Gordon & MacPhail influence the taste of the Tomatin 2009/2021?
faq.answer: As an independent bottler, Gordon & MacPhail carefully selects casks that emphasize the distillery’s character. For the Tomatin 2009/2021, they crafted a fruity profile with clear oak aromas and balanced spice, which typically isn’t found in the distillery’s standard releases.

faq.question: Why does the Tomatin 2009/2021 have no age statement on the label, even though it has matured for 12 years?
faq.answer: The Tomatin 2009/2021 is marketed as a single malt without an age statement (NAS), despite being distilled in 2009 and bottled in 2021. This is because independent bottlers like Gordon & MacPhail sometimes omit an official age statement to maintain flexibility in cask selection and blending.

item.name: The English 9 Years (2013) Slicing and dicing Cask 137.18 - The Scotch Malt Whisky Society
item.description: England and whisky? Yes, they go together - and this 9-years drop from St George's Distillery proves it emphatically.With a full 67.3% ABV and a profile that almost tastes like bourbon or strong rye, this single malt is anything but ordinary. So if you're expecting a classic Speysider, you'd better buckle up - here's a whisky that doesn't just have slicing and dicing in its name!

image.alt: The English 2013 9 Years Slicing and dicing Cask 137.18 - The Scotch Malt Whisky Society

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression? Peach meets malty sweetness, accompanied by a good portion of coconut.Then things get more interesting:A hint of gluten (the good kind, I promise!), creamy vanilla and toasted bread make their presence felt. These are joined by pear and other stone fruits - a fruity yet warming flavour with a little surprise twist.
tasting_notes.palate: Bourbon fans, watch out! Strong oak spice, soft berries and a full load of caramel and vanilla dominate the first sip. This is joined by a subtle hint of pineapple, which provides a pleasant freshness.Malt adds depth and the spice does not hold back - a whisky that scores with its intense but charming power.
tasting_notes.finish: This is where the true power of the Englishman is revealed. Strong vanilla and light oak notes characterise the finish, which lingers for a medium length and leaves a sweet trace of brown sugar and - was that banana?

conclusion.marcel: A true adventurer among whiskies, delighting with its wild blend of bourbon charm and fruity spice. If you’re looking for a bold, unconventional dram with plenty of power, this one definitely hits the mark – pure drinking pleasure that’s both fun and surprising.
conclusion.sascha: This 9-years single malt from England showcases an impressive balance between intense vanilla, oak spice, and fruity nuances. Despite its high alcohol strength, it feels structured and well-composed, making it an exciting example of modern, powerful cask maturation.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 67.3% affect the taste and drinkability of this whisky?
