conclusion.marcel: This Glenrothes 12 Year Oloroso Finish is a true explosion of flavor, delighting with its sweet-sour bitterness and fruity notes that simply make it fun to drink. For anyone searching for a sherry whisky with character and genuine drinking pleasure, this one is definitely a bullseye – it certainly keeps your tongue busy!
conclusion.sascha: An excellently structured Speyside malt, whose balance between sweet fruit notes and a distinctive, tart bitterness is impressive. The complex aroma profile, with hints of orange peel and cinnamon, along with the long-lasting, oak-driven finish, make it a technically very well-executed Oloroso finish whisky.

faq.question: How does the Oloroso finish influence the taste of the Glenrothes 12 Year?
faq.answer: The Oloroso finish imparts a pronounced sweetness to the Glenrothes 12 Year, with notes of ripe cherries, dark chocolate, and dried fruits such as figs and raisins. At the same time, it adds a pleasant spiciness through aromas of orange peel and cinnamon, as well as a long-lasting, intense finish featuring coffee and oak notes.

faq.question: Why is the Glenrothes 12 Year Oloroso Finish bottled at 56.6% ABV?
faq.answer: The higher bottling strength of 56.6% ABV allows the whisky’s complex aromas to be perceived more intensely. Especially with cask strength releases like this one, the depth of the Oloroso finish is preserved, making the flavor more robust and multi-layered.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Glenrothes 12 Year Oloroso Finish from other Glenrothes releases?
faq.answer: Unlike the classic Glenrothes standard releases, which usually have a sherry or bourbon finish, this whisky was finished by an independent bottler (Atom Brands Group) with a special Oloroso sherry finish. This gives it a more intense fruit character, greater spiciness, and a distinctive, long-lasting finish.

item.name: Balcones Pot Still Bourbon
item.description: The Balcones Pot Still Bourbon comes from Texas and is characterised by its unique composition of roasted blue corn, wheat, rye and malt. This whisky is produced entirely on site, from the milling of the grains to storage and distillation.

tasting_notes.nose: At first sniff, we immediately notice the distinctive scent of burnt caramel and the rich nuances of pecan or walnut. A hint of green apple (Granny Smith) and the aroma of baked chestnuts complete this inviting range of flavours.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip reveals a creamy, spicy and sweet explosion of flavour. The intensity of black pepper, which is defined here not by heat but by flavour, stands out in particular. The well-known "Balcones Funk" note, which is already familiar from our Texas Rye Review, gives this bourbon a unique flavour. Toffee, candied apple and a hint of corn combine to create a full-bodied flavour experience that is accompanied by a subtle influence of high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS).
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long-lasting and offers a roasted malt note accompanied by a hint of burnt flavour. The sweetness remains present and dry, giving our whisky a balanced and appealing aftertaste.

conclusion.marcel: The Balcones Pot Still Bourbon is a true character, delivering drinking pleasure with its spicy 'Balcones Funk' and sweet, creamy toffee notes. Not necessarily for purists, but a lively experience for anyone looking for something different from the usual bourbon.
conclusion.sascha: With its balanced structure of spicy black pepper, sweet toffee, and a subtle malt roast, the Balcones Pot Still Bourbon demonstrates a successful harmony. The complex aroma lingers for a long time, even though the influence of HFCS slightly distorts the overall impression.

faq.question: What makes Balcones Pot Still Bourbon special compared to other bourbons?
faq.answer: Balcones Pot Still Bourbon uses an unusual corn mash bill featuring roasted blue corn, combined with wheat, rye, and malt. Additionally, the entire production takes place on-site in Texas—from milling the grains to distillation and aging—giving it a unique flavor diversity and a strong regional identity.

faq.question: How does the 'Balcones Funk' note influence the taste of the Pot Still Bourbon?
faq.answer: 'Balcones Funk' refers to a distinctive spicy and slightly earthy aroma that adds special depth to the bourbon. It manifests as a pleasant spiciness that highlights black pepper without being sharp, creating a truly unmistakable tasting experience.

faq.question: Why is there a perception of High-Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS) in the taste of Balcones Pot Still Bourbon, even though none is added?
faq.answer: The impression of High-Fructose Corn Syrup arises from the natural sweetness and corn-based aromas that are intensified during the production process. It is not an additive but a sensory perception resulting from the use of roasted blue corn and the fermentation process.

item.name: GlenAllachie 8 Years
item.description: Billy Walker, the master blender at GlenAllachie, describes the 2022 bottling as follows: "From the outset, it has been my aim to release quality whiskies at fair and accessible price points. This can be a significant challenge when you also choose to deploy an extensive and costly wood management policy, however, I believe The GlenAllachie 8-years fits the bill without any compromises or corner-cutting." By the way, the GlenAllachie 8 years follows the same recipe as the highly acclaimed GlenAllachie Cask Strength series. The casks are Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso sherry puncheons, as well as virgin oak casks and red wine barriques.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose of the GlenAllachie 8 Years is a beautiful blend of spicy and sweet notes. Hints of cinnamon and gingerbread spices dance on the nose, while subtle nuances of burnt caramel and walnuts add a distinct depth. The sweet warmth of forest honey combines with the coniferous forest after rain.
tasting_notes.palate: The flavour of the single malt is just as exciting as its aroma. It is viscous and full-bodied on the tongue, with a buttery-soft texture on the palate. The flavours of caramel and cinnamon dominate, followed by a dash of sugar syrup that leaves a pleasant sweetness. An unexpected kick of eucalyptus adds a refreshing complexity.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the Scotch is of medium length and offers notes of oak and spice. A light floral note rounds off the overall picture.

conclusion.marcel: Here, the GlenAllachie 8 Years reveals its approachable yet characterful side – a whisky that’s genuinely enjoyable, delighting with its warm sweetness and a hint of eucalyptus. For anyone seeking uncomplicated pleasure with a touch of spice, this is a real winner, even if it doesn’t quite make it onto the podium.
conclusion.sascha: The GlenAllachie 8 Years impresses with a solid balance between sweet sherry notes and spicy oak, though it falls a bit short in complexity. The texture is pleasantly full-bodied, and the finish is medium-length with floral accents – a well-structured Speyside malt that represents a reliable choice in its price range.

faq.question: What types of casks were used to mature the GlenAllachie 8 Years?
faq.answer: The GlenAllachie 8 Years was matured in a combination of Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso sherry puncheons, virgin oak casks, and red wine barriques, contributing to its complex flavor profile.

faq.question: How does the GlenAllachie 8 Years differ from the distillery’s Cask Strength series?
faq.answer: The GlenAllachie 8 Years follows the same recipe as the Cask Strength series but is bottled at 46% ABV, making it more accessible, whereas the Cask Strength variants are uncut and often stronger.

faq.question: Which aromas dominate the taste and nose of the GlenAllachie 8 Years?
faq.answer: The nose is dominated by spicy notes like cinnamon and gingerbread spices, complemented by sweet hints of caramel and forest honey. On the palate, caramel and cinnamon combine with a buttery texture and a refreshing touch of eucalyptus.

item.name: Balmenach 8 Years (2013/2022) Barrique Finish - Best Dram
item.description: Balmenach does not have a core range. If you want to taste such a whisky, you have to resort to independent bottlings. Michel Reick - one of the best-known German independent bottlers of single malt whisky - has bottled a Balmenach with a 1st fill Banyuls barrique finish in the "Best Dram" series.

image.alt: Balmenach 2013/2022 8 Years Barrique Finish - Best Dram

tasting_notes.nose: The nose of the 8-years Balmenach single malt is intense and immediately present. A clear note of ripe cherry jam immediately hits the senses, accompanied by deep nuances of fermented plums. A touch of heavy red wine and a hint of cane sugar round off the bouquet, while deep red wine gums add a sweet and tantalising dimension.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip reveals a powerfully sweet flavour characterised by ripe fruit and subtle vinous notes. Dark wine gums provide a complex sweetness, while a pleasant warmth envelops the palate. A surprising sulphur note, reminiscent of rotten egg, adds an unexpected nuance.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is extremely spicy and long-lasting, with a lingering sweetness that gradually gives way to a drier character. Dark chocolate remains.

conclusion.marcel: This Balmenach 8 Years reveals itself as a true character with its intense fruity sweetness and surprisingly dry finish, leaving you wanting more. The sulfur notes are divisive but also add intriguing edges and corners to the glass – not everyone’s cup of tea, but definitely an entertaining dram for adventurous connoisseurs.
conclusion.sascha: The Balmenach 8 Years Best Dram impresses with its balanced structure between sweet fruit aromas and a spicy, dry finish. Despite the distracting sulfur note, the complexity remains intact, while the barrique finish gives the whisky an interesting depth that fans of complex Speyside whiskies will particularly appreciate.

faq.question: Why does Balmenach not have a fixed core range and how does this affect availability?
faq.answer: Balmenach does not produce a clearly defined core range, which means their whiskies are mainly available through independent bottlers like Best Dram. This results in each bottling being unique and availability varying greatly.

faq.question: How does the 1st Fill Banyuls Barrique Finish influence the character of the Balmenach 2013/2022?
faq.answer: The Banyuls Barrique Finish imparts intense fruity notes such as cherry jam and plums, along with a sweet, wine-like complexity. It also brings a deep sweetness and subtle red wine aromas that distinctly shape the whisky’s flavor.

faq.question: What is behind the unusual sulfur note in the taste and how typical is it for this whisky?
faq.answer: The sulfur note, reminiscent of rotten eggs, likely originates from natural sulfur compounds in the cask or maturation process. It is surprising and not typical for all Balmenach bottlings, but it contributes to the complexity and uniqueness of this 8-years whisky.

item.name: Cardhu 18 Years
item.description: Cardhu is part of the core range of spirits giant Diageo. While the Cardhu 12 Years is one of the Classic Malts of Scotland, the 18 years is rarely seen. Cardhu single malts are traditionally matured in ex-bourbon barrels and ex-sherry casks. By the way: the barley is said to be slightly peated.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, the Speysider opens heavy but not intense. Distinct woody flavours cover the lighter notes of light fruits such as pears and the hint of honey with vanilla that resonates. Floral nuances and a hint of peach or apricot can be sensed.
tasting_notes.palate: The Cardhu 18 years is unfortunately a little weak on the chest. Soft and spicy dry, it has notes of oak, peach, avocado and honeydew melon. The Speysider is infused with a slight sweetness and some baking cocoa. The flavour profile is pleasantly mild, but the nose clearly promised us more here.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is nicely tart and spicy.

conclusion.marcel: The Cardhu 18 Years has charm, but it comes across as somewhat reserved and tame – it lacks the promised depth one expects from such an aged malt. It’s fine for a relaxed evening, but not a dramatic experience. A pity, as it could have been infused with more passion.
conclusion.sascha: The Cardhu 18 Years presents itself with a clean structure, yet the 40% ABV and chill filtration noticeably mute the aromatics. The balance between wood, fruit, and spice is there, but overall it remains too flat and insufficiently complex for an 18-years Speysider.

faq.question: How does maturation in ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks influence the flavor of the Cardhu 18 Years?
faq.answer: The combination of ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks gives the Cardhu 18 Years a balanced profile of sweet vanilla and honey notes alongside spicy, woody aromas. The bourbon casks contribute fruitiness and vanilla, while the sherry casks add subtle depth and spice.

faq.question: Why is the barley in the Cardhu 18 Years lightly peated, and how does this affect the whisky?
faq.answer: The lightly peated barley imparts a subtle smoky note typical of some Speyside whiskies. This gentle peatiness complements the fruity and floral aromas without making the whisky overly smoky or dominant.

faq.question: How does the Cardhu 18 Years differ from the better-known Cardhu 12 Years?
faq.answer: The Cardhu 18 Years is less commonly available and displays a more complex flavor profile with pronounced wood notes and a mild spiciness on the finish. In comparison, the 12 Years is fresher and lighter, while the 18 Years offers a more mature, somewhat restrained aromatic character.

item.name: Longrow Peated (2021)
item.description: We have chosen the Longrow Peated from 2021, a smoky variant from Springbank in Campbeltown. This bottling promises a robust and characterful experience.

tasting_notes.nose: At first sniff, the nose is enveloped by a wealth of flavours. An earthy and peaty aroma wafts across, like a hint of a campfire on the beach. This is joined by mineral nuances that lend depth to the bouquet. Light phenolic notes dance with a hint of salt, accompanied by subtle hints of seaweed and kelp. A sweet note of vanilla sugar and a hint of pineapple provide a pleasant sweetness, while a light floral note resonates in the background.
tasting_notes.palate: The flavour of Longrow Peated reveals a harmonious combination of smoky and sweet notes. The strong peat smoke is present, but well balanced by a pleasant sweetness of sugar and honey. Surprising textures of chalk and aseptic paper add an interesting complexity to the flavour, while fruity elements of peach and salty hints of seaweed round off the profile.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long-lasting and impressive. A maritime saltiness lingers, paired with a hint of oak and an intense smoke that caresses the palate.

conclusion.marcel: A smoky delight with enough character to instantly spark a craving for more – Longrow Peated is robust, honest, and simply a lot of fun. For a NAS whisky, it makes a real statement in Campbeltown with its salty-sweet balance.
conclusion.sascha: Longrow Peated impresses with its balanced structure between peaty smoke and maritime notes, complemented by subtle sweetness and mineral depth. Despite its youth, it shows remarkable balance and complexity, making it a solid representative of the Campbeltown region.

faq.question: What makes the Longrow Peated (2021) from Campbeltown special compared to other smoky whiskies?
faq.answer: The Longrow Peated (2021) stands out through its combination of powerful peat smoke and maritime saltiness, enhanced by mineral and fruity nuances like peach and pineapple. This balance between smoke, sweetness, and salty notes is typical for Campbeltown and gives it a distinctive complexity.

faq.question: Why does the Longrow Peated (2021) have no age statement, and does that affect its quality?
faq.answer: This bottling is a so-called No Age Statement (NAS) whisky, meaning no exact age is specified. That doesn’t automatically imply a drop in quality. Often, carefully matured casks are blended to create a balanced flavor profile, as is the case with Longrow Peated (2021).

faq.question: How does the 46% ABV influence the taste and drinkability of the Longrow Peated (2021)?
faq.answer: At 46% ABV, the whisky sits slightly above the standard 40 to 43%. This provides more intensity and allows the aromas to unfold better. At the same time, it remains very drinkable, especially when diluted with a few drops of water to better perceive the complex flavor notes like peat smoke, sweetness, and salty nuances.

item.name: Horst Lüning Blended Malt Batch 2 10 Years
item.description: Within the German whisky community, Horst Lüning from Whisky.de is an unavoidable figure. Somewhere between meme and true expertise, he has succeeded in living his great passion for whisky with tremendous success. Authentically and skillfully staged are his YouTube reviews, which he has been regularly publishing with his son Ben for quite some time now. Great success naturally brings its own advantages. In addition to exclusive bottlings, he has, according to his own statements, fulfilled a long-cherished dream and created his own blended malt. Today, we have the second batch of his blends in the glass.

image.alt: Horst Lüning Blended Malt Batch 2 10 Years

tasting_notes.nose: Already in the aroma, the blend presents itself as very fresh and fruity. We discover clear notes of citrus, vanilla, and sweet maltiness. Accompanied by a finely tuned spiciness. The sherry casks leave a light influence of raisins.
tasting_notes.palate: In the mouth, the malt remains true to its line. The sweetness of malt and the freshness of citrus fruits prevail. The slight spiciness and the subtle hints of sherry aromas hold back a bit but are recognizable. Overall, it provides a pleasantly creamy mouthfeel.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of this whisky is medium-long and warming. It remains very mild and pleasant. A slight dryness spreads across the palate.

conclusion.marcel: A genuine pleasure to drink, hitting the mark perfectly with its gentle sweetness and fresh citrus notes. Horst Lüning demonstrates here that an honest blended malt without any frills can be a real joy – ideal for connoisseurs seeking an uncomplicated companion.
conclusion.sascha: The Horst Lüning Blended Malt Batch 2 impresses with a well-balanced harmony between malt sweetness, citrus freshness, and a subtle sherry note that lends the 10-years blend a refined complexity. Despite its mild structure, it occasionally lacks a bit of depth but remains a solid and well-crafted release in this price range.

faq.question: What sets the Horst Lüning Blended Malt Batch 2 10 Years apart from other blended malts?
faq.answer: The Horst Lüning Blended Malt Batch 2 is an exclusive release from an independent bottler, personally crafted by Horst Lüning. It combines fresh citrus notes, sweet maltiness, and subtle sherry influences, giving it a balanced and creamy texture that distinguishes it from typical standard blends.

faq.question: What role do the sherry casks play in the flavor of this whisky?
faq.answer: Maturation in sherry casks imparts subtle raisin and sherry aromas that remain discreetly in the background. This cask influence complements the sweet malt and citrus notes without overpowering the blend, adding a fine spiciness and complexity.

faq.question: How suitable is the Horst Lüning Blended Malt Batch 2 10 Years for whisky beginners?
faq.answer: With a moderate alcohol content of 46% and a balanced profile of fruity, sweet, and spicy notes, this whisky is quite approachable. Its mild, creamy mouthfeel and pleasant, medium-length finish make it a good choice for beginners looking to explore more complex flavors.

item.name: The Westfalian TW57
item.description: It's hard to believe, but at 8 years old, this single cask single malt whisky from cask 57 is currently one of the older bottlings from Paderborn. Once again, we are excited to see what has been conjured up in our glass.

tasting_notes.nose: A hint of BBQ and the scent of a charred barbecue grill awaken our senses and remind us of summer barbecues. Earthy nuances lend the bouquet a down-to-earth depth, while the gentle hint of an outgoing fireplace adds a nostalgic note. Sweet hints of ripe plums and the tantalising aroma of dates wrapped in bacon round off the olfactory experience.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip reveals a creamy texture that caresses the palate. Cold smoke and ash meet meaty notes reminiscent of a savoury BBQ sauce. The deep, dark chocolate combines harmoniously with a hint of burnt caramel, while the ultra-earthy notes lend an unmistakable depth. Surprising nuances of smoked ham, burnt fairground mushrooms and the aroma of leather and rosemary contribute to a fascinating flavour experience.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long and astringent, with a subtle tart note of Amarena cherries unfolding. Black pepper and a subtle oak presence round off the finish, which ends with a pleasant dryness and a hint of spice.

conclusion.marcel: With its smoky BBQ charm and sweet-savory notes, The Westfalian TW57 is an experience that’s both fun and surprising. It challenges the drinker without ever becoming dull – a true character from Paderborn that leaves you wanting more.
conclusion.sascha: The Westfalian TW57 impresses with its balanced structure and the complex interplay of smoky, sweet, and earthy aromas. The precise balance between intense flavor nuances and the long, spicy finish makes it a technically accomplished single cask whisky with great depth.

faq.question: How does Single Cask Barrel 57 influence the flavor of The Westfalian TW57?
faq.answer: Single Cask Barrel 57 imparts The Westfalian TW57 with its distinctive depth and complexity. The specific barrel maturation brings out smoky BBQ notes, a hint of burnt caramel, as well as earthy and meaty aromas, making the whisky particularly multifaceted.

faq.question: What distinguishes The Westfalian TW57 compared to other German whiskies?
faq.answer: At 8 years old, The Westfalian TW57 is among the older German single malts and stands out with its intense combination of cold smoke, dark chocolate, and unique notes like smoked ham and fairground mushrooms. Additionally, its high alcohol content of 53.9% delivers an intense tasting experience.

faq.question: How should The Westfalian TW57 be best enjoyed to fully appreciate its aromas?
faq.answer: To fully experience the complex aromas of The Westfalian TW57, it is recommended to drink it at room temperature and possibly add a few drops of water. This helps open up the smoky, fruity, and spicy notes and allows the long, astringent finish to come through in full.

item.name: Ruadh Maor 12 Years - Dràm Mòr
item.description: Bottles from the independent bottler Dràm Mòr have been discussed on this page several times before. With great dedication and expertise, Dràm Mòr strives to preserve the tradition of Scotch whisky. They describe their cask selection process as open and open-minded. From the summer selection of 2023, today we have a 12-years Ruadh Maor. Behind this name, which is probably completely unknown to the mass market, lies the smoky distillate of the Glenturret distillery. This cask, with the number #9002123, received additional maturation in a First Fill Bourbon Hogshead.

tasting_notes.nose: The aroma reminds us of a burning, rusty fire barrel. We perceive the smoke of smoldering fire without finding earthier notes of peat or tar. The smoke has a hint of iodine, but by no means as plaster-like as one would expect from Islay malts. After a few minutes in the glass, the First Fill Bourbon cask can unfold its effect. We detect sweet vanilla and a hint of honey.
tasting_notes.palate: Upon first sip, we immediately taste the sweetness of glazed honey bacon. An almost barbecue-like combination of sweet vanilla and meaty smoke unfolds on the palate. The notion of iodine, present in the aroma, has completely disappeared. The mouthfeel is very pleasant. The malt has high viscosity, is slightly dry, and has a tingling dusty quality.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is simple and rather short for a smoky whisky. The delicate smoky profile lingers somewhat. Otherwise, we perceive fine oak notes and some sweet-spicy grill aromas.

conclusion.marcel: This Ruadh Maor delivers exactly what it promises: a smoky campfire experience with a wonderfully sweet bacon note. It’s not the most complex malt, but that’s precisely what makes it so charming and easy to drink – perfect for relaxed evenings by the fire.
conclusion.sascha: A clearly defined smoke profile meets a balanced sweetness here, convincingly reflecting the cask character of the First Fill Bourbon Hogsheads. Despite its straightforward aroma, the malt remains intriguing thanks to its pleasant viscosity and finely tuned finish.

faq.question: What makes the Ruadh Maor 12 Years from Dràm Mòr special compared to other Glenturret releases?
faq.answer: The Ruadh Maor 12 Years is distinguished by its maturation in a First Fill Bourbon Hogshead, which imparts a sweet vanilla note and a complex smoky character. Unlike typical peat smoke, the smoke here is more burning and slightly iodine-rich, without the earthy notes commonly found in Islay whiskies.

faq.question: How does the First Fill Bourbon Hogshead influence the flavors of the Ruadh Maor 12 Years?
faq.answer: The First Fill Bourbon Hogshead gives the whisky sweet vanilla and honey notes that balance the smoky character. This creates a combination of sweet, almost barbecue-like flavors with a pleasantly dry and slightly dusty mouthfeel.

faq.question: Why is the finish of the Ruadh Maor 12 Years relatively short and delicate despite its high alcohol content?
faq.answer: Although the whisky is bottled at a fairly high 56.4% ABV, the maturation in a First Fill Bourbon Hogshead ensures a balanced profile, allowing for a simple, rather short finish with a fine smoky character and subtle oak notes, without being overpowering.

item.name: I Am Root, Too! - The Spirits Alchemist
item.description: Sebastian Büssing aka The Spirits Alchemist is known for his experimental barrels. Here - for the first time? - a sugar beet barrel was used for the 12-month finish after the single malt was allowed to mature for four years in an ex-bourbon barrel. We are more than excited!

image.alt: I Am Root, Too! - The Spirits Alchemist

tasting_notes.nose: The nose is greeted by a rich aroma of vanilla and malt, infused with sweet notes of sugar, perhaps even sugar cane. Earthy nuances are present, accompanied by a hint of smoke, which is really minimal. A slight presence of quince and even a hint of what reminds you of socks after a football match round off the unusual flavour profile.
tasting_notes.palate: At the first sip, the question of a hint of smoke remains in the air. What is clear, however, is the intense earthiness that dominates the palate. It's almost as if you're biting straight into the grass, while sweet notes clearly recede into the background. Surprising elements such as peanut butter and eucalyptus mingle with the flavours of rock sugar and rooty vegetables.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is of short to medium duration and brings out the earth and a loamy note. A hint of vanilla and a slight influence of oak round off the finish.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that definitely divides opinion with its unusual finish in a sugar beet cask – it surprises with earthy notes and a hint of football sock aroma, something you don't come across every day. For connoisseurs who enjoy venturing off the beaten path, "I Am Root, Too!" is an intriguing companion full of character and potential surprises.
conclusion.sascha: This 5-years single malt impresses with its complex balance between sweet vanilla and malt notes and a dominant earthy structure, which gains an unusual spiciness from the sugar beet finish. Despite its high strength, it feels well integrated, although the short finish with its clayey accents won’t be to everyone’s taste.

faq.question: How does the sugar beet cask influence the flavor profile of 'I Am Root, Too!'?
faq.answer: The 12-month finish in the sugar beet cask imparts earthy and sweet notes reminiscent of sugar and root vegetables. It creates an unusual combination of intense earthiness, candied sugar, and a subtle smokiness that clearly sets the aroma apart from a classic bourbon finish.

faq.question: Why is the alcohol content of 62.1% so high in this whisky?
faq.answer: The high alcohol content of 62.1% results from a cask strength bottling, where the whisky is not diluted before bottling. This preserves the full intensity of the flavors and allows the drinker to dilute the whisky with water according to their personal preference.

faq.question: What role does maturation in the ex-bourbon cask play before the finish in the sugar beet cask?
faq.answer: The four-year maturation in the ex-bourbon cask provides the foundation with vanilla and malt notes. This classic cask aging creates the base structure onto which the subsequent finish in the sugar beet cask adds its earthy and sweet nuances, resulting in a complex flavor profile.

item.name: Penderyn Myth
item.description: The Welsh Penderyn whisky called Myth from the Dragon series was matured in ex-Buffalo Trace casks and finished in ex-red wine casks. Seems very promising.

tasting_notes.nose: Notes of chewing gum, grape and sugar dance harmoniously together, followed by a hint of molasses and light hay. A light floral note hovers over everything, while a gentle creamy note and light stone fruits round off the olfactory experience.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip reveals a remarkable combination of grassy and hay-like flavours accompanied by floral notes. The texture is creamy and creamy, with a hint of sweetness and a delicate pear flavour that caresses the palate.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the Penderyn Myth is short and surprisingly characterised by an enormous bitterness from the oak. A slight dryness remains on the palate, while the finish is perceived as somewhat unexciting overall.

conclusion.marcel: Penderyn Myth is a smooth pleasure with a playful interplay of aromas that’s enjoyable, even though the finish, with its oak bitterness, slightly dampens the fun. For a relaxed dram in between, it’s definitely a good choice that shows character without becoming too complicated.
conclusion.sascha: Penderyn Myth impresses with a balanced creaminess and floral nuances, but loses harmony due to a brief, very bitter finish. Overall, it’s a solid composition that would have benefited from a stronger balance between fruitiness and oak.

faq.question: How does the finish in ex-red wine casks influence the taste of Penderyn Myth?
faq.answer: The finish in ex-red wine casks imparts additional fruity and floral notes to Penderyn Myth, which blend with the grassy and hay-like aromas. This results in a more complex aromatic profile with a subtle sweetness and a hint of stone fruits.

faq.question: Why is the finish of Penderyn Myth described as bitter and short?
faq.answer: The finish is marked by the intense bitterness of the oak, which develops during cask maturation. This bitterness creates a dry note on the palate that contrasts with the otherwise creamy and sweet character of the whisky, making the finish feel somewhat uninspiring overall.

faq.question: What are the unique aspects of Penderyn Myth’s production compared to other Welsh whiskies?
faq.answer: Penderyn Myth initially matures in ex-Buffalo Trace bourbon barrels and then receives its finish in ex-red wine casks. This combination is unusual for Welsh whiskies and creates the distinctive blend of sweet, fruity, and floral aromas with a creamy texture and a unique bitterness profile on the finish.

item.name: Penderyn Legend
item.description: The Welsh Penderyn whisky called Legend from the Dragon series was matured in ex-Buffalo Trace casks and finished in Madeira casks. An exciting fusion of two worlds of flavour.

tasting_notes.nose: Nosing the Penderyn Legend reveals sweet honey, fresh green apples and a light pear sweetness. A malty vanilla note gently dominates, accompanied by a hint of Obstler (schnapps made from fruit, mostly produced in Germany). The freshness is remarkable, with a bright, spicy presence.
tasting_notes.palate: The flavour of the Singe Malt from Wales is initially sweet and malty, with a hint of brown sugar that lends a warm and inviting note. A creamy, almost waxy texture envelops the palate, while fruity flavours of mango and blackberries unfold. A slice of apple pie mingles with a subtle citrus freshness of lemon zest, giving the flavour a balanced complexity.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium long and slightly spicy, with a subtle note of burnt oak. A drop of peach tea lends a soothing quality, while a youthful note flashes at the end.

conclusion.marcel: Penderyn Legend brings fun to the glass with its sweet fruity notes and creamy texture, even though the Madeira finish could, in my opinion, show a bit more punch. A pleasant companion for relaxed evenings, it scores with its youthful freshness but doesn’t create any drama.
conclusion.sascha: The combination of ex-Buffalo Trace and Madeira cask maturation provides a solid structure with balanced sweetness and a light spiciness, although the Madeira finish remains somewhat restrained. Overall, a well-balanced single malt that, however, lacks a deeper cask intensity to add more complexity.

faq.question: What makes the Madeira cask finish in Penderyn Legend special?
faq.answer: The Madeira cask finish imparts a fruity sweetness and subtle spiciness to Penderyn Legend, enriching the aroma with notes of dried fruit and a delicate acidity.

faq.question: How does maturation in ex-Buffalo Trace casks influence the flavor of this whisky?
faq.answer: The ex-Buffalo Trace casks contribute warm vanilla and caramel notes to the whisky, which combine harmoniously with the fruity freshness of the Madeira finish.

faq.question: Why does Penderyn Legend have no age statement (NAS)?
faq.answer: Penderyn Legend is bottled without an age statement to achieve an optimal flavor balance by blending various younger and more mature casks, without committing to a specific age.

item.name: Penderyn Celt
item.description: The Welsh Penderyn whisky named Celt from the Dragon series was matured in ex-Buffalo Trace casks and finished in ex-Laphroaig casks. An exciting combination.

tasting_notes.nose: Starting with the Ex-Laphroaig peated cask, we perceive light phenolic smoke, some fire smoke and an idea of salty coast. The ex-bourbon cask then delivers vanilla-sweet notes, neutral chewing gum flavour and some orange peel. A good match.
tasting_notes.palate: The Penderyn Celt plays out both flavours here. Medicinal, phenolic smoke flavours with a little iodine meet vanilla, cane sugar, slightly floral and hay-like notes. Somewhat tart grapefruit and green apple round off the flavour.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long to long Finish is characterised by cold smoke with a maritime touch.

conclusion.marcel: The Penderyn Celt is an uncomplicated malt that pleasantly surprises with its smoky and vanilla blend – ideal for anyone looking for an affordable entry into Welsh whisky. While it lacks the depth of an older malt, it’s precisely this fresh youthfulness that makes it charming and easy to drink.
conclusion.sascha: With a balanced interplay between phenolic smoke and sweet bourbon notes, the Penderyn Celt impresses through clear cask integration and a clean, maritime-influenced finish. Despite its rather simple aromatic profile, it shows a solid structure, though it falls short of expectations in intensity and complexity.

faq.question: How do the ex-Buffalo Trace and ex-Laphroaig casks influence the flavor of the Penderyn Celt?
faq.answer: Maturation in ex-Buffalo Trace casks imparts vanilla-sweet notes and a hint of orange peel, while the finish in ex-Laphroaig casks adds smoky, phenolic aromas with a maritime touch and a salty coastal character.

faq.question: Why does the Penderyn Celt have no age statement (No Age Statement)?
faq.answer: Penderyn uses a combination of different maturation periods for this whisky to achieve the desired flavor profile without committing to a specific age. This results in a complex blend without a fixed age statement.

faq.question: How does the Penderyn Celt pair with food or occasions?
faq.answer: Thanks to its balanced mix of smoky, sweet, and fruity notes, the Penderyn Celt pairs well with grilled fish, mild cheeses, or as an aperitif for convivial evenings.

item.name: Owensboro - Anam na h-Alba
item.description: The German Independent Bottler Anam na h-Alba from Oberhausen proudly labels itself as "The Soul of Scotland." Hence, it was quite surprising when we stumbled upon a Kentucky Straight Bourbon bottled by them. The label promises a just shy of four years old Single Barrel bottling from Owensboro, seemingly distilled by Green River Distilling from Owensboro, Kentucky. Let's dive into what this cask-strength whiskey has to offer.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose is immediately hit with the full flavor payload of a cask-strength bourbon. The scent is intensely nutty and oaky, showcasing the impressive work done by the fresh cask. Notes of burnt caramel and a subtle leather undertone emerge. After letting the whiskey rest for a bit, fruity notes reveal themselves—thinking of exotic fruits like pineapple or passion fruit.
tasting_notes.palate: The young bourbon starts of intense on the palate. Sweet caramel, pronounced oak, and a wonderfully oily viscosity present themselves. Add to that a slight tingling of white pepper – all without the 59.9% feeling overpowering. While we initially thought of peanuts on the nose, the taste leans towards a spicier profile reminiscent of nutmeg. Tropical fruit flavors reappear, accompanied by a faint hint of solvent, likely owing to its youth.
tasting_notes.finish: In the medium-length finish, no big surprises await. The intense oaky and nutty characteristics linger, leaving behind a delightful aroma. A subtle caramel sweetness complements the rather heavy, spicy notes.

conclusion.marcel: A bourbon that’s fun with its raw power and intense aromatics – perfect for those who like it rough and honest. The nutty-caramel notes bring drinking pleasure, even if the edges and corners sometimes feel a bit coarse. Not a collector’s item, but a solid enjoyment for an easygoing evening.
conclusion.sascha: Technically clean and presenting a convincing balance between sweetness, spice, and oak, this cask-strength bourbon from Owensboro shows a robust structure. However, it lacks the fine complexity that would round out its character a bit more. Overall, a well-made, if somewhat undifferentiated, single barrel bourbon.

faq.question: Why is a German bottler responsible for a Kentucky Straight Bourbon?
faq.answer: The independent German bottler Anam na h-Alba purchases cask-strength whisky from distilleries like Green River in Owensboro, Kentucky, and releases it under their own label. This way, they combine their expertise with American bourbon.

faq.question: How does the cask strength of 59.9% affect the taste of this young bourbon?
faq.answer: The high cask strength delivers intense aromas and an oily texture without the alcohol feeling harsh. It accentuates spicy notes such as white pepper and nutmeg more prominently.

faq.question: What role does the age of just under four years play in the aroma profile of the Anam na h-Alba Owensboro Bourbon?
faq.answer: The young age is reflected in fruity and spicy notes as well as a slight solvent character. At the same time, maturation in fresh casks provides strong oak and nutty aromas.

item.name: Balcones Texas Rye
item.description: Balcones use several types of rye in the mash for their Texas Rye. These include Elbon rye from the north-west of Texas as well as crystal, chocolate and roasted rye.

tasting_notes.nose: Rye opens properly, with lots of chocolate. There is also some salted caramel, malted grain and a tiny hint of smoke on the nose. The chocolate rye is particularly interesting here and makes the whisky seem like a rye beer.
tasting_notes.palate: Chilli chocolate - intensely flavoured and yet light. The Balcones Texas Rye Whisky whets the appetite for more with aromas of rye spice, German grey bread and something malt-like with oak spice and even more dark chocolate. Beautifully balanced, even if still a little youthful in places.
tasting_notes.finish: Chocolate and some leather remain in the medium-long finish.

conclusion.marcel: The Balcones Texas Rye brings plenty of fun with its chocolaty rye spice and breathes fresh life into the glass. Sure, its youth is still evident, but that’s exactly what gives it a lively, almost cheeky personality – definitely a rye that’s enjoyable and piques your curiosity for more.
conclusion.sascha: A well-structured rye with a balanced interplay between chocolaty notes and a spicy rye profile, though clearly marked by its youth. The blend of different rye varieties creates interesting aromas, but it’s still uncertain whether longer maturation might smooth out some of the edges.

faq.question: Which rye varieties are used in Balcones Texas Rye and how do they influence the flavor?
faq.answer: Balcones uses several rye varieties, including Elbon rye from Northwest Texas as well as crystal, chocolate, and roasted rye. This combination gives the whisky a complex rye character with chocolaty and spicy aromas reminiscent of rye beer.

faq.question: Why does Balcones Texas Rye have no age statement (NAS) and does this affect its quality?
faq.answer: Balcones Texas Rye is a no age statement whisky, meaning it contains whiskies of various ages without a fixed minimum maturation period. This allows the producer to blend the best barrels for a balanced flavor profile without compromising quality.

faq.question: How does the 50% ABV affect the drinking experience of Balcones Texas Rye?
faq.answer: At 50% ABV, Balcones Texas Rye offers an intense aroma and depth of flavor that particularly highlights the spicy and chocolaty notes. The higher alcohol content delivers a powerful yet still balanced character.

item.name: Torabhaig Allt Gleann The Legacy Series
item.description: Founded in 2017, Torabhaig is only the second distillery on the Isle of Skye, alongside the famous Talisker distillery. Built by the independent bottler Mossburn, it produces peaty single malt whiskies. Since 2021, small batches have been released as part of the Legacy Series. The Allt Gleann Legacy Series is only the second of these bottlings. Torabhaig demonstrates its integrity here: no filtration, no coloring, and a wonderful 46% ABV.

image.alt: Torabhaig Allt Gleann The Legacy Series

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, it becomes clear what it's all about: the smoke profile takes center stage. Refined sugar and vanilla from the bourbon casks form the foundation. Barbecue bacon is ever-present. The smoke presents itself mineral-maritime, almost asphalt-like. We think of a rocky pebble beach.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the peat fire is also in the foreground. A smoldering campfire surrounded by sugary sweetness and a hint of malt. Here, the maritime flavors of seaweed and sea salt become slightly more prominent. There's also a certain vegetable-like quality that we would describe as white beans and parsnips.
tasting_notes.finish: On the finish, Torabhaig is long, dry, and powerfully salty. The central theme of smoke, ash, coast, and sea continues. In the finish, there's a hint of oak - like a barrel of pickles.

conclusion.marcel: Torabhaig Allt Gleann captivates with its smoky character and maritime notes that are genuinely enjoyable. Despite its youth, the whisky feels surprisingly rounded and invites relaxed evenings by the fireplace. A promising representative from the Isle of Skye that leaves you wanting more!
conclusion.sascha: With a clearly defined smoke profile and a well-balanced coastal maturation, Torabhaig Allt Gleann demonstrates an impressive harmony between mineral freshness and sweet bourbon notes. The structure feels mature and harmonious despite the lack of an age statement, with maritime saltiness shaping the finish. A technically convincing single malt with great potential for development.

faq.question: What distinguishes Torabhaig Allt Gleann The Legacy Series from other Isle of Skye whiskies?
faq.answer: Torabhaig Allt Gleann The Legacy Series stands out due to its intense, mineral and maritime-influenced smoke profile, setting it apart from the well-known Talisker distillery. Additionally, Torabhaig avoids chill filtration and coloring, making the whisky particularly natural and unadulterated.

faq.question: How does maturation in bourbon casks influence the flavor of this whisky?
faq.answer: Maturation in bourbon casks imparts sweet notes of vanilla and refined sugar to Torabhaig Allt Gleann, complementing its peaty, smoky profile. This combination creates a balanced interplay between sweetness and smoke, supported by the cask-driven notes on the finish.

faq.question: Why is the whisky not age-stated, and how does this affect the flavor?
faq.answer: Torabhaig Allt Gleann The Legacy Series is a so-called 'No Age Statement' whisky, meaning no minimum age is specified. This allows the master distiller to blend different vintages and cask types to achieve a balanced flavor profile characterized by smoky, maritime, and sweet notes.

item.name: Begemann Whisky Eichenschild 5 Years
item.description: The Gutshofbrennerei Begatal has been producing whisky hyperlocally since 2016. Only raw materials from the Lippe region are used. The family tree of the family business can be traced back to 1284 - the building in which the distillery was built has been standing since 1535.

tasting_notes.nose: The Begemann opens with damp grass and unpleasant petrol. Unfortunately, the smell of new wellies with metallic notes doesn't make the whole thing any better.
tasting_notes.palate: The single malt whisky can best be described as watery with a little sugar, vomit and ethanol. With some sandpit sand.
tasting_notes.finish: There is virtually no finish, apart from a slight afterburn.

conclusion.marcel: Someone clearly tried to rush things – this whisky feels like it’s been parked in a metal tank for five years, lacking any real joy or character. There’s a bit of consolation in the hyper-local approach, but flavor-wise it’s rather disappointing.
conclusion.sascha: The structure of the Begemann Eichenschild comes across as unbalanced and watery, accompanied by unpleasant metallic and chemical notes. Despite its regional ambitions, this 5-years malt lacks maturity and harmonious complexity.

faq.question: What raw materials are used for Begemann Whisky Eichenschild 5 Years?
faq.answer: Begemann Whisky Eichenschild 5 Years uses exclusively raw materials sourced from the Lipperland region. The hyper-local production ensures a regional origin of the ingredients.

faq.question: How does the 5-year age affect the taste of Begemann Whisky?
faq.answer: With only 5 years of maturation, the whisky is still relatively young, which results in a rather watery mouthfeel and a less pronounced finish. Complex aromas typically found in older whiskies are barely present here.

faq.question: Why does Begemann Whisky Eichenschild 5 Years smell like petrol and new rubber boots?
faq.answer: Unusual aromas such as petrol and new rubber boots can be attributed to the young aging process and the type of storage. Such impressions are not uncommon in young whiskies that have matured atypically.

item.name: Loch Lomond 18 Years
item.description: The distillery Loch Lomond, named after the famous lake it resides by, is nestled in the heart of the Scottish Highlands. Renowned for its versatility, it produces a wide range of whiskies: from smoky single malts distilled in pot stills, grain whiskies produced on Coffey stills, to even the "Glen's Vodka". Today, we sample the Loch Lomond 18 Year Single Malt, one of their flagship releases.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, this single malt presents an intriguing combination of roasted aromas and exotic fruitiness. We detect caramel and toasted pine nuts, accompanied by hints of grapefruit and a grainy banana bread. The fruity sweetness is enveloped in a light but cool smoke, reminiscent of egg carton (which may sound negative, but is not meant as such).
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, Loch Lomond 18 begins with a fruity sweetness. The mouthfeel is soft and oily, spreading astringently and presenting a full-bodied berry note with hints of plums and oranges. Additionally, there are exotic citrus-like flavors. After a short while, the aroma shifts to herbal mint notes and surprisingly clear phenolic almost rubber-like smoke.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium-length and warming, leaving an enduring impression of spicy oak and a hint of white pepper, without being sharp. Tobacco and tea leaves round off the experience.

conclusion.marcel: Loch Lomond 18 is a pleasant companion for relaxed evenings, offering fun with its fruity and spicy character. Despite the harmonious aromas, I miss a little spark of surprise that would elevate this malt to a true highlight. It's a pity that E150 slightly clouds the natural character – that really wasn’t necessary.
conclusion.sascha: The Loch Lomond 18 impresses with a solid balance of fruity and smoky notes that create a complex structure. The medium-length maturation reveals itself elegantly, although the caramel color loses some authenticity due to the use of caramel coloring. Overall, a well-crafted single malt with minor compromises in purity.

faq.question: What makes the Loch Lomond 18 Years special compared to other Highland whiskies?
faq.answer: The Loch Lomond 18 Years stands out through its combination of smoky pot still notes and fruity sweetness. Its versatility comes from the use of different stills, giving it a unique profile with roasted aromas, exotic fruits, and a hint of phenolic smoke.

faq.question: How does the 18-year age influence the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: The 18 years of maturation provide a balanced interplay of soft, oily textures and complex flavors like caramel, oak, and spices. The extended aging smooths out sharp edges and brings forward spicy and fruity notes along with a warming finish.

faq.question: What is the recommended drinking temperature for Loch Lomond 18 Years to best reveal its aromas?
faq.answer: Loch Lomond 18 Years should ideally be tasted at room temperature (around 18-20 °C). At this temperature, the complex aromas of roasted pine nuts, exotic fruits, and delicate smoke unfold best. A few drops of water can also help to release hidden nuances.

item.name: Springbank 15 Years
item.description: The distillery Springbank is one of the few remaining Scottish distilleries that have not been acquired by any major corporation. Additionally, it is one of the few distilleries in the historic region of Campbeltown. Known for its traditional production methods and its distinctive, artisanal heritage, Springbank has been hard to overlook recently. Today, we're tasting the 15-years expression.

tasting_notes.nose: Upon nosing, robust fruity notes greet us immediately. We encounter a generous portion of sherry accompanied by dark chocolate, roasted dates, and a hint of caramel. In the background, there's a subtle citrus note. The velvety aroma is rounded off by light smoke and some maritime notes.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, this Springbank is also very robust. Strong spice and pleasant oak underpin a bouquet of nuts and dried fruits. Allowing it some time in the mouth reveals leathery flavors, raisins, figs, and a touch of earthiness.
tasting_notes.finish: The Single Malt continues its expected trajectory in the finish as well. Robust, powerful, spicy, and very long. We discover great malty sweetness and the lingering aromas of oak, leather, and sherry.

conclusion.marcel: The Springbank 15 captivates with its multi-layered character and creamy indulgence – a true Campbeltown treasure that’s a joy to drink. Despite the hefty price tag, you can definitely treat yourself to the dramatic pleasure of this artisanal malt, even if the hype seems a bit overblown.
conclusion.sascha: An impressively balanced composition of sherry, oak, and spicy notes that gives the 15-years Springbank a strong structural backbone. The complex harmony justifies the craftsmanship, though price and availability remain points to scrutinize critically.

faq.question: What makes the production method of Springbank 15 years special compared to other Scotch whiskies?
faq.answer: Springbank 15 years is produced entirely on-site at the distillery—from malt preparation through distillation to maturation. The distillery employs traditional methods, including triple distillation and extended aging in sherry casks, which impart the whisky’s complex character.

faq.question: How does the origin from the Campbeltown region influence the taste of Springbank 15 years?
faq.answer: Campbeltown is known for its maritime influences and lightly smoky whiskies. In the Springbank 15 years, these traits are reflected in subtle sea notes, gentle smoke, and a spicy depth typical of this historic region.

faq.question: Which aromas are particularly noticeable when tasting Springbank 15 years?
faq.answer: On the nose, fruity sherry notes dominate, along with dark chocolate, roasted dates, and caramel, accented by hints of citrus and light smoke. On the palate, spicy oak flavors, nuts, dried fruits, as well as leathery and earthy nuances emerge, accompanied in the long finish by malty sweetness and wood tones.

item.name: Springbank 10 Years
item.description: The 10-years single malt whisky from the Springbank distillery in Campbeltown, Scotland, has been the talk of the town in recent years and has experienced a real hype - unfortunately also in terms of price. In the meantime, the hype is slowly dying down and you can now and again get hold of a bottle at the RRP (yes, that's also high).

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression is slightly alcoholic and rather simple. After a while in the glass, the flavours unfold. The usual salty, dirty earthiness of Springbank whiskies spreads out. The flavours then move towards amaretto, marzipan and almonds. A subtle layer of smoke hovers above this. If you allow more time to pass, the nose takes on a sweetish flavour. We think of blossom and forest honey, as well as butter biscuits and white tea. Now and again, there are hints of spicy oak.
tasting_notes.palate: We find many delicate and well-balanced flavours in the taste. Light smoke is present again. However, as in the nose, this remains more of a subtle companion. Now it starts with a pleasant fruit acidity, with lots of oak and a good portion of dried fruit. Accompanied by sea asparagus. A few sips later, aromas of banana chips, honey and almost burnt almonds dominate the flavour, which can be described as round and mild.
tasting_notes.finish: The malty finish is of medium length and brings sweet almonds, spicy oak and a little pepper kick.

conclusion.marcel: Springbank 10 Year brings real joy to the glass with its sweet-salty complexity and delicate smoke – a true characterful dram that’s a lot of fun, though at this price you really need to be a big fan.
conclusion.sascha: A well-balanced single malt with a harmonious structure and subtle smoky note, impressing with its complexity, but given the high price, it doesn’t quite offer the best value for money.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Springbank 10 Year compared to other Campbeltown whiskies?
faq.answer: Springbank 10 Year combines the typical salty, earthy grittiness of Campbeltown with a fine balance of amaretto, almonds, and a subtle smoky note. This combination makes it complex yet approachable, setting it apart from other whiskies in the region.

faq.question: How should you best enjoy the Springbank 10 Year to fully experience its aromas?
faq.answer: It’s best to let the whisky breathe in the glass for a few minutes after pouring, allowing the aromas of amaretto, honey, and smoke to unfold. It works well neat or with a small splash of water to better highlight the fruity acidity and oak notes.

faq.question: Why is the Springbank 10 Year so complex in flavor despite being only 10 years old?
faq.answer: Springbank uses a combination of different cask types and a traditional production method involving partial triple distillation. This creates a layered aromatic profile where fruity, spicy, and smoky notes blend harmoniously, even though the whisky has matured for just 10 years.

item.name: Green Spot
item.description: Green Spot, an Irish single pot still whiskey, invites connoisseurs on a journey through the lush landscapes and vibrant flavours of Ireland. With its roots in Midleton's distilling heritage, this expression promises a flavour of tradition with a modern twist.

tasting_notes.nose: Upon nosing, Green Spot unveils a bouquet of familiar scents. Notes of malted biscuits and ripe peach mingle with the sweetness of honey and the crispness of red apple. There's a whimsical touch of cotton candy, balanced by the assertive presence of oak and subtle hints of potato starch.
tasting_notes.palate: With each sip, the Whiskey delights the palate with a symphony of flavors. Apricot and peach dance gracefully alongside the juicy essence of apple and pear. A gentle spiciness adds depth, while the lingering bitterness of oak lends complexity to the experience.
tasting_notes.finish: The journey concludes with a flourish of peppery warmth and a subtle undercurrent of bittersweet chocolate. The finish is long, leaving a lasting impression that beckons another sip.

conclusion.marcel: Green Spot delivers a lot of drinking pleasure with its fruity freshness and a hint of cotton candy, even though the oak can sometimes be a bit too prominent. For fans of the Irish character, it’s a pleasant companion, though it doesn’t quite reach the heights of the great classics.
conclusion.sascha: With a balanced combination of fruity notes and a noticeable oak bitterness, Green Spot shows a solid structure that occasionally lacks fullness. A typical representative of the Midleton style, it scores points for its balance but loses some depth compared to premium whiskies.

faq.question: What distinguishes Green Spot from other Irish whiskeys?
faq.answer: Green Spot is a Single Pot Still whiskey made exclusively from malted and unmalted barley at a single distillery. This results in a complex flavor profile with fruity and spicy notes typical of this traditional Irish production method.

faq.question: How should Green Spot ideally be served to best experience its aroma?
faq.answer: Green Spot reveals its full aroma best when enjoyed neat or with a splash of still water. This way, the fruity notes of peach, apple, and pear, along with the delicate oak tones and peppery spice, come through particularly well.

faq.question: What role does cask maturation play in Green Spot, even though no age statement is given?
faq.answer: Even without an age statement, Green Spot benefits from careful maturation in a combination of bourbon and sherry casks. This cask mix gives the whiskey its balanced interplay between sweet fruit aromas and spicy oak notes.

item.name: Gilors 10 Years PX Sherry
item.description: Very good spirits are known to come from the Henrich distillery. The Gilors 10 Year PX Sherry, a whisky from this very distillery, was bottled in 2021 at 60.1% ABV after maturing for 3 years in an ex-islay cask and 7 years in a Pedro Ximénez sherry cask. 10-year matured single malts from Germany are still rare at the moment.

tasting_notes.nose: Intense and rich sherry flavours, almost like a sip from a real PX sherry bottle, open the mouth. Espresso, cinnamon, caramel, roasted almonds, honey marinade, light tobacco notes and some fruit acidity follow in quick succession. After the nose has become accustomed, the peat-smoke flavours are slightly perceptible, but really very subtle.
tasting_notes.palate: Musty, yet powerfully sweet and with spicy fruit acidity, the malt is heavy on the tongue. There is a clear presence of sherry notes and dark fruits. After a while, the espresso notes and some dark chocolate are also present. The light peat smoke in the nose turns into a real peaty flavour - more earthy with old leather and not really smoky.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish can be described as rather tart, medium-long and a little bit bitter with light nutty notes.

conclusion.marcel: With its full cask strength and intense PX sherry notes, the Gilors 10 Years is a true experience for connoisseurs who appreciate power and complexity. A sip that tells stories – from espresso to subtle peatiness – while delivering great drinking pleasure. Not a whisky to rush through, but one that deserves time and leisure.
conclusion.sascha: The Gilors 10 Years PX Sherry impresses with its balanced structure between sweet sherry aromas and subtle peat, with the 60.1% cask strength well balanced and easily fine-tuned with water. The complex aroma profile and the medium-length, slightly bitter-nutty finish make it a remarkable German single malt that shows no loss of balance despite its strength.

faq.question: How does the combination of ex-Islay and Pedro Ximénez casks influence the taste of the Gilors 10 Years PX Sherry?
faq.answer: The whisky spent its first 3 years maturing in ex-Islay casks, which impart subtle peat and smoky notes. The following 7 years in Pedro Ximénez sherry casks add intense sherry aromas such as caramel, dark fruits, and spices. This combination creates a complex balance of sweet, spicy, and lightly smoky flavor notes.

faq.question: Why are 10-years single malts from Germany particularly noteworthy?
faq.answer: German single malts aged for 10 years are still quite rare on the market. Many German distilleries produce younger whiskies, so a 10-years malt like the Gilors PX Sherry represents a special rarity and quality.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 60.1% affect the drinking experience?
faq.answer: At 60.1% ABV, the Gilors 10 Years PX Sherry is a powerful whisky that brings out intense aromas and flavor nuances especially clearly. For a balanced tasting experience, many enthusiasts recommend adding a little water to better highlight the flavor profiles and soften the sharpness.

item.name: Port Ellen (1982) - Malts of Scotland
item.description: It's not every day that you get the chance to taste a 1982' Port Ellen (with no further age statement). So we are all the more delighted to be able to taste such a special dram. The distillery has not produced whisky since 1983, and a reopening is planned for this year. Getting hold of bottlings - especially at reasonable prices - is almost impossible at the moment.

tasting_notes.nose: We are sitting in a small old wooden hut by the sea on an old leather sofa with a view of the Port Ellen distillery on the island of Islay in Scotland. In front of us is a buffet of dried dates wrapped in bacon, figs, sea asparagus and some good German wholemeal bread, which smells wonderful. It is accompanied by salted caramel and chocolate-coated coffee beans. A gentle sea breeze blows through the open window and light smoke from the campfire with paprika, stick bread and smoked bacon.
tasting_notes.palate: We reach for the bowl with a varied mix of red berries, which are fruity, sweet and quite creamy. A few berries taste a little strange. They are the redcurrants - they must have been lying in ashes from an old barbecue. A mill with white pepper and a salt mill are on the table, probably for the peppers on the skewers by the campfire. The mills certainly won't be for our mint and herb tea. Now we are served the smoked bacon - it goes surprisingly well with mint tea. It starts to rain outside.
tasting_notes.finish: The campfire extinguishes. Woody and ashy flavours linger for a long time, slightly musty from the rain and yet dusty and dry.

conclusion.marcel: This Port Ellen 1982 is an unforgettable dance between smoky power and deep, fruity notes – a true fireworks display for the senses that leaves you wanting more. If you don’t enjoy this, maybe you should give whisky another try. A genuine experience that shows just how much character a single dram can hold!
conclusion.sascha: The Port Ellen 1982 impresses with its balanced combination of intense cask strength and complex maturation, delivering a precisely structured aroma profile that blends smoky and fruity facets. Despite its power, it feels surprisingly harmonious and multi-layered, making it a benchmark for high-quality Islay whiskies.

faq.question: Why does the Port Ellen 1982 have no age statement, even though the year 1982 is mentioned?
faq.answer: The year 1982 indicates the distillation year, not the whisky’s age at bottling. Since there is no age statement on the label, the exact age was not officially declared. Such releases often come from different casks with slightly varying ages.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 58.1% affect the taste and drinking experience of this whisky?
faq.answer: At 58.1% ABV, this whisky is very powerful and intense. The high alcohol content amplifies aromas and flavor depth but can also come across as sharp. It’s recommended to add a few drops of water to better release the aromas and make the whisky more approachable.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Port Ellen 1982 from Malts of Scotland compared to other Islay whiskies?
faq.answer: This Port Ellen comes from a distillery that has been closed since 1983, making it particularly rare. The Malts of Scotland bottling showcases a complex combination of maritime, smoky, and fruity notes with an unusual ashy and musty character in the finish, shaped by its age and maturation conditions.

item.name: Campbeltown Loch Edition (2023)
item.description: Springbank has been the talk of the town in recent years. Now that prices have slowly normalised, one could almost dare to buy a bottle of the 10 years or even reach for the cask strength? However, if you want to get to grips with the Campbeltown region, there is - alongside Glen Scotia - the Campbeltown Loch Blended Malt, which is released almost every year. Today we have the 2023 version in our glass.

tasting_notes.nose: Cold smoke and a wet towel open up your nose. Sounds worse than it is. With a little kiwi, cement and green apple, the result is a really exciting olfactory profile, as the flavours almost form a work of art in composition. Incidentally, every now and then there is a flash of an odour reminiscent of a chainsaw after pruning.
tasting_notes.palate: The blended malt from the Cambeltown region is oily and soft on the tongue. A very special pepper - namely Madagascar pepper (the flavour - not the spiciness) opens intensely, but clears the way relatively quickly for an almost dried-out riverbed of bittersweet coffee chocolate.
tasting_notes.finish: The long finish is accompanied by cold smoke, a little oak and spices such as cinnamon and nutmeg.

conclusion.marcel: This Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023 is like a jazzy evening with friends – imperfect, lively, and full of surprises. The smoky and fruity notes run wild without losing any of the fun. For anyone looking for something different, this is a genuine drinking pleasure with character.
conclusion.sascha: The Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023 impresses with its balanced structure between smoke, fruitiness, and spices that blend harmoniously. Its complex aroma profile and long finish make it a solid example of a well-priced Campbeltown blended malt with good balance.

faq.question: What sets the Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023 apart from other Campbeltown whiskies?
faq.answer: The Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023 is a blended malt that combines typical Campbeltown notes like cold smoke and spicy aromas with an unusual mix of kiwi, green apple, and a hint of chainsaw wood. It offers an oily and soft texture with an intense flavor of Madagascar pepper and bittersweet coffee chocolate, distinguishing it from single malts from the region.

faq.question: Why does the Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023 have no age statement (NAS)?
faq.answer: The Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023 is a NAS whisky (No Age Statement), meaning no minimum age is indicated on the bottle. This allows the producer to blend different vintages to ensure a consistent flavor profile or to incorporate new aromas.

faq.question: How does the 46% alcohol content affect the taste of the Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023?
faq.answer: At 46% ABV, the Campbeltown Loch Edition 2023 is slightly above the usual standard of 40-43%. This higher alcohol content enhances the aromas, provides more depth, and delivers a more intense mouthfeel without being too harsh, particularly supporting the spicy notes and the long smoky finish.

item.name: Bunnahabhain 12 Years
item.description: Matured 75% in bourbon barrels and 25% in sherry casks, the Bunnahabhain 12 Years is bottled at a decent 46.3% ABV. Nice for a standard, by the way: no colouring or chill-filtering was used.

tasting_notes.nose: The single malt has very pleasant flavours. We smell a varied palette of fruits, such as Nashi pears and apricots, nectarines, quinces and mirabelle plums. This is followed by sweet brown sugar with a fine sherry fruit note. We think of plums and dates. The nose is rounded off with some oak and a hint of liquorice.
tasting_notes.palate: In the tasting, the somewhat more bitter flavours come to the fore. Baking cocoa, oat flakes and a good portion of oak open up. Vanilla, black tea, pear and something pleasantly sour - perhaps green apple - are found in the middle section. Later, the malt opens up further and brings orange peel and some cinnamon. Round, creamy and soft.
tasting_notes.finish: Pleasant and maritime, the whisky from the island of Islay remains long-lasting, warming and slightly peppery.

conclusion.marcel: A true delight for anyone who enjoys a fruity-spicy profile that's also well-rounded. The Bunnahabhain 12 delivers plenty of drinking pleasure without any pretension – perfect for relaxed evenings. Highly recommended, especially when you catch it on sale!
conclusion.sascha: Well-balanced and structured, this Islay malt reveals a harmonious blend of fruit, oak, and a subtle maritime note. At 46.3% ABV and non-chill filtered, the Bunnahabhain 12 offers solid value for money, even if its complexity might feel a bit restrained to some.

faq.question: Why does the Bunnahabhain 12 Year omit chill filtration and coloring?
faq.answer: The Bunnahabhain 12 Year is bottled without chill filtration and without added coloring to preserve the whisky’s natural character and aromas as authentically as possible. This results in a fuller mouthfeel and a genuine color.

faq.question: How does maturation in bourbon and sherry casks influence the flavor of the Bunnahabhain 12 Year?
faq.answer: With 75% maturation in bourbon casks, the whisky gains notes of vanilla and caramel along with a creamy texture. The 25% sherry cask maturation adds fruity and spicy nuances like plums, dates, and a delicate sherry sweetness, making the flavor complex and well-balanced.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Bunnahabhain 12 Year from other Islay whiskies?
faq.answer: Unlike many Islay whiskies, the Bunnahabhain 12 Year is less smoky and peaty, instead showcasing fruity and maritime notes. The combination of fruity aromas, oak, and a light pepperiness makes it especially approachable and versatile.

item.name: Blair Athol 9 Years (2014/2023) - Signatory Vintage
item.description: The 9-years Blair Athol - number 3 in Signatory Vintage's 100 proof series - was distilled in 20214 and bottled in winter 2023 and sold out within a few hours, just like the other two bottlings in the series. And rightly so? At 57.1% ABV, the alcohol content of the bottlings corresponds exactly to the minimum level at which gunpowder soaked in alcohol ignites - or 100 British proof.

tasting_notes.nose: Wow. That's exciting. Cough syrup, eucalyptus and Ricola Swiss herbal sweets are the dominant aromas on the nose, followed by coffee, leather and blackcurrant. If that wasn't enough, we also smell a good portion of liquorice. All extremely intense.
tasting_notes.palate: Clearly 99.9% dark chocolate paired with freshly ground coffee, tobacco (almost ashy) and rubber. The herbal and cough syrupy notes are also present in the flavour. On the fruity side, we taste a few plums. The whole flavour is overwhelming (both positively and negatively), intense, dry and extremely full-bodied.
tasting_notes.finish: The long, smooth finish is characterised by tobacco, pepper, leather and burnt oak.

conclusion.marcel: This Blair Athol is a wild ride through dark flavor realms – intense, almost overloaded, but precisely because of that, exciting. Ideal for enthusiasts willing to embrace a powerful character with notes of chocolate, leather, and robust herbs, but definitely not an easy-going whisky for casual social evenings.
conclusion.sascha: The cask strength of 57.1% delivers an overwhelming presence that structurally dominates the Blair Athol and shifts the balance in favor of intense, bitter notes. A demanding malt with complex aromatics, distinguished less by harmony and more by power and depth – not necessarily accessible, but technically fascinating.

faq.question: What does the designation "100 Proof" mean for this Blair Athol whisky?
faq.answer: "100 Proof" corresponds to an alcohol content of 57.1% ABV. Historically, "Proof" referred to the alcohol level at which gunpowder soaked in alcohol could still ignite. For this whisky, it signals a particularly high drinking strength.

faq.question: How does the age of 9 years influence the taste of this Blair Athol whisky?
faq.answer: At 9 years, the whisky is relatively young, which is reflected in its intensity and freshness. The flavors are powerful and full-bodied, with pronounced notes of herbs, licorice, and dark chocolate, which gain sufficient depth and complexity through maturation in the wooden cask.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Signatory Vintage bottling compared to standard Blair Athol whiskies?
faq.answer: Signatory Vintage is an independent bottler that often releases special cask strengths and limited editions. This bottling at 57.1% ABV is significantly stronger than many standard versions and offers an intense aroma and flavor experience, characterized by the selection of special casks and bottling timings.

item.name: Lochlea 5 Years (2018/2023)
item.description: 5 years and 5 casks. The first single malt whisky with an age statement from the still young Lochlea whisky distillery in the Scottish Lowlands was sold out faster than you could believe. The combination of first fill bourbon, first fill oloroso sherry, double matured Pedro Ximénez sherry and double matured oloroso sherry promises a versatile spirit.

tasting_notes.nose: And it is precisely this promised versatility that we perceive on the nose. First of all, an aroma that inevitably reminds us of roasted almonds and brown sugar. Then we smell rich and full-bodied Hawaiian pizza with a hint of colourful pepper. Exciting!
tasting_notes.palate: Very pleasantly oily and with some subtle oak, we are embraced by an orangy-lemony acidity - not too much and very tasty. The barrel combination also delivers, of course. Dates, overripe pears, cranberries and a good slice of sugar cake with a little caramel sauce.
tasting_notes.finish: In the medium to long finish, we are again presented with colourful pepper, slightly leathery notes and red fruits.

conclusion.marcel: A true delight that instantly lifts the mood with its lively cask combination and playful character. The Lochlea 5 Years shows just how much joy a young single malt can bring – full of sweet and spicy surprises that make you eager for the next sip.
conclusion.sascha: The harmonious balance between robust alcohol and complex cask maturation convinces across the board. Lochlea 5 Years delivers a structured, well-integrated composition where the various sherry cask notes are precise yet never overpowering – a technically clean Lowland whisky.

faq.question: How do the different cask types influence the flavor of the Lochlea 5 Years 2018/2023?
faq.answer: The Lochlea 5 Years matured in five different casks: first-fill bourbon, first-fill Oloroso sherry, double matured Pedro Ximénez sherry, and double matured Oloroso sherry. This combination creates a complex aroma profile with notes of dates, overripe pears, cranberries, and caramel sauce, complemented by spicy and slightly leathery nuances on the finish.

faq.question: What makes the Lochlea 5 Years 2018/2023 special as a Lowlands single malt?
faq.answer: As one of the first age-statement single malts from the young Lochlea distillery in the Lowlands, this whisky stands out for its versatility and the blend of various sherry and bourbon casks. At 50% ABV, it offers an intense yet balanced tasting experience, typical for the region and its cask selection.

faq.question: How should one best enjoy the Lochlea 5 Years 2018/2023 to fully experience its aromas?
faq.answer: The whisky reveals its aromas best at room temperature, neat or with a few drops of water to highlight the orangey-lemony acidity and the complex fruit and spice notes. A calm tasting with some time allows you to discover the layered notes of roasted almonds, brown sugar, and colorful pepper.

item.name: Westheimer Bier-Whisky
item.description: The Wünnenberg Heritage Association takes care of preserving the history of the town of Wünnenberg. In the cellar of the small Fachwerkhaus, which the association calls its home, stands a small copper still. Every now and then, whisky is also distilled there. The nearby Westheimer Brewery supplies a small amount of their beer mash for this purpose. From this, the association produces its own house-made “Bier-Whisky”. Today we are tasting the bottling from 2023. According to the label, it's a genuine Single Barrel Malt with a Bordeaux Wood Finish, bottled at a wonderful 46% - so definitely not overly diluted. In conversation, they reveal to us its age: the whisky has spent 3 years maturing in wooden casks.

tasting_notes.nose: The aroma is fruity but also herbaceous. We detect a herbal note. This harmonizes nicely with the fruity aromas of black cherries, red grapes, and dried plums. There's also a slight maltiness and a hidden hint of jerky.
tasting_notes.palate: The mouthfeel is creamy and spicy right away. Here we still strongly feel the distillery character - typical for such a young whisky. However, the aromas of the nose pleasantly continue on the palate. Herbal with a hint of red wine, rounded off by something unique that reminds us most of sandalwood.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium-long with spicy oak. However, there is no trace of any ethanol sharpness or metallic note here either. Rounded and smooth, there is also a reminiscence of the raw spirit in the mouth later on.

conclusion.marcel: There’s a lot of passion and craftsmanship poured into this – the Westheimer Beer Whisky is charmingly unpolished and delivers genuine drinking pleasure with its fruity and spicy notes.
