Sure, it’s not a groundbreaking masterpiece, but that’s exactly what makes it likable and honest. For a brief detour into the region, it’s definitely a little gem not to be missed.
conclusion.sascha: The Westheimer Beer Whisky shows a solid balance between fruity and spicy aromas, with the youthful maturation clearly noticeable and not fully masking the distillery character. The structure is quite straightforward, with a medium-length, soft finish free of any harshness – a respectable result for a three-year Bordeaux cask finish. Its value for money and regional character make this malt an interesting example of artisanal small-batch production.

faq.question: How does the use of beer mash influence the flavor of the Westheimer Beer Whisky?
faq.answer: The beer mash from the Westheimer Brewery imparts a unique maltiness and fruity notes to the whisky, reflected in aromas like Morello cherries and red grapes. This base creates a harmonious link between the typical whisky elements and its beer origins.

faq.question: What does the Bordeaux Wood Finish mean for the Westheimer Beer Whisky?
faq.answer: The Bordeaux Wood Finish refers to the whisky’s secondary maturation in casks previously used for Bordeaux wine. This imparts additional red wine aromas and a subtle woodiness that enhances the flavor profile with fine spiciness and a hint of sandalwood.

faq.question: How does the relatively short maturation of 3 years affect the whisky?
faq.answer: With only 3 years of maturation, the Westheimer Beer Whisky still shows a pronounced distillery character and a certain freshness. The youthful aromas are lively and spicy, while the finish remains soft without any sharp or metallic notes, which is remarkable for a young single barrel malt.

item.name: Deanston 11 Years (2009/2021) - Cask 116 - Brave New Spirits
item.description: As part of The Rebels The Guerilla Casks series, a Deanston was bottled from a first fill Merlot cask. With this series, the independent bottler Brave New Spritis has set itself the task of offering whiskies from unusual casks, such as wine barriques from Asia, tequila casks from Mexico or casks for sweet wines from islands such as Madeira or Sicily.

image.alt: Deanston 11 Years 2009/2021 - Cask 116 - Brave New Spirits

tasting_notes.nose: The Deanston offers some really exciting flavours in the nosing. The variety of flavours reminds us of nut corners with icing, sour cherry jam and fresh red grapes. There is also a slight vinegar note, which is not unpleasant, but rather provides a little freshness.
tasting_notes.palate: We also encounter nut corners again in the flavour. The entire range of fairground sweets can also be found. Everything from roasted almonds and candied strawberries to mulled wine sweets can be enjoyed. Some candy floss rounds off the oily appearance of the single malt.
tasting_notes.finish: Spicy, intense and warming, the cask strength Deanston has a medium finish with a slight chilli tingle in the back of the throat.

conclusion.marcel: If you're looking for a malt in the cold season that scores with sweet fairground notes and a warming spice, you've found a real winner here. The Deanston First Fill Merlot is simply fun and brings a bit of Christmas market atmosphere to the glass – perfect for cozy evenings of indulgence.
conclusion.sascha: This Deanston shows a remarkable balance between fruity Merlot notes and precisely dosed spice that clearly conveys the cask profile. The structured aromatics with their medium-length, warm finish make it a convincing example of successful maturation in First Fill Merlot casks.

faq.question: How does the First Fill Merlot Cask influence the flavor of the Deanston 11 Years?
faq.answer: The First Fill Merlot Cask imparts a distinctive fruitiness and sweetness to the Deanston, reminiscent of sour cherry jam and red grapes. At the same time, it introduces spicy and slightly bitter notes that make the flavor profile complex and intriguing.

faq.question: What makes the Brave New Spirits “The Rebels The Guerilla Casks” series special?
faq.answer: The series is characterized by the use of unusual cask types, such as Asian wine barriques or tequila barrels from Mexico. This results in unique flavor profiles that clearly set them apart from classic whisky releases.

faq.question: How should the 54.8% ABV alcohol content of the Deanston 11 Years be evaluated?
faq.answer: At 54.8% ABV, this Deanston is a cask strength whisky that offers more intense aromas and a stronger presence on the palate. It is especially suited for connoisseurs who prefer powerful and complex whiskies and can be opened up with a little water if desired.

item.name: Talisker 18 Years
item.description: Talisker is known for harmonious Whiskys. Not too much of anything, no aroma fights its way to the fore. These characterful Whiskys have been distilled on the Isle of Skye on the west coast of Scotland for almost 200 years."Made by the Sea" is the motto under which Talisker is distilled and marketed. The entire marketing strategy is based on this principle. This fits wonderfully, because the maritime notes can also be directly assigned during blind tasting. It doesn't matter whether we have Talisker 8, Talisker 10 or Talisker 18 in the glass. Talisker always has a special signature, a real character - rough seas in a glass. The 18 years takes this signature to a perfectly balanced composition.

tasting_notes.nose: Smoky like vanilla cigarillos, later greeted by slightly mossy oak. Exceptionally balanced and pleasant on the nose. Small salty accents evoke memories of the Baltic Sea beach in the rain. On the fruity side, we find oranges and peaches refined with a hint of marzipan.
tasting_notes.palate: The almost two-decade-old whisky is soft and flattering, with a minimal and subtle smoke that is reminiscent of charcoal in a fire basket. The oranges and peach from the nose are present in the mid-palate with a little rock sugar and subtle sea salt. Interesting chalky notes as well as some earthy aromas and vanilla round off the flavour.
tasting_notes.finish: Sweet and salty, we feel the Talisker on the palate for a long time. Light tannins follow, but sweet vanilla remains at the front of the tongue. The Talisker chilli catch is of course also noticeable, but not as prominent as in other bottlings.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that’s pure enjoyment and instantly captivates with its smoky-salty signature – the Talisker 18 is like a mild evening by the sea, elegant yet wild. For lovers seeking character and drinking pleasure, this is top-tier stuff, even if we leave the ultimate winner open.
conclusion.sascha: The Talisker 18 impresses with its balanced interplay of smoke, fruit, and salty notes, creating a complex yet harmonious structure. A technically superb single malt that clearly makes its mark in the world of premium Scotch whiskies, without quite claiming the throne.

faq.question: How does the 18-year maturation influence the taste of Talisker 18 Years?
faq.answer: The 18-year maturation results in a particularly balanced composition where smoky notes, sweet vanilla, and fruity hints like orange and peach come together harmoniously. The extended aging softens the smoke and brings out subtle salty and earthy nuances.

faq.question: What role does the maritime environment of the Isle of Skye play in the character of Talisker 18 Years?
faq.answer: The distillery’s location on Skye’s west coast imparts Talisker 18 Years with its distinctive maritime notes. Salty accents and a light sea breeze are reflected in the aroma and flavor, giving the whisky its typical ‘Rough Sea in a Glass’ signature.

faq.question: How does Talisker 18 Years differ from younger Talisker expressions like the 10 Years?
faq.answer: Compared to the Talisker 10 Years, the 18-years whisky is softer and more complex, with less dominant smoky notes. It shows more sweet vanilla, fruity aromas, and a more balanced interplay of smoke, salt, and sweetness, which is due to the longer maturation.

item.name: Four Roses Small Batch
item.description: Bourbon is as much a part of Super Bowl Day in the USA as scotch is on Robert Burns Day in Scotland. For today's Super Bowl match day, we have chosen a standard bourbon that can be enjoyed neat or in a cocktail.

tasting_notes.nose: The Four Roses Small Batch opens with a sweet note reminiscent of icing sugar, followed by caramel and maple syrup. Slightly nutty flavours with a splash of citrus join in. On the more savoury side, we find something reminiscent of sourdough and rye.
tasting_notes.palate: The bourbon is (surprisingly) spicy. Here, too, we think of rye. As in the nose, we find a caramel undertone, as well as sweet notes - similar to a honeydew melon. Of course, vanilla is also present in this bourbon! In general, the mouthfeel can be described as creamy, sweet and spicy.
tasting_notes.finish: The oaky, spicy finish is medium to long and has some roasted flavours and burnt corn on the cob.

conclusion.marcel: The Four Roses Small Batch is a pleasant companion for Super Bowl night, without stirring up any strong emotions. It shows character but remains somewhat restrained overall – more of a solid everyday bourbon than a standout. For a bit more excitement in the glass, I’d rather reach for Bulleit or Buffalo Trace next time.
conclusion.sascha: Despite decent spice and a creamy texture, the Four Roses Small Batch lacks the complexity needed to truly impress. The balance between sweetness and rye spice is well done, yet the overall profile feels somewhat linear and predictable. For the price-performance ratio, Bulleit 10 Year or Buffalo Trace offer more structure and depth.

faq.question: What sets the Four Roses Small Batch apart from other bourbons?
faq.answer: The Four Roses Small Batch is distinguished by its complex blend of sweet and spicy notes, particularly the combination of caramel, maple syrup, and rye spice. It delivers a creamy, sweet-spicy mouthfeel and a medium-length, oaky-spicy finish with roasted aromas.

faq.question: How is the Four Roses Small Batch best enjoyed?
faq.answer: This bourbon is suitable both for sipping neat and as a base for cocktails. Its balanced sweetness and spice make it versatile, for example in a classic Old Fashioned or a Bourbon Sour.

faq.question: Why does the Four Roses Small Batch have no age statement on the bottle?
faq.answer: The Four Roses Small Batch is a so-called No Age Statement (NAS) bourbon. This means it is blended from barrels of varying ages to ensure a consistent flavor without specifying a particular age.

item.name: Glenlossie 12 Years (2009/2021) - Malts of Scotland
item.description: Malts of Scotland is an independent bottler from Paderborn in Westphalia, Germany. Known for its high quality bottlings at good prices, almost every whisky is a real treat - is this also true for this Glenlossie 12 years?

tasting_notes.nose: Christmas! Gingerbread, cinnamon and cloves dominate the nosing at first. A hint of honey wine flashes through briefly in places, but is quickly replaced by spices, dried fruit and plums. As if that wasn't enough, cranberries, dried cherries and yes - really - Nutella follow after some time in the glass.
tasting_notes.palate: We continue our visit to the Christmas market with caramel and the sweet, full-bodied flavour of red fruits and a juicy mango. Roasted almonds and star fruit are not to be missed. Cheekily, there is also a little chilli kick hiding in the corner. The alcohol is wonderfully integrated as an intense flavour carrier.
tasting_notes.finish: The Glenlossie remains long and warming with bitter dark chocolate and a little aniseed.

conclusion.marcel: A Christmas whisky that’s a lot of fun and delights with its fruity-sweet surprises like mango and Nutella – the Glenlossie 12 Years from Malts of Scotland brings festive cheer to the glass and makes the cold season feel much warmer.
conclusion.sascha: This bottling impresses with a complex aroma profile featuring a balanced interplay of spices, fruitiness, and a subtle chili kick that keeps the structure exciting; a technically clean Speyside malt with a long, warm finish and excellent cask balance.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 57.3% affect the taste of the Glenlossie 12 Years?
faq.answer: The 57.3% alcohol content ensures that the aromas come through intensely and powerfully. Despite the strength, the alcohol is well integrated and acts more as a carrier of flavors, making the spices, fruits, and sweet notes particularly pronounced.

faq.question: What role does maturation play at Malts of Scotland for this Glenlossie 12 Years?
faq.answer: As an independent bottler, Malts of Scotland selects special casks for finishing to highlight the character of Glenlossie. The 12-year maturation combined with the choice of casks brings out complex aromas such as spices, dried fruits, and sweet notes.

faq.question: What flavor characteristics make this Glenlossie 12 Years a special whisky?
faq.answer: This Glenlossie stands out with a Christmas-like aroma featuring gingerbread, cinnamon, and clove, complemented by fruity notes like plums, cranberries, and mango. A surprising chili kick and the long-lasting finish with dark chocolate and anise give it a unique complexity.

item.name: P&M Red Oak
item.description: Distillation has been a tradition in Corsica for over 150 years, while the Pietra brewery has only been brewing beer since 1995. Pietra and the Mavela distillery have recently entered into a collaboration, releasing their products under the name P&M Single Malt Corsican Whisky. Today, we're exploring the "Red Oak" special edition, featuring spring and winter barley, 5ppm of smoke, a Holstein Pot Still, maturation in ex-Niellucciu (a Corsican grape variety) red wine casks, and a limited run of 567 bottles. Unfortunately, the age of the whisky remains a secret.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose of the Red Oak immediately captivates with a decidedly exotic character. A strong grain note accompanied by pronounced maltiness meets typical aromas of caramel, walnut, and a hint of vanilla. However, it gets particularly interesting – a touch of eccentricity best described as "funky." Our primary associations lead us to mango and lychee, providing an extraordinarily exotic fruitiness.
tasting_notes.palate: The taste of this single malt is wonderfully creamy and smooth. Sweet fruitiness of mirabelle plums joins the more typical apple and pear notes, all while retaining the unique flavors experienced on the nose. Now, a hint of oak also appears on the palate, and with careful exploration, a trace of smoke emerges in the background.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish leaves a short impression, characterized by subtle marzipan notes and a gentle, tingling sensation. A pleasant dryness envelops the palate, accompanied by delicate maritime, slightly salty undertones.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that really delights with its exotic fruitiness and soft creaminess – like a little holiday in Corsica in a glass. The unusual aromas are bold, but that’s exactly what gives it character and drinking pleasure. Whether the price is justified is something each person must decide for themselves, but the P&M Red Oak is definitely worth a Corsican adventure.
conclusion.sascha: The P&M Red Oak impresses with an interesting balance between sweet fruitiness and subtle notes of oak and smoke, although the lack of an age statement leaves a bit to be desired in terms of transparency. The structure is solid, but the short finish and exotic accents feel somewhat unbalanced. Overall, a technically well-crafted single malt with an exciting regional profile that still shows potential for further maturation.

faq.question: Why is the age of the P&M Red Oak whisky not disclosed?
faq.answer: The age of the P&M Red Oak whisky is deliberately kept a secret, which is common among some distilleries and bottlings to focus attention on the flavor and maturation in special casks rather than on age alone.

faq.question: What role do the ex-Niellucciu red wine casks play in the maturation of this whisky?
faq.answer: Maturation in ex-Niellucciu red wine casks imparts the P&M Red Oak with its distinctive fruitiness and complexity by introducing aromas of red berries and a subtle woodiness that supports the flavors of mirabelle plums and exotic fruits.

faq.question: How does the low peat level of 5ppm influence the taste of the whisky?
faq.answer: With a peat level of 5ppm, the P&M Red Oak shows only a delicate smoky note, which manifests as a fine hint on the finish and complements the whisky’s creamy, fruity character without overpowering it.

item.name: Benrinnes 12 Years - 82 Chapters to Newcastle
item.description: Anyone who reads here regularly should already be familiar with the bottlings from 82 Chapters to Newcastle. The Benrinnes 12 years is one of the Creatures of Cask Strength bottlings, but at the same time it is not. Why not? We have no idea. In Asterix and Obelix terms, you'd say: the guys are crazy.

tasting_notes.nose: When nosing, Benrinnes inevitably reminds us of strawberries and those cherry lollipops that every child has eaten. There is also a pleasant sweetness of dark fruit and an unmistakable hint of wine. Not surprising given the 12-month 1st fill port cask finish in a hogshead.
tasting_notes.palate: Significantly more oaky than in the nosing, this single malt at cask strength opens with a creamy flavour. A pleasant, subtle sweetness interweaves with a great fruity flavour. Again with a strong strawberry flavour, the Benrinnes impressively demonstrates what a finish in a good cask can achieve. We also find the wine flavours here again, as well as a note that does not remind us of real strawberries, but rather of those pseudo-strawberry flavours from children's sweets.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the Scotch whisky with the censored squirrel on the label is in fact the least exciting thing about the bottling and can be described in three words: Long, Spicy, Fruity.

conclusion.marcel: Strawberries. Really good. And children's sweets. Also really good. With the Benrinnes 12 years, 82 Chapters once again demonstrates an excellent knack for selecting casks. The alcohol is well integrated and the finish is outstanding. By the way: we "food-paired" the malt with chocolate-covered strawberries after the review tasting - highly recommended!
conclusion.sascha: Strawberries. Really good. And children's sweets. Also really good. With the Benrinnes 12 years, 82 Chapters once again demonstrates an excellent knack for selecting casks. The alcohol is well integrated and the finish is outstanding. By the way: we "food-paired" the malt with chocolate-covered strawberries after the review tasting - highly recommended!

item.name: Benrinnes 15 Years (2008/2023) - Malts of Scotland
item.description: The original bottlings from the Benrinnes distillery can be counted on one hand - there is exactly one and it appears in the Flora & Fauna series. If you still want to enjoy single malts from the distillery, you have to look around at independent bottlers such as Malts of Scotland.

tasting_notes.nose: Nosing the Benrinnes 15 Years is an exciting journey through the sherry hogshead. The maltiness and almost extreme cereal notes form the basis, while fruity nuances of blackberry, redcurrant, cranberry and damson unfold. A hint of dark chocolate and subtle roasted flavours round things off.
tasting_notes.palate: The single malt with malt from Roseisle Maltings presents a strong spiciness and earthy depth on the palate. Notes of tobacco and sweet plums mingle with morello cherries and a kick of colourful pepper. The molasses, reminiscent of honey BBQ, lends the flavour a sweet intensity. We can't help but think a little of an old-man's room - the Speysider has a slight leathery, mouldy quality to it.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of this single malt is tinglingly spicy, short to medium in length and characterised by intense oak. Leathery nuances combine with a hint of chilli chocolate and leave a lasting, multi-faceted impression.

conclusion.marcel: This Benrinnes 15 Years shows just how exciting a sherry cask can be – with bold blackberry and tobacco notes that really make you want to take another sip. A true character whisky that, with its sweet spice and a hint of leather, also reveals a few rough edges. For anyone looking to enjoy something off the beaten path, this is an absolute recommendation.
conclusion.sascha: The Benrinnes 15 Years impresses with its balanced structure between intense sherry cask maturation and complex maltiness. The combination of fruity berry aromas, spicy tobacco notes, and a lasting oak tannin presence creates a harmonious and multifaceted flavor profile. Despite the powerful aromas, the malt remains well balanced, though it shows slight rustic nuances on the finish.

faq.question: Why is the Benrinnes 15 Years 2008/2023 from Malts of Scotland especially appealing to fans of sherry cask aromas?
faq.answer: The Benrinnes 15 Years was matured in a sherry hogshead, which imparts pronounced fruity notes of blackberry, blackcurrant, and plum, along with subtle roasted aromas and a dark chocolate note. This combination makes it particularly interesting for fans of sherry cask-influenced whiskies.

faq.question: How does the malt from Roseisle Maltings influence the flavor of this Benrinnes single malt?
faq.answer: The malt from Roseisle Maltings contributes a robust spiciness and earthy depth that blends with notes of tobacco, plum, and pepper. It provides a complex structure that strongly shapes the character of the whisky.

faq.question: What makes the Malts of Scotland bottling interesting for Benrinnes fans compared to the official Flora & Fauna edition?
faq.answer: Since Benrinnes officially only has one bottling in the Flora & Fauna series, independent bottlers like Malts of Scotland offer the opportunity to explore different cask maturations and vintages. This provides access to more diverse flavor profiles and limited editions.

item.name: Tullibardine 500
item.description: The Tullibardine 500 is based on the Tullibardine Sovereign and undergoes a 12-month finish in a classic sherry cask, which should be familiar to any whisky enthusiast.

tasting_notes.nose: Vanilla and malt open the nose of this Highland whisky. A hint of sherry paired with butterscotch merges with fermented fruit (apple, pear), giving it a slightly acidic flavour.
tasting_notes.palate: The whisky is soft and creamy, almost watery on the tongue. The classic fig and date flavours from the sherry cask would round off the taste well if it weren't for the disturbing ethanol note. Too bad.
tasting_notes.finish: Unfortunately, the 500's finish is short and bitter.

conclusion.marcel: Unfortunately, the Tullibardine 500 comes across as somewhat thin, with the sherry influence remaining rather subtle. Despite a slight improvement in quality compared to the Sovereign, it lacks the character that truly makes drinking it enjoyable – I would have hoped for more depth and warmth here.
conclusion.sascha: From a structural standpoint, the Tullibardine 500 feels unbalanced and weakly defined, with the sherry finish staying superficial. The distracting ethanol note and the short, bitter finish undermine the harmony, so the cask finish only marginally enhances the whisky’s potential.

faq.question: How does the 12-month sherry finish influence the taste of the Tullibardine 500?
faq.answer: The 12-month finish in a classic sherry cask imparts notes of fig, date, and a light sherry sweetness to the whisky. At the same time, aromas of vanilla, malt, and butter toffee emerge, which are typical for the Tullibardine 500.

faq.question: Why does the Tullibardine 500 have no age statement (NAS)?
faq.answer: The Tullibardine 500 is a No Age Statement (NAS) whisky, meaning the bottling does not carry a specific age declaration. The distillery focuses here on the flavor and quality of the maturation in the sherry cask rather than a minimum age.

faq.question: Which aromas should one particularly pay attention to in the Tullibardine 500?
faq.answer: On the nose, vanilla, malt, and a slight acidity from fermented fruits like apple and pear dominate. On the palate, the whisky is soft and creamy with sherry-typical notes of fig and date, although accompanied by a noticeable ethanol note.

item.name: Tullibardine 228
item.description: The Tullibardine 228 is based on the Tullibardine Sovereign and undergoes a 12-month finish in Château de Chassagne Montrachet Red Burgundy barrels (228 litre capacity). The estate, owned by the Picard family, is located in the heart of the UNESCO World Heritage vineyards and is renowned for its excellent wines.

tasting_notes.nose: The single malt opens with raspberries, dark fruits and a little chocolate. This is joined by a little honey, vanilla and cherry. With a little imagination, hazelnuts can also be sensed.
tasting_notes.palate: Orange zest and chocolate, like a good dark chocolate with pieces of orange. The malt from the Highlands also offers hazelnut and raspberries - just like on the nose - and is pleasantly dry, like a good red wine.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the Highlander is medium-long, oaky-spicy and sweetish-winey.

conclusion.marcel: The Tullibardine 228 brings a sweetly fruity playfulness to the glass with its Burgundy finish that simply lifts the spirits. Raspberries, chocolate, and a hint of nuts create drinking pleasure, even though it could show a bit more character for the price.
conclusion.sascha: The 12-month finish in 228-liter Burgundy casks gives the Tullibardine 228 a balanced interplay between fruitiness and oak notes, but it remains somewhat superficial in its structural build. For the asking price, the complexity still has room for improvement.

faq.question: What makes the finish in Château de Chassagne Montrachet Red Burgundy casks special for the Tullibardine 228?
faq.answer: The 12-month finish in 228-liter casks from Château de Chassagne Montrachet imparts the Tullibardine 228 with complex aromas of dark fruits, vanilla, and a subtle oak note typical of high-quality red wine barrels.

faq.question: How does the Tullibardine 228 differ in taste from other Highland whiskies?
faq.answer: The Tullibardine 228 is distinguished by a unique combination of raspberries, hazelnuts, and bittersweet orange chocolate, which, thanks to the red wine cask finish, delivers a pleasant dryness and a sweetly vinous spiciness on the finish.

faq.question: Why does the Tullibardine 228 have no stated age despite having a finish?
faq.answer: The Tullibardine 228 is based on the Tullibardine Sovereign, whose age is not explicitly disclosed. The 12-month finish in red wine casks is listed separately, which is why the whisky is classified as NAS (No Age Statement).

item.name: Tullibardine 225
item.description: The Tullibardine 225 is based on the Tullibardine Sovereign and undergoes a 12-month finish in Sauternes barrels (225 litre capacity). Sauternes is a French wine-growing region known for its noble sweet white wine.

tasting_notes.nose: We perceive distinct malty, cereal flavours in the nosing. Underneath are vanilla notes and something sour, reminiscent of pineapple or lemon squash. Something like clarified butter also tickles the nose.
tasting_notes.palate: The Highlander is buttery soft and creamy in the mouth. Flavours of pineapple and lemon tickle the taste buds, while vanilla and a slightly vinous grape form the core.
tasting_notes.finish: The single malt lingers on the palate for a medium length with a pleasant grapey bitterness.

conclusion.marcel: The Tullibardine 225 brings some pleasant sweet Sauternes finish aromas to the table, but remains somewhat pale and unremarkable. It’s fine for a relaxed drink now and then, but unfortunately, the fireworks of character we were hoping for never materialize.
conclusion.sascha: A well-structured Highland malt that gains additional fruity notes and a subtle grape bitterness from the 12-month Sauternes finish, yet still leaves room for improvement in aroma depth and complexity. The balance between maltiness and sweet accents is solid, though it doesn’t deliver any real highlights.

faq.question: What makes the Sauternes finish in the Tullibardine 225 special?
faq.answer: The 12-month finish in 225-liter Sauternes casks imparts a sweet-fruity note with aromas of pineapple, lemon, and a lightly winey grape character typical of the noble sweet white wine from the French wine region.

faq.question: How does the Tullibardine 225 differ from the standard Tullibardine Sovereign?
faq.answer: The Tullibardine 225 is based on the Sovereign but gains additional fruity and vanilla complexity through the special Sauternes finish, which is less pronounced in the standard Sovereign.

faq.question: Why does the Tullibardine 225 have no age statement (NAS)?
faq.answer: The whisky is a No Age Statement (NAS) because it can come from various vintages and is especially characterized by the finish in Sauternes casks, without specifying a minimum maturation period.

item.name: Tullibardine Sovereign
item.description: Tullibardine Sovereign is the entry into the core range of whiskies from the distillery in the Scottish Highlands. At the same time, it also forms the basis for further core range bottlings, each of which receives a 12-month finish in a different type of cask than the bourbon cask.

tasting_notes.nose: The Sovereign is very faint on the nose. Somewhat oaky and malty, a little vanilla here and there and a hint of pear. Half a spoonful of apricot yoghurt.
tasting_notes.palate: The flavour is also relatively "compact". Also oak and vanilla. Instead of great flavours, we encounter hardly any fruit and more ethanol, which makes it tingling.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is short with a hint of cinnamon and some oaky spice.

conclusion.marcel: The Tullibardine Sovereign remains rather restrained and unfortunately offers little drinking pleasure – a whisky that tastes more like cask than character. It might be nice for Highland beginners, but true highlights are hard to find here. Let’s see if the finishes bring more life to it.
conclusion.sascha: The Sovereign shows a solid but quite flat structure with dominant bourbon cask notes that reveal little depth or complexity. The balance between malt, oak, and ethanol feels unrefined, and the finish is short and unspectacular. A basic bottling that could be interesting mainly as a playground for experimenting with finishes.

faq.question: What distinguishes Tullibardine Sovereign from other whiskies from the distillery?
faq.answer: Tullibardine Sovereign is the entry point into the core range and is matured exclusively in bourbon casks without any additional finishing. Other bottlings from the distillery receive a 12-month finish in various types of casks.

faq.question: Why does Tullibardine Sovereign have no age statement (No Age Statement)?
faq.answer: The Sovereign is offered as a No Age Statement whisky, meaning no minimum maturation period is specified. This allows the distillery to blend different vintages to ensure a consistent flavor profile.

faq.question: What flavor notes should I expect from Tullibardine Sovereign?
faq.answer: The whisky presents a rather restrained nose with notes of oak, malt, vanilla, and a hint of pear. On the palate, oak, vanilla, and a tingling ethanol note dominate, while the finish is short, fading out with cinnamon and oaky spice.

item.name: Tullibardine 8 Years (2015/2023) - Signatory Vintage
item.description: The 8 years Tullibardine - # 2 in Signatory Vintage's 100 proof series - was distilled in 2015, bottled in winter 2023 and sold out within hours, as did the other 2 bottlings in the series. And rightly so? At 57.1% ABV, the alcohol content of the bottlings corresponds exactly to the minimum level at which gunpowder soaked in alcohol ignites - or 100 British proof.

tasting_notes.nose: This single malt whisky from the Scottish Highlands opens with an earthy, crisp flavour, like a real sherry. The typical profile is played out here: Dark, red fruits, cereal maltiness, dark chocolate and a dash of oak spice. Very classic, very intense - the cask has really delivered here. However, the secondary notes of root vegetables such as beetroot and carrots are exciting.
tasting_notes.palate: The colour already shows what is to come. The flavour of the Tullibardine 8 years is also really progressive. We taste dark fruits and a handful of blackberries in an equally earthy, powerful and full-bodied, oily flavour. We also encounter the maltiness from the nosing again here. The fruity-fresh distillery character comes through in some places, but is kept very small by the cask.
tasting_notes.finish: The Highlander has a slightly salty, yet fruity finish over a long period of time.

conclusion.marcel: A genuine sherry tornado with plenty of punch – this Tullibardine really hits hard and is a lot of fun if you enjoy intense, fruity yet earthy aromas. A bargain for fans of powerful Highlanders, it charms with its rugged character but doesn’t hold back.
conclusion.sascha: This bottling impresses with a clear, intense sherry cask influence and a balanced structure of fruit, malt, and spice that doesn’t feel overwhelmed despite the high 57.1% ABV. Still, the whisky’s complexity comes across as somewhat rough around the edges and might seem too dominant for purists with a more sensitive palate.

faq.question: Why does the Tullibardine 8 Years 2015/2023 have such a high alcohol content of 57.1%?
faq.answer: The high alcohol content of 57.1% ABV corresponds to 100 British proof and is typical for the 100 Proof series from Signatory Vintage. It ensures a more intense aromatic profile and reflects the cask strength at which the whisky was bottled.

faq.question: What role does the sherry cask play in the flavor development of this Tullibardine whisky?
faq.answer: The sherry cask imparts the whisky’s earthy and fruity notes, such as dark red fruits, bitter chocolate, and a spicy oak component. It provides the intense color and the classic sherry-typical aromas that shape the tasting experience.

faq.question: How does the Tullibardine 8 Years 2015/2023 differ from other Highland single malts?
faq.answer: This whisky stands out through its combination of robust maltiness, earthy sherry notes, and an unusual secondary note of root vegetables. Additionally, the high alcohol content delivers a full, intense flavor experience with a long-lasting, slightly salty finish.

item.name: GORFH Whiskey Cask 1
item.description: Go Out Ride Find Happiness (GORFH) – that's the name of the three-years whiskey aged in pre-filled 600-liter White Burgundy oak casks at the Distillery in Merdingen. After a triple distillation of the corn-barley-mash and refinement in small, new 20-liter casks, the water of life was bottled and adorned with a label featuring a stag with magnificent antlers drinking from a spring. We had the privilege of tasting the first bottling from the first cask – Bottle number 24 out of a total of 56.

tasting_notes.nose: First and foremost, a highly floral-perfumed scent greets us. The notes evoke rose water, elderflower, and curry herb. Alongside that, there's a substantial dose of vanilla and a hint of caramel. Rounded off with grainy aromas that unequivocally lean towards cereals, not in the direction of bread. Overall, the nose is pleasantly fresh and floral but not herbaceous.
tasting_notes.palate: The floral theme of the nose subsides somewhat. We still find a flowery, but now more citrusy note on the palate. Vanilla and the freshness of the cask's oak join the ensemble. The experience is overall soft and surprisingly rounded for its young age. GORFH leaves a truly delightful grainy spiciness on the palate. The mouthfeel tends towards the creamy side, but the texture is not yet fully matured.
tasting_notes.finish: We will describe the finish in one word: mild. GORFH doesn't linger for long. The subtle spiciness on the palate echoes briefly and then transitions to a dry mouthfeel. Considering its young age, a vast array of flavors shouldn't be anticipated here.

conclusion.marcel: GORFH already shows a lot of character with its floral freshness and delicate vanilla note, but it still feels a bit like a young wild one that has yet to find its full maturity. For a first sip from the stag of Merdingen, however, it’s a truly likeable debut that makes you eager for more.
conclusion.sascha: The structure of GORFH is remarkably round and balanced for such a young whisky, with the fresh Pinot Blanc casks providing an interesting, albeit not yet fully developed, flavor complexity. The mouthfeel remains creamy, but it still lacks depth and lasting presence to fully convince over time.

faq.question: How does maturation in Pinot Blanc oak casks influence the flavor of GORFH Whiskey Cask 1?
faq.answer: Maturation in previously used 600-liter Pinot Blanc oak casks imparts floral and fresh notes such as rosewater and elderflower, along with mild vanilla and caramel tones. The Pinot Blanc casks contribute to a light fruitiness and a subtle oak spiciness.

faq.question: What does the triple distillation mean for this whisky and how does it affect the final product?
faq.answer: Triple distillation ensures an especially pure and soft spirit by removing more unwanted substances. This makes GORFH Whiskey Cask 1 smoother and cleaner in taste, which is particularly advantageous given its young age of four years.

faq.question: Why is the bottling limited to only 56 bottles and how does this affect the quality?
faq.answer: The limitation to 56 bottles comes from the first cask of production, making this bottling particularly exclusive. The small quantity allows for careful selection and control, emphasizing the whisky’s uniqueness and character.

item.name: Blair Athol 11 Years (2009/2021) - The Old Friends
item.description: Scotch Universe is a German independent bottler based in Dülmen. In collaboration with their office in Glasgow, they offer special releases under various brands. The "The Old Friends" series showcases single cask bottlings at cask strength - unfiltered and with no added coloring. The marketing approach revolves around presenting these exceptional malts as gifts for old friends. Today, we have a Blair Athol in the glass, matured for 11 years in a red wine barrique cask.

image.alt: Blair Athol 11 Years 2009/2021 - The Old Friends

tasting_notes.nose: Right from the start, this malt reveals itself as exceptionally robust. Intense notes of red fruits like plums and dark grapes take the center stage. Light walnut aromas and subtly charred notes accompany them. Fresh roasted malt, complemented by a discreet leather note and a touch of pepper, round out the flavor profile.
tasting_notes.palate: The mouthfeel is immediately extremely oily, caressing the palate intensely. As expected, the taste is powerful but less sweet than the nose might suggest. Dried red fruits, a distinctive leather-tobacco note, and pronounced wine aromas characterize this malt. The whole experience is enveloped by intense tannins.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium to long, displaying pronounced oaky spiciness. Reminiscent of mixed peppers, a slight bitterness from the tannins continues to shine.

conclusion.marcel: This Blair Athol 11 Year matured in a Bordeaux cask is like a warming friend on cold days – powerful, robust, and full of character. A dram that’s a joy to drink, truly convincing with its intense fruit notes and smoky leather, even if it occasionally leans a bit too heavily on the tannins.
conclusion.sascha: A well-structured single cask with a clear balance between intense fruit aromas and spicy oak notes. The 54% cask strength supports the oily texture, yet the pronounced tannins and slight bitterness in the finish demand attention and might be polarizing for some palates.

faq.question: How does maturation in the red wine barrique influence the flavor of the Blair Athol 11 Year?
faq.answer: The 11 years of maturation in a red wine barrique impart intense notes of red fruits such as plums and dark grapes, along with pronounced tannins and a distinct oaky spiciness on the finish. This cask choice creates a complex combination of fruity, woody, and slightly bitter flavors.

faq.question: Why does Scotch Universe bottle the Blair Athol 11 Year unfiltered and without added color?
faq.answer: Scotch Universe prioritizes offering the whisky at cask strength, unfiltered, and without added color to preserve the natural aroma and the distinctive characteristics of the single cask unaltered. This ensures an authentic tasting experience that reflects the original maturation and the distillery’s character.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Blair Athol 11 Year compared to other Highland malts?
faq.answer: The Blair Athol 11 Year stands out with its robust, oily texture and the combination of bold red fruit aromas, leather and tobacco notes, as well as its pronounced wine influence. The maturation in red wine barrique and the cask strength bottling deliver an intense and complex flavor profile that sets it apart from many other Highland malts.

item.name: Chivas Regal 12 Years
item.description: The youngest release from Chivas Brothers, the Chivas Regal 12, is an indispensable component of any well-stocked spirits department. Since the early 20th century, this bottling has enjoyed widespread popularity, especially among non-experts who delight in savoring it on special occasions. The primary malt in the blend is notably sourced from the Strathisla Distillery on the Speyside. Today, we take the time to see how it presents itself from our perspective.

tasting_notes.nose: The initial impression of the aroma is somewhat delicate. Typical notes of oak and honey are present, accompanied by a spicy nuance reminiscent of herbal tea. There's nothing bothersome or unexpected, although the nose is not exactly a fireworks display.
tasting_notes.palate: The taste pleasantly surprises, offering more excitement than the nose might suggest. A pronounced honey note is complemented by subtle hints of apples and pears. The blend is not overly sweet but is exceptionally smooth, creamy, and well-balanced. It distinguishes itself with a successful balance between fruity and nutty-spicy flavors.
tasting_notes.finish: In the finish, the blend loses a bit of momentum. It remains medium in length, retaining its creamy-spicy character with pleasant oak tones. Nevertheless, towards the end, it appears somewhat one-dimensional.

conclusion.marcel: Chivas Regal 12 delights with its creamy texture and classic Scotch aromas that make it enjoyable to drink – even if it doesn’t exactly deliver surprises. For those who seek uncomplicated enjoyment, it’s a safe bet, but true adventures in the glass are missing.
conclusion.sascha: This blend impresses with its balanced harmony of honey, fruit, and oak, yet the finish reveals a certain one-dimensionality. For a 12-years standard offering, the structure is solid, but it lacks the complex tension that truly challenges connoisseurs.

faq.question: What role does the Strathisla distillery play in Chivas Regal 12 Year?
faq.answer: The Strathisla distillery provides the main malt for the Chivas Regal 12 Year blend. As one of the oldest distilleries in Speyside, its malts significantly shape the distinctive flavor of this whisky.

faq.question: How does the taste of Chivas Regal 12 Year differ from its aroma?
faq.answer: While the nose reveals delicate notes of oak, honey, and herbal tea, the taste surprises with a more intense honey character, accompanied by fruity hints of apple and pear. The blend feels rounded and creamy on the palate, which isn’t immediately apparent from the aroma.

faq.question: Who is Chivas Regal 12 Year particularly suited for?
faq.answer: Chivas Regal 12 Year is especially popular among whisky beginners and casual drinkers due to its balanced, mild profile. It works well for special occasions and as part of a well-stocked home bar.

item.name: Ben Nevis 10 Years (2013/2024) - Dràm Mòr
item.description: Some time ago, we had the pleasure of tasting the blended malt "Dumbarton Rock" from the Independent Bottler Dràm Mòr. The company's primary focus lies in bottling individual single malt casks. They showcase a seasonal selection of special releases quarterly – of course uncolored and unfiltered. Today, we savor a 10-years Ben Nevis from the Summer Collection 2023, featuring a finish of undisclosed duration in Palo Cortado Sherry Hogsheads.

tasting_notes.nose: The opening on the nose is characterized by intense maltiness. A fascinating blend of sweet scents evokes memories of roasted marshmallows and milk chocolate. Over time, the aroma unfolds into more robust notes. A hint of old leather becomes perceptible, and the chocolate note evolves towards bittersweet. In the background, a subtle notion of blackcurrants emerges. Top notes carry a touch of honey and vanilla.
tasting_notes.palate: Notably, the malt exhibits a pronounced dry mouthfeel. Coupled with its high oiliness, it imparts a distinctive astringency to the whisky. On the palate, it presents a spicy-oaky profile with subtle hints of forest honey on the tip of the tongue. These harmoniously evolve into typical Sherry notes of forest berries and chocolate, where the "Sherry bomb" is present in flavor but not overpowering. Towards the back it reveals an almost mineral note reminiscent of tobacco leaves.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of this Ben Nevis is medium-length and warming, with the image of an old leather sofa consistently present. Here, a persistent honey note remains, while the conclusion is pleasantly spicy, accompanied by zesty white pepper.

conclusion.marcel: This Ben Nevis showcases how uncomplicated drinking pleasure tastes with its pleasant sweetness and subtle sherry finish. It combines malty warmth with a hint of leather and honey, never once becoming boring – a perfect companion for relaxed evenings. Not the most extravagant, but an absolute delight that leaves you wanting more.
conclusion.sascha: The Ben Nevis 2013/2024 impresses with its balanced structure between dry spice, intense sherry notes, and an oily texture that creates a precise harmony. The subtle minerality and medium-length, warming finish with peppery accents underline its complexity, even though the cask strength can occasionally feel a bit dominant. Overall, a technically clean whisky with a clear profile and solid maturation work.

faq.question: What makes the Palo Cortado sherry finish special in the Ben Nevis 2013/2024?
faq.answer: The Palo Cortado sherry finish imparts the whisky with complex aromas of forest berries, chocolate, and a subtle oak note. It delivers a balanced sherry bomb that enriches the malt’s flavor without overpowering it.

faq.question: How do the high oiliness and dry mouthfeel affect the enjoyment of this whisky?
faq.answer: The combination of high oiliness and dry mouthfeel creates a distinctive astringent effect that intensifies the flavor and particularly highlights the spicy, honeyed, and peppery notes.

faq.question: Why is this Ben Nevis whisky bottled unfiltered and without coloring?
faq.answer: Unfiltered and non-colored bottlings preserve the whisky’s natural aromas and original color, providing a more authentic tasting experience and better showcasing the typical characteristics of the cask.

item.name: Old Pulteney 18 Years
item.description: The Pulteney distillery from the Scottish Highlands offers a relatively broad and high-quality core range at acceptable prices. In today's review, we have "The Maritime Malt", matured for 18 years in ex-bourbon casks and Spanish oak casks. Spoiler: A really nice Christmas whisky!

tasting_notes.nose: The 18-years matured single malt whisky is a festive delight with flavours of Christmas biscuits and sweet vanilla, accompanied by a hint of cinnamon and a seductive sugar glaze. The present, tart oak adds depth, while chocolate-coated nuts add an exquisite note. Later, a harmonious blend of mango and peach unfolds.
tasting_notes.palate: The Old Pulteney offers a balanced and multi-faceted experience. Light spicy notes of cloves and chocolate, accompanied by toffee, oak and subtle honey herald the Christmas festivities. The vanilla adds a pleasant complexity. The flavour is peppery-tingling and tangy, the texture oily with a dry accent.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish fulfils the promise of a salty, maritime character with white pepper. A tingling mouthfeel that hints at age leaves a sweet film on the palate.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that enriches the festive season with a warm embrace of vanilla and Christmas cookies – the Old Pulteney 18 Year is pure drinking pleasure with a maritime flair. It continually surprises with sweet and spicy nuances that invite you to sip and savor. A true Christmas hero that never gets boring!
conclusion.sascha: The Old Pulteney 18 Year impresses with its balanced structure and the harmonious interplay of sweet vanilla notes, robust oak, and a subtle salty finish. The balance between maritime elements and complex spice tones makes it a technically solid, if not outstanding, single malt from the Highlands. For lovers of well-composed, medium-bodied whiskies, it’s a recommendable choice.

faq.question: How does maturation in ex-bourbon and Spanish oak casks influence the flavor of the Old Pulteney 18 Year?
faq.answer: The combination of ex-bourbon and Spanish oak casks gives the Old Pulteney 18 Year its complex aroma profile. The bourbon casks contribute sweet vanilla and toffee notes, while the Spanish oak casks add spicy and rich wood tones that provide depth and a harmonious balance to the whisky.

faq.question: What characteristics make the Old Pulteney 18 Year a typical 'Maritime Malt'?
faq.answer: The Old Pulteney 18 Year is distinguished by its salty, maritime notes, which are typical for whiskies from the Pulteney distillery. This maritime influence is especially evident in the finish, with a hint of white pepper and a tingling mouthfeel that evokes the proximity to the sea.

faq.question: How should one best enjoy the Old Pulteney 18 Year to fully experience its aromas?
faq.answer: For an optimal tasting experience, the Old Pulteney 18 Year is best enjoyed neat or with a few drops of water to unlock the complex aromas of Christmas cookies, vanilla, and spices. A room temperature between 18 and 20 degrees Celsius supports the full development of its multifaceted notes.

item.name: Eagle Rare 10 Years
item.description: Buffalo Trace is undoubtedly one of the most respected distilleries in Kentucky, perhaps even in the entire USA. They present different variations of their Kentucky Straight Bourbon under various brand names. Today we're looking at Eagle Rare's 10-years whiskey, which is based on their secret "Mash Bill #1", which is thought to contain less than 10% rye.

tasting_notes.nose: The aroma begins with an intense sweet-fruity flavour. Vanilla, caramel and an extremely pleasant orange note unfold first. This is followed by spicier nuances: oak, dark chocolate and a herbaceous, spicy freshness reminiscent of mint. The scent evokes associations with a wonderfully empty warehouse full of old whiskey barrels.
tasting_notes.palate: The wonderful oiliness impresses on the tongue, and the flavour fulfils the promises of the nose. Sweet nuances of honey, buttered popcorn and milk chocolate unfold, while spicier flavours such as oak, bitter orange and an exotic coconut note send the palate on a journey. A slight tingle and a pinch of cinnamon round off the flavour experience.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish completes the Eagle Rare experience in an equally impressive way. Tart, spicy oak notes dominate here, comparable to a delicious rye bread with butter. The sweetness resonates subtly and is primarily reminiscent of caramel. The whiskey is soft and warming on the palate.

conclusion.marcel: Eagle Rare 10 Year is a true delight, bursting with character and a surprising oiliness that enlivens the drinking experience. For anyone seeking a sweet and spicy bourbon with some edge and complexity, this whiskey is a fantastic companion – and all at a very fair price. Here, drinking pleasure meets quality you can taste!
conclusion.sascha: With a balanced interplay of sweet and bitter notes and a complex aromatic profile, Eagle Rare 10 Year impresses as a technically very clean Kentucky Straight Bourbon. The distinct barrel influence and harmonious viscosity create a structured mouthfeel that appeals to both newcomers and seasoned connoisseurs alike.

faq.question: What sets Eagle Rare 10 Year apart from other Kentucky Straight Bourbons?
faq.answer: Eagle Rare 10 Year is based on Buffalo Trace’s secret "Mash Bill #1," which contains less than 10% rye. This results in a bourbon that is less spicy and instead showcases pronounced sweet-fruity and vanilla notes, combined with a subtle oak spiciness.

faq.question: How should Eagle Rare 10 Year be best enjoyed to experience all its aromas?
faq.answer: The best way to enjoy Eagle Rare 10 Year is neat or with a small drop of water to fully unlock its complex aromas of vanilla, caramel, dark chocolate, and oak. This also brings out its oiliness and long finish to their fullest.

faq.question: Which flavor notes are typical for Eagle Rare 10 Year compared to other Buffalo Trace whiskeys?
faq.answer: Typical are the combination of sweet notes like honey, buttered popcorn, and milk chocolate, alongside spicy aromas of oak wood, bitter orange, and exotic coconut. This is rounded off by a dry-spicy finish with hints of rye bread and caramel, completing the tasting experience.

item.name: Oban Hand Filled Distillery Exclusive 11 Years Batch 1
item.description: When travelling to Scotland and visiting local distilleries, it is almost a must to fill at least one bottle yourself. This is usually offered on site. On our last trip to Scotland, we visited the Oban distillery, among others. As Oban does not have the wide range of core bottlings and is also rarely chosen by independent bottlers, it made sense to fill a bottle there ourselves at cask strength. The question is: Is it actually worth it? We take a closer look at the Oban Hand Filled Distillery Exclusive 11 Years Batch 1.

image.alt: Oban Hand Filled Distillery Exclusive 11 Years Batch 1

tasting_notes.nose: The Oban 11 years in cask strength seduces with fresh vanilla, floral nuances and sweet honey. Hay-like elements and fruity notes of apple and pear harmonise with an exotic coconut note. A splash of lemon frees the nose, while a surprising olive adds depth to the bouquet. Fresh and intense.
tasting_notes.palate: After an inspiring nose, the single malt impresses with its creamy texture, dry intensity and nutty charm. Salty accents in the throat complement the sweet vanilla, honeydew melon and delicate caramel flavour. A hint of white pepper with walnut on the palate completes the flavour experience.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the whisky is surprisingly long and intense, carried by fine fresh oak and a pot of white tea. You don't really get that kind of length and intensity from a pure bourbon cask bottling. The warming note is skilfully accompanied by an astringent character.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that simply delights with its creamy texture and fruity notes – classic Oban through and through. For enthusiasts seeking something special, this cask strength release is definitely worth the journey, even if it just misses that final kick needed for 90+ points.
conclusion.sascha: The excellent balance between sweet vanilla, salty hints, and the unusually long oak finish reflects a well-considered cask selection, though it falls a bit short of expectations in complexity. For connoisseurs who appreciate structure and intensity, this Oban is a solid example of a well-matured bourbon cask.

faq.question: What makes the Oban Hand Filled Distillery Exclusive 11 Years Batch 1 special compared to other Oban releases?
faq.answer: This whisky is bottled on-site at the Oban distillery at cask strength, giving it a more intense aroma and a higher alcohol content of 53.8%. Additionally, at 11 years it is relatively young, but thanks to the cask strength and unique bottling, it presents a complex combination of fruity, nutty, and salty notes that are less common in standard releases.

faq.question: How does the cask strength influence the taste and drinking experience of this Oban whisky?
faq.answer: The cask strength of 53.8% delivers a more intense and concentrated aroma. This allows the fresh vanilla, honey, and fruit notes to stand out more clearly, while the salty and spicy accents become more pronounced. The creamy texture and long, complex finish are enhanced by the higher alcohol level, making the overall tasting experience more vibrant.

faq.question: Is the Oban Hand Filled Distillery Exclusive 11 Years Batch 1 suitable for whisky beginners?
faq.answer: Due to its cask strength and complex aromas, this whisky is better suited for experienced drinkers who appreciate bold and layered whiskies. Beginners might find the alcohol content too intense. Those who still want to try it should dilute the whisky with a little water to better perceive the flavors.

item.name: Glenmorangie The Quinta Ruban 14 Years
item.description: For several years now, a selection of longer-matured releases has enriched Glenmorangie's core range, including the Quinta Ruban. This single malt matures for a minimum of 10 years in former bourbon casks before undergoing additional maturation in port wine casks, elevating its total age to 14 years. It's noteworthy that this whisky is bottled without chill filtration. While the design of Glenmorangie's packaging recently underwent a complete overhaul, we still have a bottle from the older series, the contents of which are purportedly unchanged.

tasting_notes.nose: Upon the initial nosing, the Quinta Ruban reveals itself with the characteristic Glenmorangie aroma, though noticeably more robust. The sweet and tangy fragrance is interwoven with fruity nuances reminiscent of blood orange, grapefruit, and fig. Spicy notes of ground hazelnuts and a hint of dark chocolate also make their presence known.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the Quinta Ruban presents itself in the manner typical of Glenmorangie: silky-smooth and oily. The influences of the port wine casks fully unfold here. The harmonious combination of citrusy sweetness and chocolate brings to mind a soft cake. A slightly herbal note, most reminiscent of mint, joins the symphony. In combination with the chocolate lingering on the palate, this creates a truly wonderful melange.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long and intense. Peppery spiciness, coupled with a generous dose of oak, lingers on the palate. Citrus notes and a subtle hint of baking cocoa or dark chocolate persist in the background.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that simply delights with its fruity-sweet port wine notes and silky texture – perfect for those seeking more intense malts without feeling overwhelmed. Glenmorangie demonstrates here how 14 years of maturation can combine character and drinkability without becoming boring.
conclusion.sascha: The Quinta Ruban impresses with its balanced interplay between fruity sweetness and spicy oak spice, supported by a solid 46% ABV. Its complex structure and long finish make it a successful addition to the Glenmorangie range, although a touch more finesse wouldn’t have gone amiss.

faq.question: How does the finishing in port wine casks influence the flavor of Glenmorangie The Quinta Ruban 14 Years?
faq.answer: The finishing in port wine casks gives Glenmorangie The Quinta Ruban 14 Years its characteristic fruity sweetness with notes of blood orange and fig, along with a velvety chocolate and nutty character that complements and refines the typical bourbon cask aromas.

faq.question: Why is Glenmorangie The Quinta Ruban 14 Years bottled without chill filtration, and what does this mean for the whisky?
faq.answer: Bottling without chill filtration preserves all the natural aromas and oils in the whisky, resulting in a more intense flavor experience and a fuller texture. This can cause the whisky to become slightly cloudy at cooler temperatures, but this does not affect its quality.

faq.question: How does Glenmorangie The Quinta Ruban 14 Years differ from other Glenmorangie core range expressions?
faq.answer: The Quinta Ruban matures longer than the standard expressions and undergoes additional aging in port wine casks, which imparts a more intense fruitiness and spiciness. It is also bottled at 46% ABV and without chill filtration, making it particularly powerful and aromatic.

item.name: Rebels 0,0 Malt Blend
item.description: The Swiss from Freespirited have proclaimed the "Cheers Revolution" with their 0.0% Malt Blend, an initiative for alcohol-free alternatives to popular spirits, ideal for festive occasions when the desire for alcohol is lacking. This cutting-edge concept pursues absolute individual freedom in beverage selection. The noble aim of creating an alcohol-free option for "whisky-based long drinks" is intriguing, even though it's not a whisky but rather an innovative alternative with malt distillate.

tasting_notes.nose: The initial impression through the scent is overwhelming, though not necessarily in a pleasant way. A bold mix of perfumed cherries and artificial fruit caramel hits you. The aroma is more reminiscent of an artificial cocktail than a subtle spirit. A hint of spiced wine based on red fruits influences the senses, accompanied by distinct notes of cinnamon, orange, and a subtle hint of cloves.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the immediate lack of viscosity is noticeable. Without any texture, it clearly feels like water. Here, the botanicals dominate—primarily orange and clove. There's an idea of caramel and a slight tingling spiciness on the palate.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is minimal – what remains is a distinctly ginger-based sharpness.

conclusion.marcel: If you're looking for an alcohol-free whisky experience, you'll be disappointed here – Rebels 0.0 Malt Blend feels more like a sweet cocktail substitute without any soul. Neat, the drink comes across as artificial and hollow, and the high price doesn’t help. For me, it remains more of a gimmick than genuine drinking pleasure.
conclusion.sascha: The structure of Rebels 0.0 Malt Blend is completely lacking; the balance between aroma and flavor feels uneven and artificial. Especially the absence of viscosity and the dominant, synthetic fruit note prevent it from being an authentic whisky alternative. The price-performance ratio and marketing claims bear no meaningful relation to the sensory experience.

faq.question: How does Rebels 0.0 Malt Blend differ from conventional whisky?
faq.answer: Rebels 0.0 Malt Blend contains no alcohol and is based on a malt distillate without maturation, which means it lacks the typical whisky viscosity and depth. It is designed as an alcohol-free alternative, offering a flavor experience with botanical notes instead of classic whisky aromas.

faq.question: Who is Rebels 0.0 Malt Blend particularly suited for?
faq.answer: This product is ideal for people who want to avoid alcohol but still enjoy the taste and ritual of a whisky-based long drink. It is especially suitable for festive occasions where alcohol-free options are in demand.

faq.question: Which aromas define the taste of Rebels 0.0 Malt Blend despite the absence of alcohol?
faq.answer: The flavor is dominated by botanical notes such as orange, clove, and ginger, complemented by a caramel sweetness and a slight spiciness on the palate. The texture is watery, and the finish is short with a ginger-based sharpness.

item.name: Gauldrons Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing
item.description: The Gauldrons is bottled by independent bottler Douglas Laing and promises a journey through the Campbeltown region of Scotland. We take a closer look at the blend today.

image.alt: Gauldrons Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing

tasting_notes.nose: The Gauldrons unfolds a tantalising symphony of fragrances: sweet vanilla and malty warmth combine with fresh, grassy aromas. Spicy nuances dance harmoniously with hints of brown sugar, creamy caramel and the invigorating freshness of pear peel. An aromatic work of art.
tasting_notes.palate: The blend unfolds on the palate in a spicy, pithy feast: peppery intensity, strong oak notes and a pinch of salted caramel form a robust base. The flavour of roasted peanuts combines elegantly with a hint of light forest honey. A powerful pleasure.
tasting_notes.finish: The whisky closes with a sweet, medium-length finish. The flavour of burnt sugar lingers gently and leaves a delicious aftertaste.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that’s a joy to drink and brings Campbeltown to life with its spicy and sweet diversity. Gauldrons Small Batch isn’t a complicated thinker, but an honest companion for enjoyable evenings – with a touch of humor and plenty of character.
conclusion.sascha: The Gauldrons Small Batch Release impresses with a balanced aromatic profile and a solid structure that captures Campbeltown’s typical maritime note well. Despite its youthful character, it shows a successful balance between spice, sweetness, and a light saltiness that rounds off the blend nicely.

faq.question: What makes the Gauldrons Small Batch Release from Campbeltown special compared to other blends?
faq.answer: The Gauldrons Small Batch Release combines typical Campbeltown characteristics such as spicy oak and salty tones with a balanced sweetness of vanilla, caramel, and fresh fruit notes. This balance between spice, sweetness, and maritime influences sets it apart from other blends.

faq.question: How should one best enjoy the Gauldrons to fully appreciate all its aromas?
faq.answer: The best way to enjoy Gauldrons is slightly chilled or at room temperature in a tulip-shaped whisky glass. A few drops of water can help release the intense aromas of pepper, caramel, and pear skin more effectively.

faq.question: Why does the Gauldrons Small Batch Release have no age statement, and does that affect the taste?
faq.answer: Gauldrons is a no age statement (NAS) blend, meaning it combines whiskies of different ages. This allows the bottler to craft the flavor profile deliberately without being tied to a specific age, often resulting in a balanced and complex character.

item.name: Edinburgh Castle 10 Years
item.description: High above Castle Rock, amidst the Scottish capital, majestically stands Edinburgh Castle. This historic landmark is cared for and preserved by Historic Environment Scotland (HES). It is there that Edinburgh Castle 10 was created, a single malt whisky paying homage to Scotland's most significant attraction. Today, we take the time to explore its taste.

tasting_notes.nose: Upon the first sip, this whisky unfolds with a robust, earthy note. We are in disagreement, speculating if a hint of smoke might be lingering in the glass. Given a little time, it opens up and reveals nuances of vanilla, honey, and buttery biscuits, accompanied by a slight bitterness of orange peel.
tasting_notes.palate: On the tip of the tongue, Edinburgh Castle 10 initially delivers an intensely fruity sweetness, evoking thoughts of red fruits, particularly creamy candies. The oily mouthfeel carries the flavors of vanilla, cereal, and honey, all while maintaining its intense earthiness and hints of bitter orange — again perhaps even a touch of smoke?
tasting_notes.finish: In the medium-length finish, earthy notes return, accompanied by a hint of oak wood and a sweet-spicy aftertaste, completing the experience.

conclusion.marcel: Edinburgh Castle 10 is a true character whisky that pleasantly surprises with its earthy depth and sweet vanilla notes. Quite unusual for a Highlander, but that's exactly what makes it so enjoyable to drink – a whisky that never bores and leaves you wanting more.
conclusion.sascha: The Edinburgh Castle 10 strikes a successful balance between earthy and fruity aromas, with its moderate strength providing a subtle yet complex structure. Despite minor blurriness in the smoke and wood notes, it convinces as a harmoniously composed Highland single malt.

faq.question: What makes the Edinburgh Castle 10 years special compared to other Highland whiskies?
faq.answer: The Edinburgh Castle 10 years is distinguished by its earthy and lightly smoky notes, accompanied by sweet aromas such as vanilla, honey, and orange peel. This combination gives it a balanced complexity despite its 10 years of maturation, typical of the Highlands but with its own distinctive character.

faq.question: How should one best enjoy the Edinburgh Castle 10 years to fully appreciate its aromas?
faq.answer: It is recommended to drink the whisky at room temperature and let it breathe briefly in the glass before tasting, so the complex aromas of vanilla, honey, and earthy notes can fully develop. A few drops of water can also help to highlight the fruity and smoky nuances.

faq.question: Are there any clues about the distillery or the independent bottler of the Edinburgh Castle 10 years?
faq.answer: The Edinburgh Castle 10 years is offered as an independent bottling, but neither the exact distillery nor the bottler is officially disclosed. This is common with many independent bottlings, aiming to focus attention on the taste and uniqueness of the whisky.

item.name: Glen Moray 15 Years
item.description: The Glen Moray distillery is located in the vicinity of Elgin in the Speyside region of Scotland. In recent years, they have steadily expanded their production capacity. Today, Glen Moray offers an impressive variety of releases under their own name, with different age statements and cask finishes, all at highly competitive prices. In this review, we delve into the world of the 15-years expression from the Elgin Heritage Collection.

tasting_notes.nose: Right from the first whiff, a powerful and harmonious aroma unfolds. The nose detects a caramel note beautifully accompanied by spicy oak, delicate wine notes, and sweet sherry fruits. It evokes memories of chocolate-covered raisins and a delicious bar of milk chocolate.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, flavors of chocolate and raisins immediately come to life, accompanied by toasty notes and a delightful, spicy-salty fullness. The intense oak, considering its 15-year age, is surprisingly present without leaving any bitterness. A hint of dryness on the palate rounds off the tasting experience. Overall, the whisky on the palate presents itself as less fruity than the nose suggested, revealing maltiness, hints of baking cocoa, and a touch of overripe mango.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish, especially for a non-peated whisky, proves to be long. It leaves a wonderful spiciness with a harmonious blend of chocolate, oak, and malt, accompanied by a subtle tingle.

conclusion.marcel: The Glen Moray 15 Year simply brings joy to the glass – sweet, spicy, and with a warm dose of character that feels just right without overwhelming. For enthusiasts seeking uncomplicated enjoyment without sacrificing depth, this is a clear winner at a fair price. Perfect for leaving a solid impression on friends without boasting about complexity.
conclusion.sascha: A well-balanced Speyside malt that impresses with its harmonious structure and pronounced oak spice, without coming across as overly complex. The moderate fruitiness gives way to a balanced maltiness and a long, spicy finish, making the Glen Moray 15 a solid representative of its category. From a technical standpoint, it’s a whisky that combines versatility and approachability at an attractive price.

faq.question: How does the 15-year age influence the taste of Glen Moray 15 Year?
faq.answer: The 15-year age imparts an intense oak note that gives the whisky depth and complexity without bitterness. At the same time, harmonious aromas of chocolate, raisins, and malt develop, rounding out the tasting experience.

faq.question: What type of casks are used for maturing Glen Moray 15 Year?
faq.answer: Glen Moray primarily uses traditional oak casks, including some with a sherry finish, which explains the whisky’s sweet sherry fruit and spicy oak notes.

faq.question: How does Glen Moray 15 Year differ from younger releases from the distillery?
faq.answer: Compared to younger releases, the 15-year Glen Moray shows a noticeably stronger oak spiciness and more complex aromas, including notes of milk chocolate, chocolate-covered raisins, and a longer-lasting spice on the finish.

item.name: Dumbarton Rock Blended Whisky
item.description: The independent bottler Dràm Mòr, run by Kenny and Viktorija Macdonald, is mainly known for its unique single cask bottlings. They regularly release not only single malts, but also rum and armagnac. Starting this year, they additionally offer the blended malt Dumbarton Rock. Today we take the time to taste this blend, which consists of five different single malts.

tasting_notes.nose: This blend beguiles the nose with malty pear and a hint of chocolate, so rich that even the bottle cap becomes envious. A spicy foamy sugar dream, like marshmallows with honey tickle the senses.
tasting_notes.palate: The Dumbarton Rock doesn't quite know where it wants to be yet. It tastes of grain is oily, like a biscuit with a hit of vanilla honey. The oak gives it a subtle seriousness while it dabbles with a pinch of citrus and a hint of sultanas or figs, sweetly fruity but not too berry. Taste that shows that youth can sometimes be sweet and indecisive!
tasting_notes.finish: The malt says goodbye with ginger spiciness and lemon acidity in staccato. A finish like when you steal the last piece of shortbread. Thanks to the oak, the finish is short but with a pinch of elegance - a farewell that calls for an encore.

conclusion.marcel: Dumbarton Rock is a real crowd-pleaser among blends – lively, malty, and with a cheeky ginger spice that leaves you wanting more. It’s a whisky that pleasantly surprises with its untamed youthfulness and sweet vanilla notes, even though it could still gain a bit more clarity.
conclusion.sascha: Dumbarton Rock impresses with its balanced interplay between sweet fruity notes and subtle oak, yet its development still feels slightly uncertain. A longer maturation in casks could give the blend the necessary depth and structure to harmoniously unite its complex components.

faq.question: What makes Dumbarton Rock Blended Whisky by Dràm Mòr special compared to other blended malts?
faq.answer: Dumbarton Rock is composed of five different single malts carefully combined into a complex blend. It comes from an independent bottler known for their unique single cask releases, which gives the whisky a distinctive individuality.

faq.question: How does the lack of an age statement affect the taste and quality of Dumbarton Rock Blended Whisky?
faq.answer: Without an age statement, Dumbarton Rock displays a youthful freshness with sweet and fruity notes like pear, honey, and citrus. The oak adds a subtle seriousness, while the finish surprises with ginger spice and lemon acidity. Here, quality depends more on the selection of single malts than on age.

faq.question: In which region of Scotland is Dumbarton Rock Blended Whisky produced, and how does this influence the character of the whisky?
faq.answer: Dumbarton Rock originates from the Scottish Highlands, a region known for its diverse and often malty whiskies. This origin contributes to the malty and fruity aromas that define the blend, as well as a balanced oak note on the finish.

item.name: Redbreast Tawny Port
item.description: We've had Irish whiskeys with the iconic robin logo in our glasses time and time again in the past. And the Redbreast releases from the Middleton distillery have consistently won us over. As part of the Iberian Series, we now have the Redbreast Tawny Port, following a well-established recipe. This Single Pot Still Irish Whiskey aged in Bourbon and Oloroso casks, followed by a final 14-25 month finishing period in Tawny Port Hogsheads. The end product, however, is bottled without specifying its age.

tasting_notes.nose: On smelling the Redbreast Tawny Port, a mild symphony of cereal aromas unfolds, accompanied by a hint of plastic cherry lollipops that evoke sweet memories. A harmonious mix of caramel, vanilla and oak adds a complex depth. The freshness and softness add a gentle, refreshing dimension to the bouquet. A balanced whiskey that appeals to the senses with its mildness and multi-layered sweetness.
tasting_notes.palate: When tasting the whiskey, one first experiences a tingling caramel, followed by a subtle fruitiness that refreshes the palate. The malt gives it a pleasant depth, while the honey adds a sweet, enticing note. The texture is creamy and adds a velvety smoothness to the enjoyment. Overall, this Redbreast presents itself light yet full of flavour nuances.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the Single Pot Still Irish Whiskey is medium long and leaves a subtle but bitter-rich finish on the palate.

conclusion.marcel: Despite its well-known Redbreast character, the Tawny Port Finish brings a fruity freshness that’s genuinely enjoyable – even if the whisky doesn’t feel quite as elegant as the older expressions. For those who appreciate straightforward drinking pleasure with a touch of sweetness, it’s a solid companion, though not entirely worth every penny.
conclusion.sascha: The Redbreast Tawny Port demonstrates a solid balance between malt, caramel, and port wine-induced fruitiness, yet it falls short in complexity compared to the 12- and 15-years releases. The structure feels somewhat less delicate, which is a critical point given the higher price, but the creamy texture and harmonious finish still impress.

faq.question: What makes the Redbreast Tawny Port special compared to other Redbreast expressions?
faq.answer: The Redbreast Tawny Port undergoes an additional maturation of 14 to 25 months in Tawny Port hogsheads after aging in bourbon and Oloroso sherry casks. This combination imparts a unique fruity sweetness and complexity that sets it apart from the classic Redbreast variants.

faq.question: Why is there no exact age statement on the Redbreast Tawny Port?
faq.answer: The Redbreast Tawny Port is bottled without an age statement because the maturation periods in the different cask types vary, and the final product is a blend of various vintages to ensure a consistent and balanced flavor profile.

faq.question: How does maturation in Tawny Port casks influence the whiskey’s flavor?
faq.answer: Maturation in Tawny Port hogsheads introduces fruity and sweet notes such as caramel, vanilla, and a subtle fruitiness to the whiskey. At the same time, it provides a velvety texture and a harmonious balance between sweetness and a gentle bitterness on the finish.

item.name: Jameson Black Barrel
item.description: The Midleton Distillery markets its most popular product under the name Jameson, a blended Irish whiskey that enjoys great popularity due to its quality and high demand. This unique whiskey is created by skillfully marrying a Single Pot Still whiskey (which also serves as the foundation for Redbreast whiskeys) and a Single Grain Irish whiskey. Over the years, Jameson has continually adjusted and expanded its range. Today, let's take a look at Jameson Black Barrel.
