The distinct difference from classic Jameson lies in its maturation in double-charred barrels.

tasting_notes.nose: At first, this Irish whiskey presents itself as expected - smooth and grainy. Sweet notes of brown sugar and a hint of chocolate emerge. These are joined by spicy, lightly toasted toast, a touch of peanuts, and delicate wild honey. A subtle, hard-to-define fruity sweetness rounds out the flavor profile.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, Jameson Black Barrel also reveals its buttery side. There is no unpleasant sharpness, despite its likely low age. Primary flavors are sweet notes of toast, vanilla, caramel, and brown sugar. A subtle, almost tropical fruit note is also to be discovered. Overall, the whiskey resembles a "Lippische Strohsemmel" (a regional speciality in our area) with maple syrup.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is short and flat. It starts with gentle oak notes, complemented by slight hints of bitter almonds for a subtle bitterness. Finally, it ends with minimal coffee bean accents that add a touch of coffee to the finish.

conclusion.marcel: The Jameson Black Barrel is a pleasant companion for cozy evenings, scoring points with its mild sweetness and smooth character. While it lacks that final kick of depth and complexity, it’s a charming dram especially suited for beginners or fans of lighter whiskeys. An uncomplicated, malty Irish whiskey that’s enjoyable without demanding too much.
conclusion.sascha: With the Jameson Black Barrel, Midleton delivers a well-balanced Irish whiskey that introduces finely spiced toast and vanilla notes thanks to its double-charred barrels. Although the structure is clean and the flavors harmonious, the finish feels somewhat short and lacks intensity. Overall, it’s a technically solid but not very complex blend, distinguished primarily by its approachability.

faq.question: What makes the double-charred barrels in Jameson Black Barrel special?
faq.answer: The double-charred barrels impart more intense roasted aromas and a deeper vanilla and caramel note compared to classic Jameson. The second charring activates the wood more strongly, giving the whiskey added complexity and spiciness.

faq.question: How does Jameson Black Barrel differ in taste from classic Jameson?
faq.answer: Jameson Black Barrel presents a bolder flavor profile with pronounced notes of toasted bread, brown sugar, and caramel. While classic Jameson is softer and milder, the Black Barrel’s barrel treatment adds more spice, subtle bitter almond and coffee notes, as well as a delicate tropical fruit sweetness.

faq.question: Why does Jameson Black Barrel have no age statement (No Age Statement)?
faq.answer: Jameson Black Barrel is a blended Irish whiskey combining whiskeys of different ages. The distillery focuses on achieving a consistent flavor profile through careful marrying and barrel selection, which is why it forgoes an age statement.

item.name: Tormore 14 Years - 82 Chapters to Newcastle
item.description: The team at 82 Chapters to Newcastle introduced a series of three different Tormore Triple Wood Malts to the market last week. This gives us an opportunity to taste their previous Tormore bottling today. This bottling, lightly peated and aged for 14 years in Bourbon casks, is presented in rich cask strength as part of the Creatures of Cask Strength range.

tasting_notes.nose: Right from the first sniff, a distinct hint of cold smoke emerges. Behind it are the classic Speyside aromas: forest honey and a basket filled with autumnal fruits, including pears and apples. The intense aroma of light fruits is framed by vanilla and nearly-dried grass, akin to "almost-hay."
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, this malt makes a strong impression. Surprisingly intense campfire smoke, accompanied by the fruity notes of the familiar fruit basket, with a clear inclination toward dried apples. We also detect a hint of white pepper and a subtle nuttiness. Overall, it offers a beautifully creamy mouthfeel.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is even more pronounced with nutty characteristics, medium-length, and a slight smokiness, providing a delightful conclusion.

conclusion.marcel: 82NC presents here a truly wonderful, straightforward, and classic Speyside Single Malt. The fruity and fresh profile is exactly what we love in Bourbon cask maturation. The experience is rounded out by a pleasant, yet not overpowering, smokiness. Carried by a robust 61.6%, this is truly a work of art.
conclusion.sascha: 82NC presents here a truly wonderful, straightforward, and classic Speyside Single Malt. The fruity and fresh profile is exactly what we love in Bourbon cask maturation. The experience is rounded out by a pleasant, yet not overpowering, smokiness. Carried by a robust 61.6%, this is truly a work of art.

item.name: Buffalo Trace (45%)
item.description: In our previous report, we already delved into Buffalo Trace's entry-level offering in their portfolio. Without age statement and still extremely popular in the USA, little has changed in the characteristics mentioned since then. However, this time we have the "90 Proof" (45% alcohol by volume) version of Kentucky Straight Bourbon in front of us, which is always bottled at this strength in its country of origin.

tasting_notes.nose: Here, we encounter familiar aromas: fundamental bourbon notes of intense, buttery caramel and beautiful vanilla. In addition, there are nuances of toasted oak. The slight solvent component that was noticeable in the 40% ABV version takes a back seat here. Instead, spice notes, and especially mint, are more prominent.
tasting_notes.palate: The difference between 40% and 45% alcohol content is truly noticeable on the palate. The bourbon remains pleasantly smooth, and sweet flavors come through much more strongly, accompanied by a truly lovely oak note.
tasting_notes.finish: The expected trend continues in the finish as well. There is a presence of wood, candied popcorn, and a hint of rye and mint.

conclusion.marcel: Buffalo Trace at 45% comes across as noticeably livelier and sweeter than its 40% counterpart – this bourbon simply brings drinking pleasure without being overly complex. For a classic in this price range, it hits exactly the flavor profile you expect from a solid Kentucky Straight. Not an adventure, but a reliable companion for relaxed evenings.
conclusion.sascha: At 45% ABV, Buffalo Trace presents itself as balanced and structured, with the sweet bourbon notes and subtle oak harmonizing well. The higher alcohol strength eliminates the flat spots found in the 40% version, though the overall complexity remains moderate. A solid representative of the Kentucky Straight Bourbon category, especially impressive for its value for money.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Buffalo Trace 45% from the 40% version in terms of taste?
faq.answer: The higher alcohol content of 45% makes the bourbon taste more intense and sweeter. The vanilla and caramel notes stand out more clearly, while the slight solvent note present in the 40% version is less noticeable. Additionally, spice and mint notes are more pronounced.

faq.question: Why does Buffalo Trace Bourbon have no age statement?
faq.answer: Buffalo Trace bottles this bourbon as a No-Age-Statement (NAS), meaning no minimum age is indicated on the bottle. This allows the distillery to blend different barrel maturations to ensure a consistent flavor profile.

faq.question: What role does the Kentucky origin play for Buffalo Trace Bourbon?
faq.answer: Kentucky is the traditional heart of bourbon production in the USA. The local climate and warehouse conditions promote maturation in oak barrels, which develop characteristic aromas like vanilla, caramel, and oak in Buffalo Trace Bourbon.

item.name: Buffalo Trace (40%)
item.description: Buffalo Trace is one of the epitomes of American whiskey culture. The Kentucky distillery bottles many different bourbons and ryes under a variety of brands. The baseline is the Kentucky Straight Bourbon. Bourbon without an age statement is bottled here at a very competitive price. This always leads to discussions about the age of the whiskey that is bottled. It is very popular in the USA and there are notorious shortages.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose is very classic Bourbon. Strong caramel, vanilla and oak. Sweet popcorn and brown butter also come to mind. There is also the sometimes unpopular slight solvent note. If you leave the bourbon in the glass a little longer, you will also find more exotic notes of rye spice and a hint of mint.
tasting_notes.palate: The layman would describe the taste as 'smooth'. Some caramel and brown sugar shine through, plus a hint of charred oak. Overall, however, the bourbon is a little thin on the tongue.
tasting_notes.finish: Here it continues as expected. On the finish, the wood, brown sugar, mint and slight rye spice remain.

conclusion.marcel: Buffalo Trace is a bourbon that’s easy to drink and entertains well with its classic profile. Sure, it lacks that certain something, but especially for the price, it delivers a solid bourbon experience – perfect for relaxed evenings or as a mixer. A bit more punch in terms of alcohol strength would be desirable, though.
conclusion.sascha: Buffalo Trace presents itself with a solid, if somewhat thin, body and shows classic bourbon notes of caramel, vanilla, and oak, yet remains limited in complexity. The balance is rock solid, but one misses a more distinctive structure and intensity, especially at just 40% ABV. Interesting for beginners, for connoisseurs more of a reliable everyday whisky.

faq.question: Why does Buffalo Trace have no age statement on the bottle?
faq.answer: Buffalo Trace is bottled as a no age statement (NAS) bourbon because the distillery blends barrels with different maturation times. This allows them to maintain consistent quality without committing to a specific age.

faq.question: What explains the slight solvent note on the nose of Buffalo Trace?
faq.answer: The slight solvent note often comes from the combination of younger barrels and the yeast strain used during fermentation. This note is typical for many bourbons in their early stages and usually dissipates after some air contact in the glass.

faq.question: How does Buffalo Trace differ in taste from other Kentucky Straight Bourbons?
faq.answer: Buffalo Trace shows classic bourbon notes like caramel, vanilla, and oak, complemented by a subtle rye spiciness and hints of mint. Compared to other bourbons, it is lighter and somewhat thin on the palate, making it approachable and less intense.

item.name: Sea Shepherd
item.description: Sea Shepherd is a globally active environmental conservation organization, dedicated passionately to safeguarding the world's oceans and their inhabitants. In collaboration with Sea Shepherd, Kirsch introduces an Islay Single Malt prominently featuring the organization's emblem on its label. From a technical standpoint, Kirsch acts as an independent bottler and keeps the secret of the distillery of origin for this whisky. While Sea Shepherd is not actively involved in the production of this spirit, 10% of the proceeds from each bottle sold are donated to support their efforts.

tasting_notes.nose: What we experience here is precisely what the branding promises: a maritime and smoky Islay Malt. Upon the first sip, the cold, iodine-rich smoke leans towards the medicinal side. It is accompanied by a lovely salty sea breeze. Within this harmonious composition, we discover sweet hints of vanilla, licorice, and the fruitiness of apples and pears. This is complemented by a crisp citrus note.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, this whisky requires some time and perhaps even a second sip to fully showcase its array of flavors. Initially, the palate must acclimate to the cool campfire smoke. Once achieved, the malt unfurls with fruity-sweet apple notes and tingling limes on the tip of the tongue. A delightful malty sweetness envelops the palate, accompanied by a hint of blueberries in the back of the throat. All of this is cloaked in the almost peat bog-like peat smoke.
tasting_notes.finish: Characteristic of a smoky Single Malt, the taste lingers in the mouth for quite some time. At this point, the cool smoke and oak are quite pronounced. Hints of the fruity sweetness linger gently.

conclusion.marcel: Sea Shepherd impresses with its smoky character and the salty sea breeze that make the drinking experience exciting and lively. For those looking to get started with intensely smoky Islay malts, this is a friendly companion with a pleasant sweetness – a whisky that’s fun to drink and tells a good story.
conclusion.sascha: The Sea Shepherd presents itself as a well-balanced Islay malt with a clearly defined maritime-smoky profile and an appealing balance between sweet fruit notes and cool peat smoke. Despite its complexity, the whisky feels somewhat reserved and needs time to fully reveal its aromatic spectrum – ideal for connoisseurs who value technical precision and harmonious composition.

faq.question: Why is the distillery of the Sea Shepherd whisky not known?
faq.answer: Sea Shepherd is released by Kirsch Import as an independent bottler who does not disclose the original distillery. This is common practice among independent bottlings to keep the source a secret.

faq.question: How is the maritime theme reflected in the aromas of the Sea Shepherd whisky?
faq.answer: The whisky exhibits distinct maritime notes such as iodine-rich smoke, a salty sea breeze, and cool campfire smoke, which are typical of Islay malts and tastefully underscore the theme of the world’s oceans.

faq.question: What impact does purchasing the Sea Shepherd whisky have on environmental protection?
faq.answer: 10% of the sales revenue from each bottle goes directly to the environmental organization Sea Shepherd, which is dedicated to protecting the oceans and their inhabitants.

item.name: Talisker Surge
item.description: In 2022 Talisker added a travel exclusive called Surge to its core range of bottlings. This malt is only available at airport duty free shops. Let's see how this Talisker, matured exclusively in ex-bourbon casks with no age statement, fares.

tasting_notes.nose: At first, we are greeted by a pleasant smoke. However, there is a sweet note that reminds us of roasted pine nuts. All in all, the peat smoke for which Talisker is renowned is much softer than in other bottlings. There is also clear vanilla, rounded off by the sweet and strong smell of freshly toasted crème brûlée.
tasting_notes.palate: The taste is mildly sweet at first, like a breakfast bun with butter. Behind this are fruity aromas of apple, pear and a little honey. The aroma quickly fades and gives way to a less complex maltiness.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish has the famous Talisker pepper note and a slight maritime saltiness. We feel transported to a campfire on a rocky coastal beach. Compared to the Talisker bottlings we know, however, it is rather dull and not particularly long-lasting.

conclusion.marcel: The Talisker Surge surprises with its gentle sweetness and soft smoke, but overall it feels somewhat muted and lacks the distinctive character one expects from Talisker. For an airport exclusive, the price is hard to justify, even though the peppery note in the finish does evoke a pleasant campfire vibe.
conclusion.sascha: The Surge presents a clear structure with pronounced vanilla and fruity notes, yet it falls short in complexity and length of finish. Compared to classic Talisker standards, it comes across as unbalanced and lacking depth, which hardly justifies the high price for a travel exclusive.

faq.question: Why is the Talisker Surge only available at airports?
faq.answer: The Talisker Surge is a travel exclusive bottling produced specifically for the duty-free market at airports. This distribution strategy aims to offer travelers a special product that is not available through regular retail channels.

faq.question: How does the Talisker Surge differ in taste from other Talisker expressions?
faq.answer: The Surge features a milder and sweeter smoky character than typical Talisker whiskies. It combines sweet notes like vanilla and crème brûlée with fruity aromas of apple and pear, while the finish carries the familiar peppery spice and maritime saltiness, though it is less lingering.

faq.question: What type of cask was used for maturing the Talisker Surge and how does it influence the flavor?
faq.answer: The Talisker Surge was matured exclusively in ex-bourbon casks. This cask type imparts the whisky’s sweet vanilla and malt notes and results in a milder smokiness compared to other Talisker expressions, which are often aged in sherry or combination casks.

item.name: Jack Daniel's Bonded
item.description: Bottled-in-Bond (BIB) is one of the legal regulations governing the production of American whiskey, alongside the designation 'straight.' To be labeled as BIB, the whiskey must have been distilled by a distillery within a single distilling season. Subsequently, it must have matured for at least 4 years in a bonded warehouse and be bottled at 100 Proof (50% ABV). Jack Daniel's has recently introduced a Bonded release that we are tasting today.

tasting_notes.nose: The aroma has a powerful start. Intense caramel and vanilla greet the nose, accompanied by the characteristic scent of bananas that's typical of Jack Daniel's. Additionally, a delightful combination of spiciness and refined sugar becomes apparent.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the Tennessee Whiskey is initially typical of Jack Daniel's, but it's as if someone turned up the volume. A substantial dose of green banana comes through, which then transforms into a powerfully sweet combination of caramel and what we would best describe as Vanilla Coke. On the palate, we find a hint of spicy, charred oak.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium-length with a really nice and spicy kick. We're still thinking about charred oak, and somewhere buried in there, we discover a few cloves.

conclusion.marcel: Jack Daniel's Bonded is like a familiar buddy who suddenly turns up the volume – more power, more fun, yet still that typical charm. Neat, it’s a fantastic companion, bringing sweet caramel notes and a banana charm that simply lifts your spirits. For fans wanting more than just the Old No. 7, this is a clear home run.
conclusion.sascha: The Bonded expression strikes a successful balance between intense sweetness and spicy oak, which structurally enhances the classic Jack Daniel's character. At 50% ABV, the whiskey presents itself powerfully without losing harmony – a technically clean yet expressive Tennessee whiskey that clearly stands apart from the standard bottling.

faq.question: What does the term "Bottled-in-Bond" mean for Jack Daniel's Bonded?
faq.answer: Bottled-in-Bond (BIB) is a US legal regulation requiring that the whiskey be distilled at a single distillery within a six-month period and aged at least four years in a bonded warehouse. Additionally, it must be bottled at exactly 100 proof (50% alcohol). This designation guarantees quality and origin.

faq.question: How does Jack Daniel's Bonded differ in taste from other Jack Daniel's whiskeys?
faq.answer: Jack Daniel's Bonded exhibits a more intense and robust character than the standard releases. Notably, pronounced aromas of green banana, caramel, and vanilla combine with spicy oak notes and a subtle clove spice to offer a more complex tasting experience.

faq.question: Why did Jack Daniel's bottle the Bonded whiskey at 50% alcohol?
faq.answer: The 50% ABV (100 proof) bottling is a requirement for Bottled-in-Bond whiskeys. This higher alcohol content preserves the intense aromas and delivers a fuller flavor, better highlighting the whiskey’s characteristic profile.

item.name: Scallywag 10 Years Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing
item.description: Douglas Laing offers an array of blended malts from all the regions of Scotland in its Regional Malts range. The well known Scallywag - representing Speyside - has been extended with a 10 years bottling. A blend of Speyside malts matured in Oloroso and Pedro Ximénez ex-sherry casks. Today we find out if the age statement improves the already excellent Scallywag.

image.alt: Scallywag 10 Years Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose we are greeted by the harmony of Speyside and sherry casks. Cocoa, coffee and dark caramel are in the foreground. These are joined by fresh notes of lemon and honey. There is also some fruitiness of ripe red apples. A stronger note of old, aged leather hovers in the background.
tasting_notes.palate: On the tongue, the oiliness surprises with its high viscosity. At first, the flavour of honey and beeswax dominates. This is followed by strong aromas of sultanas and baking cocoa, rounded off by a pleasant peppery kick. On the palate, we find a spicy, floral dryness.
tasting_notes.finish: The malt's farewell is rather brief. The slight bitterness of oak and the sweetness of brown sugar remain.

conclusion.marcel: Scallywag 10 shows character with a charming blend of sweetness and spice that’s just plain fun. It’s like a cheeky mate who still commands respect with its rounded profile – perfect for enjoyable evenings without any fuss.
conclusion.sascha: The Scallywag 10 impresses with its balanced interplay between sherry-influenced fruity notes and spicy dryness, while the oiliness provides a pleasant structure. Despite a somewhat short finish, it remains a harmoniously composed Speyside blended malt with a clear cask signature.

faq.question: What makes the Scallywag 10 Year Small Batch Release special compared to the standard Scallywag?
faq.answer: The 10-years Small Batch Release is a deliberate marriage of various Speyside malts matured in Oloroso and Pedro Ximénez sherry casks. The extended aging and sherry cask maturation impart more intense aromas of cocoa, coffee, and dark caramel, as well as increased complexity compared to the standard Scallywag.

faq.question: How do the Oloroso and Pedro Ximénez sherry casks influence the flavor of this whisky?
faq.answer: The sherry casks give the whisky rich notes of raisins, baking cocoa, and a sweet honeyed touch. They also contribute to an oily texture and a spicy-floral dryness on the palate, which underline the character of the Speyside malt.

faq.question: Who is the Scallywag 10 Year Small Batch Release particularly suited for?
faq.answer: This whisky is ideal for enthusiasts who appreciate complex blended malts with a balanced combination of fruity, sweet, and spicy notes. It suits both newcomers to the Speyside region and experienced whisky drinkers seeking a harmonious sherry cask influence.

item.name: anCnoc 12 Years
item.description: The Highland malt AnCnoc is produced by the Knockdhu distillery. Supposedly they do not bottle under their own name to avoid confusion with Knockando. Today we have the 12 years and youngest bottling of the core range in the glass.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose is pleasantly malty with a good dose of grain. There are also fruity notes of banana and pear, rounded off by a fine vanilla. Behind this is we find some cinnamon spice.
tasting_notes.palate: The malt is full-bodied and pleasantly oily. The intense malt aroma lingers. We also think of dried banana chips. A beautifully integrated oak combines with nutty almond notes. On the palate we taste cinnamon and a slightly tangy acidity.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium long and fruity. The pleasant oak on the palate develops into a discreet spiciness.

conclusion.marcel: The anCnoc 12 delights with its full-bodied malt character and fruity notes, making every sip a pleasure. An uncomplicated Highland malt that’s fun and perfect for social gatherings. Absolutely recommended for anyone looking for a lively yet gentle single malt.
conclusion.sascha: The anCnoc 12 impresses with its balanced structure between malty cereals, fruity accents, and a subtle spiciness. The harmonious integration of oak and delicate acidity delivers a well-rounded tasting experience with a medium-length, fruity finish. A solid Highland malt, ideal for both beginners and connoisseurs who appreciate balance and clarity.

faq.question: Why is the whisky sold under the name anCnoc and not Knockdhu?
faq.answer: The whisky is bottled under the name anCnoc to avoid confusion with the Knockando distillery, as the names sound very similar. This is a deliberate decision by the distillery.

faq.question: What flavor notes are typical for the anCnoc 12 Year Highland Malt?
faq.answer: Typical notes include malty cereals, fruity hints of banana and pear, as well as vanilla. The profile is complemented by cinnamon spice, nutty almond tones, and a subtle oak note.

faq.question: How does the 12-year age influence the character of the anCnoc whisky?
faq.answer: With 12 years of maturation, the whisky develops a balanced harmony between intense malt aromas and integrated oak spiciness. The age contributes to a full-bodied, oily-smooth texture and a medium-length, fresh fruity finish.

item.name: Kilkerran 12 Years
item.description: Glengyle Distillery is one of the few distilleries in Campbeltown. After a long period of closure, it was reopened by Springbank at the beginning of the 21st century. Although Springbank and Glengyle are run as separate companies, in some ways they belong together. Glengyle bottles single malt whiskies under the Kilkerran brand. The 12-years, non-chill-filtered and non-coloured whisky is the current core range.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, there is a sweet smoke that is not overdone. There is also a good amount of oak and a sweet nuttiness, almost like marzipan. Once you get used to the peatiness, the fruity notes of cherries, plums and blueberries come to the fore.
tasting_notes.palate: The first thing that strikes you about Kilkerran is its oiliness. On the palate we find honey, a hint of vanilla and citrus notes. There is also a maritime saltiness, earthy-sweet peat smoke and a touch of oak.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium long, again salty and maritime. The oiliness leaves a pleasant sensation in the throat. We think of a fresh lemon roll.

conclusion.marcel: Kilkerran 12 shows that Campbeltown is capable of more than just smoky clichés. The combination of sweet nuttiness and maritime freshness is enjoyable, even if the smoke can sometimes be a bit too dominant. For fans of honest, straightforward malts, this is a worthwhile dram.
conclusion.sascha: The Kilkerran 12 impresses with its balanced interplay of oak, honey, and subtle peat smoke, while the maritime saltiness rounds out its character. Despite a solid structure, the flavor profile feels somewhat unbalanced due to the dominant smoke, which slightly disrupts the harmony.

faq.question: What does it mean that Kilkerran 12 years is non-chillfiltered and non-colored?
faq.answer: Non-chillfiltered means the whisky has not been chilled to remove cloudiness, preserving a more intense and natural flavor and texture. Non-colored indicates that no artificial coloring has been added, so the color comes solely from maturation in the cask.

faq.question: How does Kilkerran 12 years differ from other Campbeltown whiskies?
faq.answer: Kilkerran 12 years combines the maritime saltiness and earthy peat smoke typical of Campbeltown with pronounced sweet nuttiness and fruity notes like cherries and blueberries. Additionally, it is oily and non-chillfiltered, which gives it a distinctive richness of flavor.

faq.question: Why is the Glengyle Distillery important for whisky production in Campbeltown?
faq.answer: Glengyle was closed for a long time and was reopened by Springbank in the early 21st century, expanding the whisky offerings in Campbeltown. Although Glengyle and Springbank operate independently, they share resources and expertise, contributing to the region’s diversity and quality.

item.name: Ardnamurchan AD:02.22 Peated Cask Strength
item.description: AD:02.22 - behind this somewhat cryptic name we quite simply find a bottle from the Ardnamurchan distillery that was bottled in February 2022. The young single malt distillery is owned by the renowned bottler Adelphi and produces Scotch with a vision of sustainability and transparency.

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression is very sweet for a peated malt. We detect vanilla, citrus and a slight hint of oats. There is also a subtle hint of honey. Surrounded by cold fire smoke, we also find nutty flavours and some dried tobacco leaves. When the nose is allowed to breathe a little, the scent of ripe bananas emerges.
tasting_notes.palate: The great complexity of the nose continues. A blend of sugar syrup, lemon peel and vanilla. Wonderfully balanced and subtle, the sweetness is bound up in the smoke of a smouldering campfire. The very pleasant oiliness coats the tongue and palate with a slight dryness.
tasting_notes.finish: The single malt's finish is long and intense. A peppery oak note rounds off the AD.02:22 experience.

conclusion.marcel: A young whisky with character that, despite its youth, reveals an astonishing depth and a pleasant viscosity. The combination of sweet vanilla and honey notes with smoky accents turns every sip into a little adventure – definitely a treat for lovers of complex peated malt.
conclusion.sascha: The AD:02.22 presents itself as a well-structured peated malt with a successful balance between smoke, sweetness, and a subtle dryness in the finish. Despite the lack of age, the whisky offers a complex aroma profile and a lasting presence that underline its technical quality.

faq.question: What does 'Peated Cask Strength' mean for Ardnamurchan AD:02.22?
faq.answer: 'Peated' indicates that the whisky was made using peat smoke, giving it a smoky character. 'Cask Strength' means the whisky is bottled undiluted at its natural alcohol level of 58.2%.

faq.question: How does the peaty note influence the taste of this Ardnamurchan whisky?
faq.answer: The smoky flavor is present but not overpowering; it subtly blends with sweet aromas like vanilla, citrus, and honey, as well as nutty and tobacco-like nuances, creating a complex and balanced tasting experience.

faq.question: Why does Ardnamurchan AD:02.22 have no age statement (No Age Statement)?
faq.answer: The distillery deliberately omits an age statement to focus on the whisky’s flavor and quality rather than its age. This allows greater flexibility in selecting casks for bottling.

item.name: The Westfalian TW73
item.description: The Westfalian began producing regional premium whiskies in 2012. This means they can now offer 10-years bottlings of the German with the Scottish soul. We were able to secure part of the TW73 cask. A peated single malt matured in an ex-Port Charlotte sherry cask. As we are used to from The Westfalian, of course, single cask, unfiltered, uncoloured, uncut.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, the malt is voluminous, with smoked ham combined with intense fruity notes of plum and blackberry. We find aromas of grilled sausage and tobacco leaves. There is also a hint of an old leather sofa in a damp cellar. As you penetrate the heavy peat, this Westfalian becomes very fruity and the sherry cask comes to the fore.
tasting_notes.palate: Contrary to expectations, the first impression in the mouth is very sweet. Strong berry flavours are complemented by earthy smoke. Extremely complex, playing with the sweetness of jam and heavy peat smoke. The whisky is smooth and oily, but also leaves a certain dryness on the palate. We detect a hint of saltiness and subtle oak notes.
tasting_notes.finish: There is a slight tingling of pepper on the finish. The malt does not linger too long in the mouth, leaving a sweet smoke with a slight saltiness and again subtle oak.

conclusion.marcel: A genuine taste experience that immediately captivates with its smoky spice and fruity notes. The Westfalian TW73 demonstrates just how much character and drinking pleasure can be found in a German single malt – all while remaining charming and pleasantly approachable. Perfect for anyone unafraid of a bit of peat and a touch of bacon.
conclusion.sascha: A well-structured and balanced malt with complex aromas of peat smoke, sherry, and berries, distinguished by its oily texture and subtle salty note. Despite its 53.3% ABV, The Westfalian TW73 feels harmonious, offering a precise interplay of sweetness, smoke, and light oak, although the finish is somewhat short. A technical example of successful cask influence in German single malt.

faq.question: What makes the former Port Charlotte sherry cask special for the TW73?
faq.answer: The Port Charlotte sherry cask imparts an intense fruitiness to the TW73, with aromas of plum and blackberry, as well as a deep sherry sweetness that complements the whisky’s peaty character and adds complexity.

faq.question: How does the peat influence the flavor of this German single malt?
faq.answer: The peat provides smoky and earthy notes that define the whisky’s character. In the TW73, the peat is particularly heavy but balanced by sweet and fruity aromas, creating a harmonious balance.

faq.question: Why is the TW73 bottled unfiltered, uncolored, and undiluted?
faq.answer: Bottling without chill filtration, without added color, and at cask strength (53.3% ABV) preserves the whisky’s natural aromas and intensity, ensuring the single cask character is maintained to the fullest.

item.name: Dailuaine 10 Years - 82 Chapters to Newcastle
item.description: So this one is going to be tough to translate. The great team at 82 Chapters in Newcastle have released some special bottles in the past. Today we have a Community Exclusive bottling with a very pun-y marketing. This 10 years single malt from the Dailuaine distillery is dedicated to their brand ambassador Phil. As such, the german texts about it (and also the bottle's label) are riddled with puns where the name Phil in inserted instead of similar sounding syllables. Let us see if we are able to get this into english, as well. The malt has been matured for 9 years in an ex-bourbon hogshead, and was then finished for a year in a First Phil Moscatel cask.

tasting_notes.nose: The first thing we notice is the fruity scent of ripe peaches. There are also intense notes of Speyside - sweet wild honey and a strong malty sweetness. The fruit sweetness then gives way to overripe apples, surrounded by the scent of caramelised sweets. There is also a hint of red wine in the glass.
tasting_notes.palate: On the tip of the tongue, an intense sweet caramel note dominates. This quickly gives way to a great fruitiness. We Phil reminded of wild berries, cranberries and once again the typical Speyside honey. On the dry palate we find mint, some ginger and a really nice kick of white pepper.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long Philnish of the single malt is dominated by a nice oak spiciness. Again, a small amount of ginger, dark chocolate and a hint of fig.

conclusion.marcel: As always: 82 Chapters is fun. Not only the humorous marketing, but also the product quality of this bottling is first class. An excellent late summer whisky with a pleasant nose, fruity taste and spicy finish. A clear recommendation as a (not quite) insider tip, but unfortunately, as always, very quickly out of stock.
conclusion.sascha: As always: 82 Chapters is fun. Not only the humorous marketing, but also the product quality of this bottling is first class. An excellent late summer whisky with a pleasant nose, fruity taste and spicy finish. A clear recommendation as a (not quite) insider tip, but unfortunately, as always, very quickly out of stock.

item.name: Craigellachie 13 Years Bas-Armagnac Cask Finish
item.description: The 13-years single malt was post-matured in Armagnac casks from the Bas-Armagnac region - one of the three French Armagnac regions. The distillery still uses traditional worm tubs to cool the distillate. Although this is not as efficient and low-maintenance as newer condensation methods, it still gives the malt a special note.

tasting_notes.nose: The Speysider opens with lemongrass, a little honey and hay. This is joined by a colourful mix of tropical fruits, such as coconut and pineapple. The nose is rounded off by almonds, a light note of cinnamon and minimal toasted aromas.
tasting_notes.palate: Bitter, really bitter and tart like green apple in bitter almond sauce tastes the 13 years single malt. A small peppery and salty portion of brown rice mixed with synthetic lemon form an exotic flavour profile. You have to like it.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish then convinces again with slightly fruity and citrusy aromas with a hint of oak.

conclusion.marcel: If you enjoy intense bitterness, this one will be right up your alley – for everyone else, the Craigellachie 13 with Bas-Armagnac finish is more of a challenge. The fruity hints are present but get lost in the bitter-sour profile. A dramatic character that will definitely divide opinions.
conclusion.sascha: The balance of the Craigellachie 13 Years in Bas-Armagnac Cask Finish feels somewhat uneven due to the dominant bitterness and sour notes. The finish could be more intense in terms of Armagnac fruitiness to better highlight the complex structure. Overall, a solid but not entirely rounded release.

faq.question: How does the Bas-Armagnac cask finish influence the flavor of the Craigellachie 13 Years?
faq.answer: The Bas-Armagnac cask finish imparts additional fruity notes and a complex acidity typical of the region. This gives the Craigellachie 13 Years an exotic fruitiness with hints of tropical fruits and a slightly sour bitterness that complements the classic Speyside profile.

faq.question: Why does Craigellachie still use traditional worm tubs to cool the distillate?
faq.answer: Craigellachie uses traditional worm tubs because, although they are less efficient than modern condensation methods, they lend the whisky a richer, oilier texture and more distinctive roasted aromas. This contributes to a robust and unmistakable flavor.

faq.question: Which aromas dominate the flavor of the Craigellachie 13 Years Bas-Armagnac Cask Finish?
faq.answer: The flavor is dominated by a bitter, sour note of green apple and bitter almond, accompanied by peppery and salty nuances as well as a synthetic lemon note. The finish is medium-length with fruity and citrus aromas, rounded off by a touch of oak.

item.name: Bookers Small Batch (2019) 01E
item.description: One of the flagships of the Jim Beam distillery is Booker's Bourbon. Supposedly created by Booker Noe, Jim Beam only bottles 4 batches a year. "Uncut and unfiltered" means that we have the full power of a natural bourbon in our glass. Today it's Batch 2019-01E. This is the part of the first 2019 batch that was produced for the European market. The label promises 6 years and 3 months of maturation and almost 63% ABV.

tasting_notes.nose: The aroma load on the nose is a massive wall of caramel, vanilla, brown sugar - all in all an intensely sweet soup. As the nose evolves, softer notes of mint, a little apple and buttered popcorn emerge. The intense, spicy cloud of oak and a light resinous note hover over everything.
tasting_notes.palate: At first, the viscosity is overwhelming. Booker's presents its oiliness like a syrup. We find the sweetness of caramel, brown sugar and oak again. Dryness spreads over the palate. This is followed by slightly bitter orange, baked apple with vanilla sauce and a finely tuned spiciness of nuts and nutmeg.
tasting_notes.finish: This Kentucky Straight Bourbon is long lasting. Intense oak combines with a barely perceptible bitterness. At the same time, it is smooth and almost floral, with a hint of dark chocolate.

conclusion.marcel: If you're looking for a powerful bourbon with plenty of punch and character, Booker's Small Batch 2019-01E is a real heavyweight packed with sweet caramel power and spicy oak. Its intense viscosity demands your full attention but rewards you with drinking pleasure and a hint of dark chocolate – perfect for connoisseurs who enjoy their whisky bold and intense. However, its limited availability turns the drinking experience into a bit of a hunt, which slightly dampens the fun.
conclusion.sascha: Booker's Small Batch 2019-01E impresses with its clear structure and the balanced interplay of intense sweetness, oak spice, and delicate fruit notes, making the bourbon surprisingly well-rounded despite its nearly 63% ABV. The dense texture and long, nuanced finish with hints of bitter orange and dark chocolate showcase the craftsmanship behind it, though the limited availability reduces the practical value of this uncut Kentucky Straight Bourbon.

faq.question: What does "Uncut and unfiltered" mean for Booker's Small Batch 2019-01E?
faq.answer: "Uncut and unfiltered" means that the bourbon has neither been diluted with water nor filtered. As a result, it retains its full alcohol strength of around 63% ABV and preserves all natural aromas and oils, leading to an intense flavor experience.

faq.question: How does the 2019-01E batch differ from other Booker's releases?
faq.answer: The 2019-01E batch is part of the first 2019 batch, bottled specifically for the European market. It matured for about 6 years and 3 months and is distinguished by its particularly high alcohol strength and the characteristic combination of sweet and spicy aromas.

faq.question: What is the best way to drink Booker's Small Batch 2019-01E to enjoy its flavors?
faq.answer: Due to its high alcohol strength, it is recommended to drink Booker's Small Batch 2019-01E neat or with just a few drops of water. This helps open up the aromas of caramel, vanilla, oak, and spices without losing its intense character.

item.name: Glenrothes 26 Years (1988/2014) - Hart Brothers
item.description: The independent bottlers Hart Brothers advertise their bottlings as being subject to the highest standards of quality control. They distribute selected single cask bottlings, of course unfiltered and uncoloured. From this series we have a Speysider from the Glenrothes distillery in our glass today. Distilled in 1988 as a vintage whisky and then matured in casks for 26 years.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose is already very complex, with a wide range of aromas. We find vanilla, honey and a floral fruitiness similar to buttercup. These are joined by sweet notes of fresh peach and apple. When the malt is allowed to breathe a little, spicy notes of oak and malted barley emerge.
tasting_notes.palate: Like a typical Speysider, the profile evokes fruity freshness on the tongue. We think of peach iced tea, still vanilla sweetness and a malty cereal note on the tip of the tongue. This is overlaid by stronger flavours of oak, herbal tea and white pepper. There is also a very slight nuttiness, mostly reminiscent of almonds. This malt is not dry and is extremely oily.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish pushes a very pleasant, but quite discreet, amount of oak. No bitterness at all, just pure, natural, spicy oak.

conclusion.marcel: This Glenrothes 26 from Hart Brothers showcases just how elegant an old Speysider can be: fresh, rounded, and full of sweet fruit that’s genuinely enjoyable. The aromas practically dance on the tongue, inviting you to savor summer in a glass – almost like a little holiday. Those who embrace this character will be rewarded with a warm embrace that simply brings joy.
conclusion.sascha: With its balanced aroma and flavor profile, the Glenrothes 1988/2014 impresses through a precise harmony between fruitiness, oak spice, and malty depth. The perfect integration of vanilla and herbal notes, combined with an oily texture, makes it a technically very mature single malt. Despite its 26 years, the finish remains pleasantly fresh, avoiding any bitterness – an impressive example of controlled cask maturation.

faq.question: What does it mean that the Glenrothes 1988/2014 from Hart Brothers is unfiltered and natural color?
faq.answer: Unfiltered means the whisky has not undergone chill filtration, preserving more of its natural aromas and oils. Natural color indicates that no coloring agents have been added, so the color comes purely from the cask and maturation process.

faq.question: How does the 26-year cask maturation influence the taste of this Glenrothes whisky?
faq.answer: The long 26-year maturation creates a complex aromatic profile with intense oak notes that harmonize beautifully with fruity and floral nuances. At the same time, the whisky develops a velvety texture and a balanced, spicy finish without any bitterness.

faq.question: What distinguishes a Speyside whisky like this Glenrothes from other Scottish regions?
faq.answer: Speyside whiskies are typically characterized by a fruity and malty aroma, often with notes of vanilla, honey, and fresh fruit. Unlike the smokier whiskies from Islay, Speysiders tend to be milder and more elegant in flavor, as clearly demonstrated by this Glenrothes 1988/2014.

item.name: Bulleit 95 Rye
item.description: With Bulleit, Diageo presents whiskies with the rough image of the Wild West. In addition to two bourbons, the distillery, which as far as we know now actually distils itself, also offers a rye. In this case, the 95 on the label refers to the rye content of the mash bill, which is a whopping 95%. Unusually high, as you often get the "barely legal" rye with 51% rye.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, we find a very bourbon-like profile with apricots, oranges and caramel. Behind this, the typical rye spice comes to the fore. We think of muffins with dark chocolate. All in all, very pleasant, velvety and spicy with a hint of pepper.
tasting_notes.palate: The taste is also a mixture of sweet and savoury. Typically American, we have pancakes with maple syrup and chocolate-covered sultanas on the tongue. Again velvety and caramel-sweet, Bulleit Rye also changes in the mouth to spicy rye notes. These are joined by a mixture of dried herbs and brown sugar. Typically BBQ, there is a hint of oak in the finish.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish pleasantly continues the spiciness of the rye. Further light oak tones are accompanied by herbs and cloves.

conclusion.marcel: Bulleit 95 Rye is an uncomplicated pleasure with a rugged charm and a touch of humor – perfect when you just want to enjoy an honest whiskey without any fuss. It doesn’t surprise you, but it doesn’t disappoint either, making it a great fit for a relaxed evening with friends.
conclusion.sascha: The Bulleit 95 Rye impresses with a solid balance between sweet bourbon notes and spicy rye character, while maintaining a clear and approachable structure. For a rye with such a high rye content, it delivers a well-rounded profile that’s especially suitable for beginners and straightforward tastings.

faq.question: What does the high rye content of 95% in Bulleit 95 Rye mean?
faq.answer: The 95% rye content in the mash bill is unusually high and results in a pronounced, spicy rye character. Compared to typical rye whiskeys that contain at least 51% rye, Bulleit 95 Rye offers more intense peppery and herbal notes.

faq.question: How does Bulleit 95 Rye differ in taste from classic bourbons?
faq.answer: Although Bulleit 95 Rye shows typical bourbon aromas on the nose, such as apricot and caramel, the high rye content brings spicy, peppery, and herbal nuances. On the palate, sweetness combines with spicy rye notes and finishes with a slightly woody, BBQ-like character.

faq.question: Why does Bulleit 95 Rye have no age statement on the label?
faq.answer: Bulleit 95 Rye is bottled without an age statement (No Age Statement), meaning the exact maturation period is not disclosed. This allows the distillery to blend different vintages to ensure a consistent flavor and style.

item.name: Lochlea Cask Strength Batch 1
item.description: A young distillery has many "firsts". The first bottling ever, the first bottling of the Core Range and at some point also the first bottling at cask strength. This is what we have for tasting and review today.

tasting_notes.nose: The single malt opens immediately with ripe light and dark fruits - there is no need to "let it breathe". Pear, apple, blood orange, rum, cinnamon, tobacco leaves, milk chocolate, vanilla and coffee beans open a true composition of flavours. Red grapes are also perceptible, which flatter with a light wine note.
tasting_notes.palate: Full steam ahead - the usual Lochlea signature is clearly in the foreground. Bright fruits and light nutty notes. Mouth-watering and slightly dry, the whisky shines with caramel, vanilla and forest honey. Surprisingly not spicy at all and really smooth, we taste a big load of buttery cinnamon buns. The palate is rounded off by toasted croutons.
tasting_notes.finish: Medium-long, the cask-strength whisky tingles on the palate with hints of fresh oak and chocolate.

conclusion.marcel: A lively cask strength whisky that delights with its fruity variety and sweet notes of buttery cinnamon rolls. No harshness, no off-flavors – just pure drinking pleasure. Lochlea demonstrates here how a clever cask blend can bring a young malt vividly to life.
conclusion.sascha: The balanced combination of 70% bourbon and 30% oloroso casks creates a harmonious, complex flavor profile at 60.1% ABV. Despite its youth, the whisky feels structured and well-balanced, with oak and chocolate notes elegantly rounding off the medium-length finish.

faq.question: What does 'Cask Strength' mean for Lochlea Cask Strength Batch 1?
faq.answer: 'Cask Strength' means the whisky has not been diluted to drinking strength and is bottled at its natural cask strength of 60.1% alcohol by volume. This results in a more intense aroma and flavor experience.

faq.question: How does the young age affect the taste of Lochlea Cask Strength Batch 1?
faq.answer: Although the whisky is young, it reveals a surprisingly complex range of aromas with ripe fruits, spices, and sweet notes. The youthful age brings freshness and vibrancy without sacrificing depth.

faq.question: What makes Lochlea Cask Strength Batch 1 from the Lowlands region unique?
faq.answer: The Lowlands are known for lighter, often floral whiskies. Lochlea combines this with a robust cask strength and an unusually diverse flavor combination of fruits, spices, and chocolate, which is quite rare in such intensity for this region.

item.name: Arran 10 Years
item.description: Arran Distillery was established on the Isle of Arran in the mid 90's. Some of the casks were stored at Springbank until the recent expansion of the warehouses. The casks are now on site and a second distillery (Lagg) has been built. Today we taste the 10 years Core Range bottling.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose is flattering and complex. Strong vanilla from ex-bourbon casks is at the forefront. This is joined by honey, pear and sweet, fruity lemon juice. The aroma develops into an almost waxy sensation on the nose. We find ripe peaches and grated coconut. In the background there is a slight herbal note, reminiscent of green tea.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, Arran 10 is a great combination of full-bodied, oily and velvety. Fresh fruit aromas of mango and pineapple join the coconut note we picked up on the nose. The malt is fresh and cereal-like, like a bowl of muesli. Again, the vanilla is very prominent.
tasting_notes.finish: There is a wonderful oak kick on the finish. A slight crispness lingers on the palate in the medium-long finish, accompanied by intense, slightly bitter lemon zest.

conclusion.marcel: The Arran 10 surprises with its fruity freshness and creamy vanilla notes – a genuine pleasure that leaves you wanting more. Whether you're a beginner or a connoisseur, it’s a charming companion that makes drinking fun, even if it doesn’t astonish at every turn. Highly recommended for anyone looking for an uncomplicated, lively single malt.
conclusion.sascha: Structurally, the Arran 10 displays a balanced harmony between sweet vanilla, fruity notes, and a pleasantly present spicy oak finish. Despite its solid complexity, it sometimes lacks the depth of focus to truly stand out, but remains a reliable core range bottling with good value for money.

faq.question: How does maturation in ex-bourbon casks influence the flavor of the Arran 10 Years?
faq.answer: Maturation in ex-bourbon casks imparts the Arran 10 Years with its distinctive vanilla note as well as sweet aromas of honey and fruity lemon juice. These casks also contribute to the oily, velvety texture and the fruity nuances.

faq.question: What unique characteristics does the location of the Arran Distillery on the Isle of Arran bring?
faq.answer: The Isle of Arran offers a cool, humid climate with proximity to the sea, which influences the whisky’s maturation. The fresh air and maritime environment promote a balanced development of fruity and spicy aromas.

faq.question: Why were some of the casks initially stored at Springbank, and how does this affect the whisky?
faq.answer: Before the expansion of its own warehouses, casks were stored at the nearby Springbank Distillery to ensure proper maturation. This collaboration left no direct flavor traces but served quality assurance during the early phase.

item.name: The Westfalian TW134
item.description: The Westfalian stands for unique bottlings and is not afraid to experiment. Cask 134 is subtitled "Crazy Cask Experiment". A single corn whisky - distilled from maize (corn for you US folks) - matured in an ex-Old Pulteney sherry cask. You don't often find this combination in Scotland nor in the USA.

tasting_notes.nose: The first thing that comes to mind is "Herrencreme" (the delicious vanilla dessert with rum). This is accompanied by the sweet scent of grape and nut chocolate with a good portion of caramel. Behind this we find the clear, cereal-like scent of corn and oats.
tasting_notes.palate: On the tip of the tongue, this Westfalian is a real sugar bomb. We taste honey, cream toffee and butter biscuits. Accompanied by a wonderful viscosity and a surprising dryness. On the palate, chocolate and nut flavours unfold.
tasting_notes.finish: This single corn lingers surprisingly long. An interesting spiciness is left on the palate. Although there are no pronounced oak notes, the bitter-sweet blend of maize and chocolate persists.

conclusion.marcel: The Westfalian TW134 is a true character, delivering a sweet sugar bomb and rich chocolate notes that make it a real joy – this isn’t your run-of-the-mill whisky, but a wild experiment that divides opinions and excites. Those who embrace the unusual corn and sherry cask combination will experience pure drinking pleasure with a wink.
conclusion.sascha: A technically impressive Single Corn whisky that scores with a fine balance between sweetness, viscosity, and a surprising dryness. The complex interplay of corn aromas and sherry cask influences shows a boldness in innovation, though the overall profile remains somewhat distinctive and may not suit every palate.

faq.question: What makes The Westfalian TW134 Single Corn Whisky special compared to other corn whiskies?
faq.answer: The TW134 is a Single Corn whisky distilled from corn and matured in a former Pulteney sherry cask. This cask combination is rare, as corn whiskies typically mature in America, while the sherry cask originates from Scotland. This results in a unique flavor spectrum featuring sweet, chocolatey, and spicy notes.

faq.question: How does maturation in the ex-Pulteney sherry cask influence the taste of the TW134?
faq.answer: Maturation in the former Pulteney sherry cask imparts a complex blend of sweet and nutty aromas reminiscent of grape-nut chocolate and caramel. At the same time, the cask supports the development of a pleasant dryness and a long-lasting, subtly spicy finish.

faq.question: What is the alcohol content of The Westfalian TW134 and how does it affect the drinking experience?
faq.answer: The TW134 has an alcohol content of 55.8% ABV. This strength delivers an intense flavor explosion with a robust viscosity. Despite the high alcohol level, the whisky remains well balanced through its sweet and creamy aromas and reveals a surprising dryness that makes the tasting experience exciting.

item.name: The Westfalian TW121
item.description: Thomas Ewers bottles German whisky with a Scottish soul under the brand name The Westfalian. The Westfalian is only available as a single cask. Today we have Cask 121 in the glass - a 6 years single malt matured in an ex-Tobermory Sherry cask.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose is a real fruit bomb. We smell blackberries, grapes and a lot of dried fruit and dates. The nose develops into a combination of walnut and toasted marshmallow. We think of freshly toasted brown bread.
tasting_notes.palate: The malt starts off with great viscosity and oiliness. There are sweet notes of molasses and oatmeal. Then the palate changes to cinnamon, chocolate and an earthy nuttiness with walnut, hazelnut and roasted almonds. Despite the use of unpeated malt, there is a hint of smoke.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium long and sparkling. There is a slight saltiness and a good amount of dark fruit. Probably due to its young age, the oak note is barely perceptible.

conclusion.marcel: The Westfalian TW121 delivers a fruity explosion full of charm in the glass, leaving you wanting more. Despite its young age, this malt reveals an impressive depth and character, even though the price feels a bit steep due to the limited edition. A genuine experience for whisky enthusiasts who appreciate regional highlights.
conclusion.sascha: This 6-years single malt impresses with its balanced structure and complex aroma profile, ranging from fruity notes to subtle hints of smoke and nuts. The balance between viscosity and flavor depth is remarkable, although the short maturation shows a slightly uneven finish. For lovers of carefully aged, regional whiskies, this cask is well worth exploring.

faq.question: What does the ex-Tobermory sherry cask contribute to the flavor of The Westfalian TW121?
faq.answer: The ex-Tobermory sherry cask imparts pronounced fruit notes such as blackberries, grapes, and dried fruits, along with a sweet depth featuring dates. It also provides the nutty and lightly smoky nuances in the flavor.

faq.question: Why does The Westfalian TW121 have a slight smoky taste despite using unpeated malt?
faq.answer: Although unpeated malt was used, the hint of smoke likely comes from the maturation in the former Tobermory cask, which previously held smoky aromas.

faq.question: How does the young age of 6 years influence the character of this German single malt?
faq.answer: The relatively young age results in a lively and fruity aroma with a lightly pronounced oak note and a medium-length, tingling finish characterized by subtle saltiness and dark fruit.

item.name: Lochlea Sowing Edition (Second Crop)
item.description: The second year of seasonal releases from Lochlea has begun. We were kindly provided with the Sowing Edition by the distillery for review. The single malt is matured for 3-4 years in a first fill bourbon cask.

tasting_notes.nose: The malt opens with sugar syrup, red apples and vanilla - fresh and fruity with a slight hint of citrus. This is accompanied by wine gum and a slight hint of white wine (grapes). Fitting for the transitional phase from spring to summer.
tasting_notes.palate: The whisky from the Lowlands opens quite young. Almond pastry (nutty notes, cereal notes and sweet) is refined with an idea of toffee. At the same time, its youth flashes through again and again - and not too scarcely. A little oak and coniferous forest can be glimpsed in the background.
tasting_notes.finish: Sweetish in the finish, the first release of the second season lingers for a medium length and burns slightly.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that immediately delights with its fruity nose and playful character – perfect for celebrating the fresh season. On the palate, it reveals its youthful side, which also lends it a charming carefree quality. For fans of light, lively Lowland malts, this is a fun dram that leaves you wanting more.
conclusion.sascha: The Sowing Edition displays a clear structure with distinct notes of almond pastry and toffee, but the youthfulness of the whisky comes through too strongly on the palate, making it feel somewhat unbalanced. The combination of first fill bourbon cask maturation and fresh fruit notes works well on the nose, yet the palate lacks depth and harmony. A solid Lowland malt that still has room to develop.

faq.question: How does maturation in a first fill bourbon cask influence the flavor of the Lochlea Sowing Edition?
faq.answer: Maturation in a first fill bourbon cask imparts the Lochlea Sowing Edition with sweet vanilla and toffee notes, along with fruity accents of red apples and citrus. The cask also contributes a gentle oak spiciness that balances the whisky’s youthful character.

faq.question: Why is the Lochlea Sowing Edition ready to drink despite its relatively short maturation of 3-4 years?
faq.answer: Thanks to careful cask selection and distillation, the whisky shows remarkable balance that doesn’t overly emphasize its youth. The combination of fruity aromas, sweet notes, and moderate oak spice makes it accessible and interesting even at this age.

faq.question: What makes the Lochlea Sowing Edition stand out compared to other Lowland whiskies?
faq.answer: The Lochlea Sowing Edition impresses with its fresh and fruity aroma profile, characterized by sugar syrup, red apples, and a hint of citrus. Additionally, the whisky offers a rare combination of almond pastry, toffee, and a lightly resinous pine forest note, setting it apart from other Lowland single malts.

item.name: Scallywag Chocolate Edition (2023) - Douglas Laing
item.description: For the 6th time, the family-owned Douglas Laing & Co. from Glasgow is releasing the limited Chocolate Edition of the Scallywag range. 4,200 bottles of malts matured in Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso sherry casks were married to create the most chocolatey whisky possible.

tasting_notes.nose: The blend opens with wild berries and a handful of dates. These are joined by intense notes of dried fruit and clove. Last but not least - who would have thought it - a surge of fluffy dark chocolate mousse. On a second sniff, we detect espresso shake from the filter holder, citrus aroma and some fresh tobacco.
tasting_notes.palate: Exceedingly powerful and sweet with very balanced dried fruit flavours, the Scallywag, which lies somewhere between Sehrrybombe and chocolate cake, shows its prowess. A seamless continuation of the nose follows in the mouth.
tasting_notes.finish: With spicy oak, the blended malt finishes sweetly fruity.

conclusion.marcel: With the Scallywag Chocolate Edition 2023, you don’t just get a whisky, but a small taste experience full of chocolate and dried fruits – perfect for anyone who loves bold and sweet flavors. However, if you’re just starting out with whisky, you might want to wait a little longer, as this dram is more of a treat for seasoned connoisseurs.
conclusion.sascha: The Scallywag Chocolate Edition impresses with its harmonious balance between intense sherry notes and chocolaty sweetness, making it a solid representative of Speyside blends. Technically well-balanced, it does lack a bit of complexity in places to elevate it beyond very good.

faq.question: Why is the Scallywag Chocolate Edition 2023 called a blended malt?
faq.answer: The Scallywag Chocolate Edition 2023 is called a blended malt because it is composed of various malt whiskies without the addition of grain whisky. This creates a complex flavor profile that unites the characteristics of the individual malts.

faq.question: What role do the Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso sherry casks play in the maturation of this whisky?
faq.answer: The Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso sherry casks impart the whisky’s intense fruit and chocolate aromas. They provide the sweet notes of dried fruits and the distinctive chocolaty flavor that is typical for the Chocolate Edition.

faq.question: How does the Chocolate Edition differ from other Scallywag releases?
faq.answer: The Chocolate Edition is specifically matured in sherry casks and is characterized by its pronounced chocolate aromas, which arise from the combination of Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso sherry casks. Unlike other Scallywag variants, this edition focuses more strongly on sweet and fruity notes with a distinct chocolate character.

item.name: Aberfeldy 12 Years
item.description: Aberfeldy Distillery was founded by the Dewar brothers. As well as the well-known Dewar brand budget blends, they also produce a single malt under the distillery's name. Today we taste the 12 years Aberfeldy bottling - the entry point to their core single malt range.

tasting_notes.nose: The start on the nose is smooth and soft. We first smell sweet honey and the fruitiness of unripe apples and pears. This blends well with hints of lemon zest and sweetened whipped cream. There is almost a waxy texture to the nose.
tasting_notes.palate: This single malt is impressively smooth and creamy. We think of cheesecake and sugared quark desserts. There is also a light, fruity acidity that reminds us of the skin of a Granny Smith apple.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is short and crisp. No intense kick, just a light, pleasant oak note to round off the creamy, smooth malt.

conclusion.marcel: Aberfeldy 12 is a rock-solid standard in the classic Highlander profile. Those who like it a little fruitier and less floral than typical Speysiders will feel right at home here. With no corners or edges and just 40% ABV, this is a great starter that lacks a unique selling point to really stand out.
conclusion.sascha: Aberfeldy 12 is a rock-solid standard in the classic Highlander profile. Those who like it a little fruitier and less floral than typical Speysiders will feel right at home here. With no corners or edges and just 40% ABV, this is a great starter that lacks a unique selling point to really stand out.

item.name: Blade And Bow
item.description: Stitzel-Weller is an epitome of American bourbon culture. The legendary distillery was, until its closure in 1992, the original producer of many well-known bottlings. Diageo reopened the distillery in 2014. Blade and Bow is a tribute to Stitzel-Weller. Matured in the solera process, each bottle has a small part of original Stitzel-Weller in it.

tasting_notes.nose: We smell peach, biscuits and vanilla. Behind this we find hints of oak and cream toffee. If you give the bourbon some time in the glass, ideas of cinnamon and a slight alcoholic note develop.
tasting_notes.palate: Not as dull as the smell, the taste is a bit more intense. Vanilla and white pepper are accompanied by a cherry note on the palate. The oak and caramel come through more strongly. The mouthfeel is completed by an almost waxy dryness.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is rather short. We perceive hay and slightly floral notes. Also, the continuation of pepper and oak.

conclusion.marcel: Blade and Bow impresses with its wonderfully soft roundness and a pleasant blend of sweet and peppery notes – a bourbon made for relaxed enjoyment. While it lacks that final kick and a bit of depth, it’s precisely this harmlessness that makes it so likeable. A good choice for a cozy evening when you don’t want an overpowering character.
conclusion.sascha: Blade and Bow presents itself structurally clean and balanced, with a harmonious interplay of vanilla, pepper, and oak. However, the bourbon comes across as somewhat pale in terms of intensity and complexity, which limits the overall impression. For connoisseurs who value balance, it offers a solid, if not outstanding, solera construction.

faq.question: What does the solera aging mean for Blade and Bow Bourbon?
faq.answer: Solera aging is a process where younger and older bourbon components are continuously blended. This means every bottle of Blade and Bow contains a small portion of original Stitzel-Weller bourbon, ensuring consistent quality and complexity.

faq.question: What flavor notes can I expect from Blade and Bow Bourbon?
faq.answer: On the nose, peach, ladyfinger biscuit, and vanilla dominate, complemented by oak and creamy toffee. On the palate, vanilla, white pepper, and cherry unfold, accompanied by caramel and oak. The finish is short with notes of hay, floral accents, as well as pepper and oak.

faq.question: Why does Blade and Bow have no age statement on the bottle?
faq.answer: Blade and Bow uses solera aging, where different vintages are blended. The age therefore varies, and a fixed age statement is deliberately omitted to highlight the unique blend and character of the bottling.

item.name: Dalmore Valour
item.description: The Dalmore Valour is a Travel Exclusive, which was first introduced in Qatar. In principle, we are rather sceptical about Travel Exclusives, but we give every whisky a chance.

tasting_notes.nose: Fresh strong citrus note, sweet port, oranges and a little honey. A little cinnamon or cardamom and dust join in.
tasting_notes.palate: Lots of chocolate, exaggerated oaky bitterness and poorly integrated alcohol. Plus earth, light plum and even more bitterness. Little flavour and a lot of water. Harmony? Not at all.
tasting_notes.finish: Short without exciting notes. Bitter.

conclusion.marcel: From the very first sip, the Dalmore Valour reveals itself more as a tired shadow than a pleasure – too much bitterness and too little character. The claimed maturation in old Matusalem sherry casks remains an empty promise that doesn’t save the drinking experience. For me, a clear no; this one stays on the shelf.
conclusion.sascha: The Dalmore Valour exposes an unbalanced structure, dominated by bitterness and lacking harmony. Despite interesting citrus and port wine notes, the mouthfeel suffers from poor integration of the alcohol and a short, unimpressive finish. Overall, it lacks the complexity and depth to stand out as a Travel Exclusive.

faq.question: Why is the Dalmore Valour called a Travel Exclusive and where is it available?
faq.answer: The Dalmore Valour is a Travel Exclusive, meaning it was specifically produced for sale to travelers in duty-free shops. It was first introduced in Qatar and is primarily available at international airports.

faq.question: How does the Dalmore Valour differ in taste from other Dalmore releases?
faq.answer: Compared to other Dalmore whiskies, the Valour shows an unusually dominant bitterness with strong oak notes and less harmony. The freshness of citrus and sweet port wine is accompanied by a rather unpleasant alcohol note and a short, bitter finish.

faq.question: Does the Dalmore Valour have an age statement and how does that affect the whisky?
faq.answer: The Dalmore Valour is a non-age-statement (NAS) whisky. The absence of an age statement means that casks of varying ages were used for the bottling, which can lead to a less clear flavor profile and a stronger dominance of wood and bitter notes.

item.name: Ardnamurchan AD 10.21:06
item.description: The Ardnamurchan distillery is named after its location, the Ardnamurchan Peninsula in the Scottish Highlands. The still young distillery (bottling of the first cask in 2014) is considered one of the most important newcomers on the Scotch scene, perhaps also due to the long history of Adelphi Distillery Ltd, which also operates as an independent bottler.

tasting_notes.nose: A sweet and sour bouquet stings the nose intensely and is a little overwhelming. Once you have got used to these aromas, you will find a real variety of flavours underneath. A salty orange meets spicy oak, which is lightly interwoven with an iodised peat note. In between, a red baked apple in vanilla sauce flashes up.
tasting_notes.palate: The light peat smoke from the nose becomes more definite on the tongue, followed by a peppery (alcoholic) spiciness. The watery, voluminous mouthfeel opens sour and bitter (lemon balm, artichokes) and transitions into an herbaceous taste (baked potato spice mix). The journey ends with a fruity-sweet region and is again strongly reminiscent of red apple in vanilla sauce.
tasting_notes.finish: The dry, medium-long finish is slightly peaty-berry, has a bit of dark chocolate in it and is still youthfully bitter.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that’s a lot of fun with its wild combination of aromas, but can sometimes feel a bit overwhelming. The sweet and sour notes paired with the peat smoke make it enjoyable to drink, even if it’s not entirely polished yet—you can taste the young spirit of the distillery. Definitely an exciting dram from the Highlands for fans of character and surprises.
conclusion.sascha: The Ardnamurchan AD 10.21:06 presents an interesting structural profile but occasionally shows a certain imbalance between the intense fruit notes and the sharp herbal spice. The complex interplay of aromas is complemented by a medium-length, lightly peated finish, though there’s still room for greater harmony. A solid Highland malt with potential for future maturation.

faq.question: What makes the Ardnamurchan AD 10.21:06 special in the whisky scene?
faq.answer: The Ardnamurchan AD 10.21:06 comes from a relatively young distillery that has been producing since 2014. It combines traditional Highland elements with modern production techniques and is known for its complex aroma profile, ranging from sweet and sour notes to iodine-rich peat smoke.

faq.question: How does the peat smoke influence the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: The peat smoke in the Ardnamurchan AD 10.21:06 is subtle but present. It provides a smoky depth that blends with spicy oak, salty orange, and fruity notes like red apple, without overpowering the whisky.

faq.question: Why does this whisky have no age statement (NAS)?
faq.answer: The Ardnamurchan AD 10.21:06 is a No Age Statement (NAS) whisky, meaning no specific age is declared. This allows the distillery to blend different casks and vintages to achieve a consistent flavor and a complex range of aromas.

item.name: Patrisbrunna Madeira Cask (2018)
item.description: Today we continue our series on Patrisbrunna Paderborn single malts. The Kö13 restaurant distributes its own small series of extremely regional own maturations. The Madeira Cask bottling matured for 4 years in Portuguese wine casks. This gives the malt a really interesting color - almost like a rosé wine.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose presents fresh fruity with lemon and fig. This is joined by pleasant nutty notes. Rounded off by marzipan and a wonderful maltiness. Behind it, the smell becomes intensely vinous but also a little ethanol.
tasting_notes.palate: The taste is wonderfully intense fruity. We think of candied red fruits and blood orange. Behind that, as was to be expected, we find malt and a hint of walnut. The distinct vinosity of the nose resonates discreetly.
tasting_notes.finish: In the finish in the malt rather short. With a slight tingle of oak the continuation of the nutty notes present themselves. Behind that, there is also a slight youthful spiciness.

conclusion.marcel: A true character with lively fruitiness and a touch of youthful cheekiness – the Patrisbrunna Madeira Cask brings fun to the glass without ever denying its origins. For fans of regional discoveries, it’s an entertaining dram with plenty of quirks and edges.
conclusion.sascha: The Madeira Cask displays a remarkable balance between intense fruitiness and nutty notes, with the wine influence well integrated. Despite its slight youthfulness, the bottling impresses with a clean structure and a pleasantly distinctive finish.

faq.question: How does maturation in Madeira casks influence the flavor of the Patrisbrunna Madeira Cask 2018?
faq.answer: Maturation in Portuguese Madeira wine casks imparts a pronounced fruity character with hints of candied red fruits and blood orange, along with a distinctive winey note that sets it apart from traditional cask influences.

faq.question: What makes the Patrisbrunna Madeira Cask 2018 special compared to other German single malts?
faq.answer: This whisky comes from a small regional independent bottling that matured for only four years, developing a unique flavor profile featuring fruity and nutty notes, supported by the special choice of Madeira wine casks.

faq.question: What sensory experience can I expect when tasting the Patrisbrunna Madeira Cask 2018?
faq.answer: The nose is dominated by fresh citrus and fig aromas, complemented by marzipan and maltiness. On the palate, intense fruitiness, malt, and walnut emerge, accompanied by a subtle winey character. The finish is short, with light oak, nutty notes, and a youthful sharpness.

item.name: St. Kilian Signature Edition Thirteen
item.description: The St. Kilian distillery releases their Signature Editions at irregular intervals. These are strictly limited bottlings of selected casks. For the 13th Signature Edition, we were sent a deconstruction set, i.e. samples of the individual barrels, as well as a sample of the final product. A super exciting experience! Below is our impression of the Signature Edition Thirteen, as well as (at the very bottom) a breakdown of the individual parts.

tasting_notes.nose: The single malt opens with dominant citrus, vanilla and caramel notes. In the background, there is a pleasant campfire smoke note, which is greatly supported by cinnamon and a certain florality. Overall malty and mild, we like the nose very much.
tasting_notes.palate: Balanced and round, the caramel sweetness combines with a floral, grassy note that we could already perceive in the nose. Cinnamon and ginger, as well as dark chocolate pair with peach jam to create an exciting composition that is both slightly peppery and sweetly mild. The smoke is supportively present, but does not play in the foreground.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium long with a slight fruitiness and dryness.

conclusion.marcel: The Signature Edition Thirteen is a genuine delight, playfully captivating with its smoky depth and sweet caramel notes. Despite the 54ppm peat smoke, it remains approachable and invites relaxed sipping – a fantastic testament to the art of blending. Anyone who appreciates complex yet easy-drinking drams should definitely grab this one!
conclusion.sascha: A well-structured single malt that masterfully balances smoke, sweetness, and spice notes. The complexity arises from the careful selection of the five cask components, with the Palatinate oak standing out in particular. For lovers of precisely crafted signature editions, this whisky delivers a technically impressive performance.

faq.question: What does the Deconstruction Set in the St. Kilian Signature Edition Thirteen mean?
faq.answer: The Deconstruction Set contains samples of the individual casks used to compose the Signature Edition Thirteen, as well as a sample of the finished blend. This allows you to taste each cask profile separately and better understand the complexity of the bottling.

faq.question: How does the relatively young age of 4 years affect the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: At 4 years, the whisky is comparatively young but shows remarkable maturity thanks to the cask selection. The fruitiness, maltiness, and subtle smokiness are well balanced, without the whisky coming across as too sharp or underdeveloped.

faq.question: Why is the alcohol content of 53.9% in this single malt higher than in many other whiskies?
