faq.answer: An alcohol content of 53.9% indicates that the bottling is either cask strength or only lightly diluted. This preserves the intense aromas and delivers a powerful flavor experience that can be adjusted with a little water according to personal preference.

item.name: Dalwhinnie 15 Years
item.description: If you travel to Scotland, the Dalwhinnie distillery is often one of the first to be visited because of its location. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. For us, Dalwhinnie 15 Years was the first single malt we ever drank. 16 years later, we now open a bottle again.

tasting_notes.nose: Very floral with lots of vanilla and blossom honey, the Highlander opens soft and fresh. Very citrusy and tartly acidic, the sweetness is embraced. We are not quite sure whether a slight smoky note is resonating or not.
tasting_notes.palate: Like the nose, the scotch is fruity and mild. Low-fat curd (sour, dry) pairs with honey and oatmeal. Here and there we still perceive a little wood. Smoke is also present - or is it?
tasting_notes.finish: The Dalwhinnie 15 year finishes with a short, slightly bitter aftertaste.

conclusion.marcel: The Dalwhinnie 15 feels more like an old acquaintance who has lost some of their charm – soft and mild, but unfortunately quite dull as well. It impressed as a first single malt back in the day, but today it simply lacks the excitement and that certain something that makes you want more.
conclusion.sascha: The Dalwhinnie 15 presents itself with solid structure, yet without great balance or depth of flavor. The floral notes and fruity acidity are pleasant enough, but they can’t mask the overall simple and slightly bitter finish – for a 15-years Highlander, it remains rather unremarkable.

faq.question: How does the Dalwhinnie 15 differ from other Highland whiskies?
faq.answer: The Dalwhinnie 15 is characterized by its floral and vanilla nose as well as a mild, fruity flavor profile. Compared to other Highland whiskies, it is less smoky and leans more towards fresh citrus notes with a slightly bitter finish.

faq.question: Does the location of the Dalwhinnie distillery influence the whisky’s taste?
faq.answer: Yes, the distillery is situated at one of the highest elevations in the Scottish Highlands, resulting in a cooler climate. This slow maturation at lower temperatures gives the Dalwhinnie 15 its gentle, balanced, and fresh character.

faq.question: Is there a noticeable smoky note in the Dalwhinnie 15?
faq.answer: The perception of smokiness in the Dalwhinnie 15 is subjective and rather subtle. Some detect light smoke or peat aromas, others do not. Overall, a floral and fruity profile with honey and wood tones dominates.

item.name: Penderyn Madeira Finish
item.description: The Penderyn Madeira Finish Single Malt comes from Wales and thus from a rather untypical whisky region. What makes the Penderyn distillery unique is a so-called Faraday copper still, which is used in the production of the whisky. Matured in ex-bourbon casks and later in Madeira casks, the whisky is bottled in really chic bottles.

tasting_notes.nose: The single malt opens fresh and fruity with a pleasant sweetness. We think of a pear tree that bears pears that are not quite ripe yet. Excitingly, there is a slightly bitter note in the background - could be something like pineapple. This is accompanied by freshly cut wood.
tasting_notes.palate: A vineyard - green leaves, ripe green grapes and a bright blue sky with sunshine. Later we go into the wine cellar, slightly musty with lots of wood and light leather. And suddenly a sharp fruit brandy appears. This is roughly how the taste of the distillate from Wales, probably matured for 3-4 years, can be described.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is short and crisp slightly peppery, later more balanced and fruity.

conclusion.marcel: The Penderyn Madeira Finish presents itself as a pleasant, fruity companion with a charming touch of acidity – ideal for relaxed evenings. However, the young maturation still feels a bit raw, which slightly detracts from the drinking pleasure. All in all, a likeable, straightforward whisky with character and natural freshness.
conclusion.sascha: A structured single malt with clear fruitiness and a light acidity, which, however, shows a certain imbalance due to its short maturation period. The 46% ABV and the absence of chill filtration speak for quality, yet the alcoholic sharpness and the short finish suggest there is room for further development. For lovers of light, fruity whiskies, an interesting, if not perfect, choice.

faq.question: What is special about the Faraday copper still at Penderyn?
faq.answer: The Faraday copper still is a special still used at the Penderyn distillery that ensures an exceptionally pure and fruity distillation. It differs from conventional stills through its unique design, which makes the distillation process more efficient.

faq.question: How does the Madeira cask maturation influence the taste of the Penderyn Madeira Finish?
faq.answer: Maturation in Madeira casks imparts fruity and slightly tart notes reminiscent of green grapes and pineapple. This cask aging complements the fresh sweetness and wood character of the ex-bourbon cask, resulting in a complex, lively flavor.

faq.question: Why does the Penderyn Madeira Finish have no age statement on the bottle?
faq.answer: The Penderyn Madeira Finish is bottled as a No-Age-Statement (NAS) whisky, meaning no minimum age is indicated. This allows the distillery to blend different vintages and casks to achieve a consistent and balanced flavor.

item.name: Redbreast 12 Years Cask Strength (2022)
item.description: The Midleton distillery's 12-years Redbreast bottling is bottled annually in cask strength batches in addition to the 40% strength bottling. The cask strength bottling is also triple distilled in copper pot stills and matured in a combination of bourbon matured American oak casks and oloroso sherry matured Spanish oak casks before being bottled with no added colouring and non-chill filtered. Did you know? The robin is one of the few small birds that also winters in Ireland. The bird species is the eponym for this Irish whiskey.

tasting_notes.nose: Wow! Dried fruit, chocolate and vanilla opens up a manifold world of flavours. It feels like every twist of the whiskey is unique. Once we perceive dominant citrus aromas, the second time roasted aromas, light oak and some caramel. A little later, a rich fruit basket follows. Suddenly, some banana comes through, here and there a drop of honey and a gentle hint of cinnamon. The whole thing reminds us of a 5-star hotel breakfast with a gigantic selection.
tasting_notes.palate: Incredibly rich, the Single Pot Still has a lot to offer and the image of a hotel breakfast also fits here. The taste is dominated by cinnamon buns and crepes with cinnamon sugar. They are joined by hay, a little toasted white bread, nutmeg and a super exciting fruitiness. We also find honey. Almonds, Brazil nuts, vanilla and light oak spice round off the velvety smooth taste experience. The approx. 60% ABV is perfectly integrated, so that the alcohol as a flavour carrier brings out and presents the flavours cleanly.
tasting_notes.finish: The long finish of the Redbreast 12 CS is dominated by roasted dates, hay, some milk chocolate, subliminal spicy oak and dried fruit.

conclusion.marcel: This Redbreast 12 Year Cask Strength is a genuine taste experience that’s a lot of fun and delights with its rich variety of aromas. For under 70 euros, you get a true Irish whiskey gem that turns every sip into a little celebration – a must-have for connoisseurs who love character.
conclusion.sascha: With its balanced interplay of cask strength, fruitiness, and spice, this Redbreast 12 CS demonstrates impressive balance and complexity. Despite the intense 58.1% alcohol, it feels structured and clean, though it falls just a bit short compared to the absolute top tier.

faq.question: What does 'Cask Strength' mean for the Redbreast 12 Year Cask Strength (2022)?
faq.answer: 'Cask Strength' means the whisky is bottled at barrel strength, without dilution to the usual drinking strength. The Redbreast 12 CS therefore has a higher alcohol content of around 58.1%, which makes the aromas more intense and concentrated.

faq.question: How does the combination of bourbon and Oloroso sherry casks influence the flavor of this whiskey?
faq.answer: Maturation in American bourbon casks imparts vanilla and caramel notes to the whisky, while the Spanish Oloroso sherry casks contribute dried fruit, nuttiness, and a spicy complexity. Together, they create the rich and layered flavor profile of the Redbreast 12 CS.

faq.question: Why is the Redbreast 12 Year Cask Strength bottled without chill filtration and without added coloring?
faq.answer: Bottling without chill filtration and without added coloring preserves the natural oils and aromas of the whiskey. This results in greater flavor depth and an authentic drinking experience, as is typical for high-quality single pot still whiskeys.

item.name: Glendronach The Hielan 8 Years
item.description: Since 2015, The Hielan 8 Years has been the entry into the standard range of single malt whiskies from the Glendronach distillery. By the way: Hielan is a Scottish word that refers to the mountainous part of Scotland that lies to the north and west of a line that runs roughly from Dumbarton via Ballater and from there to Nairn.

tasting_notes.nose: The single malt is very expressive on the nose. The main notes are lemon peel and plum, as well as a little woody-woody-wood. In addition, a nice butter toast, cane sugar and a strong vanilla can be found.
tasting_notes.palate: The Highlander opens really, really velvety soft. Followed by powerful oak paired with orange marmalade and sandalwood, the Scotch transitions into a nuttiness (almonds, hazelnuts) before the development finds its conclusion in soft sugar biscuits. Fabulously delicious!
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is medium long and warming with spicy oak and a touch of maltiness.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that’s simply a joy – velvety smooth, with sweet vanilla notes and a pleasant fruitiness that invites you to take another sip. Glendronach The Hielan 8 Years is the perfect companion for relaxed evenings, offering a charming glimpse into the Highlands. The price-performance ratio is spot on, so you really can’t go wrong here.
conclusion.sascha: This 8-years Highland single malt presents itself well-balanced, with a clear structure of oak, citrus notes, and nutty accents. The combination of ex-bourbon and ex-sherry cask maturation creates a harmonious interplay of sweetness and spice that appeals to both newcomers and seasoned connoisseurs. A solid whisky with precise aromatics and a medium-length, warm finish.

faq.question: How does the Glendronach The Hielan 8 Years differ from older releases from the distillery?
faq.answer: The The Hielan 8 Years is the youngest standard single malt from Glendronach since 2015 and is characterized by a fresh combination of citrus notes, plum, and vanilla, whereas older releases often display more complex, mature aromas.

faq.question: What types of casks were used for aging the Glendronach The Hielan 8 Years?
faq.answer: The The Hielan 8 Years matures mainly in Oloroso sherry casks, which impart its characteristic spiciness, vanilla, and nutty notes typical of Glendronach whiskies.

faq.question: Which foods pair well with Glendronach The Hielan 8 Years?
faq.answer: Thanks to its velvety texture and notes of orange peel, nuts, and spices, this whisky pairs well with aged cheese, dark chocolate, or hearty dishes such as lamb or game.

item.name: Laphroaig Brodir Final Batch
item.description: The Laphroaig Brodir Final Batch is a limited edition, named after the Viking term for "brother", and is the final batch in a series of special bottlings honouring the distillery's heritage and connection to the island's Viking history.After several batches, this Laphroaig, matured in ex-bourbon casks and finished in a port cask, is now discontinued. The Scotch does not bear an age statement.

tasting_notes.nose: The Laphroaig Brodir Final Batch has a complex and layered aroma, with Laphroaig's characteristic phenolic and smoky notes present from the first moment. There is also a slight saltiness, followed by fruit and citrus notes, as well as hints of plums and custard. The aroma is also spicy, with hints of churros and cinnamon sugar.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the single malt is bold and full-bodied, with dominant seaweed and iodine notes and a seabed feel. Added to this are flavours of dark fruits, including intense plums and berries, as well as a pleasant and rich smoke. The profile is also rounded off by a spicy honey sweetness.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the Brodir is expectedly long and smoky, with a lingering fruity sweetness.

conclusion.marcel: A true Islay powerhouse bursting with character and a wonderful blend of smoke, spice, and fruity sweetness – simply a delight for anyone who appreciates the classic Laphroaig style. If you manage to snag a bottle at a fair price, don’t hesitate, as these limited editions sell out quickly!
conclusion.sascha: The Laphroaig Brodir Final Batch impresses with its balanced structure and the harmonious interplay of smoke, maritime notes, and fruity accents. Despite its complexity, the finish feels somewhat one-dimensional, yet it remains a successful interpretation of the peaty Islay profile in a limited release.

faq.question: What does the name “Brodir” mean in Laphroaig Brodir Final Batch?
faq.answer: “Brodir” is an Old Norse term meaning “brother.” The name pays tribute to Islay’s Viking heritage and symbolizes the connection between the distillery and its history.

faq.question: How does the port cask finish influence the flavor of this Laphroaig?
faq.answer: The port cask finish imparts an additional fruity sweetness and depth to the Laphroaig Brodir Final Batch, reflected in aromas of plums and berries that elegantly complement the signature smoke.

faq.question: Why does the Laphroaig Brodir Final Batch have no age statement?
faq.answer: The Brodir Final Batch is a no-age-statement (NAS) whisky, focusing on quality and flavor profile. The distillery combines various casks and maturation periods to achieve a balanced and complex aroma.

item.name: The Black Grouse Alpha Edition
item.description: The Black Grouse Alpha Edition is a peated version of the Famous Grouse. As it is a blend, the barley used specifically for this whisky was not peated, as is the case with a single malt. Instead, they took fully matured Islay whisky and blended it with the standard Famous Grouse. We suspect it will be Caol Ila, as is so often the case.

tasting_notes.nose: The blend opens very earthy with notes reminiscent of musty wet forest soil. A bit as if it had rained for a week after a forest fire. Something sour resonates, a little orange and here and there a little malt.
tasting_notes.palate: Surprisingly little smoke, correspondingly probably a small Islay whisky share. Surprisingly sweet and cerealy, almost bourbon-like notes of caramel and vanilla with bitter orange and roasted grain.
tasting_notes.finish: The mild finish is short and malty with charcoal and some caramel.

conclusion.marcel: The Black Grouse Alpha Edition isn’t a dramatic standout, but with its sweet notes and earthy forest character, it certainly has its own charm. It’s enough for a little venture into the peated world, though it doesn’t really knock your socks off – more of a solid, straightforward blend for casual sipping.
conclusion.sascha: The Alpha Edition shows an interesting balance between mild, sweet bourbon notes and subtle hints of smoke, yet structurally it remains rather simple and not very complex. For the price, there are definitely better-balanced alternatives in the blended whisky segment with a clearer profile.

faq.question: Why is the Black Grouse Alpha Edition less smoky than typical Islay whiskies despite being peated?
faq.answer: The Black Grouse Alpha Edition contains only a small proportion of Islay whisky, likely Caol Ila, which is fully matured. The barley used for the blend is unpeated, which means the smoky character in the flavor is much more restrained compared to single malts from Islay.

faq.question: What role does the combination of matured Islay whisky and Famous Grouse play in the flavor of this blend?
faq.answer: The combination creates a balance between earthy, lightly smoky notes and sweet, grainy aromas like caramel and vanilla. This results in a mild, complex blend with hints of damp forest floor and a bourbon-like sweetness.

faq.question: How does the Black Grouse Alpha Edition differ from a classic single malt in terms of production?
faq.answer: Unlike a single malt, the Black Grouse Alpha Edition is a blend where the barley is not peated. Instead, fully matured Islay whisky is mixed with the standard Famous Grouse, leading to a different flavor profile than a typical peated single malt.

item.name: Tomintoul 16 Years
item.description: Tomintoul's single malts are known to be among the lighter, smoother - and, as some would say, old-fashioned and boring - Speyside malts. Perhaps because of this and the high demand for intense and powerful Scotches, the distillery flies under the radar of most people.

tasting_notes.nose: Well-ordered, orange, hazelnut and dried fruit are found as base aromas. In the background, malty-grainy and slightly floral (daisies?) notes are distributed. We also find some bitter orange, honey and oats, which are infused and character-building.
tasting_notes.palate: The goal is quite clear. This whisky from the Speyside wants to be soft and velvety. Buttercrossaint and toasted sesame, as well as light citrus notes we find alongside a little toasted oak, marzipan (almonds) and walnut nuttiness. A little white pepper here and there, and that's it.
tasting_notes.finish: This malt finishes medium-bodied, clean and warming, with almond, oat and oak tones.

conclusion.marcel: The Tomintoul 16 Years presents itself as pleasantly restrained and smooth, ideal for those who prefer no wild experiments. It scores with light nutty and fruity notes but overall remains a bit too tame and generic to truly excite – yet it’s still enjoyable in the glass.
conclusion.sascha: The 16-years release from Tomintoul is balanced and cleanly structured, without any sharp edges or roughness. For lovers of light Speyside malts, the balance is well achieved, but the profile remains conventional and lacks distinctive character, making this whisky more suited for mass consumption than for connoisseurs.

faq.question: How does the Tomintoul 16 Years differ from other Speyside whiskies?
faq.answer: The Tomintoul 16 Years is an especially light and gentle Speyside malt that relies less on intense smoke or peat notes. Instead, fruity and nutty aromas dominate, making it more accessible and softer than many other whiskies from the region.

faq.question: What aromas can I expect when tasting the Tomintoul 16 Years?
faq.answer: On the nose, you’ll find notes of orange, hazelnut, and dried fruits, complemented by malty and floral nuances. On the palate, it is soft with flavors of butter croissant, toasted sesame, citrus, marzipan, and walnuts, rounded off by a subtle spice of white pepper.

faq.question: Is the Tomintoul 16 Years suitable for mixing cocktails?
faq.answer: Due to its mild and velvety character, the Tomintoul 16 Years is better suited for neat enjoyment or light whisky cocktails that don’t overpower its delicate flavors. It is less suitable for strong or smoky drinks.

item.name: Blair Athol 10 Years (2007/2019) - Douglas Laing
item.description: Although Blair Athol is not a small distillery, its Single Malts are not among the best known. Over 90% of the production goes into Bell's blended Scotch. Today we have a 10-years independent bottling of a single Blair Athol cask from Douglas Laing's Provenance series in the glass.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, the Single Malt is soft and sweet. We think of freshly baked butter cake. This is joined by notes of vanilla, honey and oats. The aroma is rounded off by a pleasant maltiness complemented by cereals.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the Blair Athol is not as soft as the nose suggested. Caramel toffee, honey and vanilla are joined by surprisingly spicy flavours. Intense oak leaves a distinct tingle on the tongue despite the creamy mouthfeel. Despite its age, the spiciness verges on light ethanol pepperiness.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish is also dominated by toasted oak spice. This leaves a clear bitterness on the palate. Behind this, there is still some grain and hints of dark chocolate.

conclusion.marcel: This Blair Athol is quite enjoyable, especially if you appreciate the sweet notes of butter cake and honey. Unfortunately, it struggles a bit with bitterness and doesn’t feel entirely balanced – yet it’s definitely worth a try for a quick tasting.
conclusion.sascha: The balance between sweet and spicy elements still feels underdeveloped here, causing the whisky’s overall structure to seem somewhat uneven. Despite interesting aromas, the integration of oak spice and bitterness leaves room for improvement.

faq.question: Why is the Blair Athol 10 Years 2007/2019 from Douglas Laing particularly interesting for whisky connoisseurs?
faq.answer: This bottling comes from a single cask at the Blair Athol distillery, independently selected by Douglas Laing. As a result, it offers an unadulterated flavor profile that stands apart from the usual standard releases, highlighting the distillery’s characteristic maltiness and oak spice.

faq.question: How does the taste of Blair Athol 10 Years differ from other Highland whiskies?
faq.answer: The Blair Athol 10 Years combines a sweet nose with notes of butter cake, vanilla, and honey, while the palate surprises with spiciness and intense oak influence. Its creamy mouthfeel and bitter oak spice on the finish set it apart from many other Highland whiskies, which tend to be milder and fruitier.

faq.question: What role does the 46% alcohol content play in this bottling?
faq.answer: At 46% ABV, the whisky sits slightly above the usual standard strength of 40-43%. This provides a more intense aroma and pronounced spiciness without sacrificing balance. The subtle ethanol warmth supports the complex flavor notes without becoming overpowering.

item.name: Glenglassaugh 8 Years (2013/2022) - The Cask Wizard
item.description: Matured in port casks and finished in Amarone casks, this 8-years single malt whisky from the Glenglassaugh distillery in Scotland was bottled by the German independent bottler "The Cask Wizard" at cask strength, uncoloured and unfiltered. As there are only 63 bottles, we assume that a barrel-share was bottled here, but we are still very happy to own one of the few bottles.

tasting_notes.nose: The nose opens with a caramel cherry-berry mix. Sweetish, like sugar syrup, wine gums and a little apple lie on the surface. Digging a little deeper, we find herbal and floral notes that make us think of buttercup and sage. Hazelnut is also present. Versatile and exciting!
tasting_notes.palate: Matching the nose, we also find caramel and wine gum in the taste. However, the initial sweetness then splits into two directions. On the outside, we find slightly peppery notes that linger for a long time. Further inside, there is hazelnut again, accompanied by almonds and a dry, herbal green tea note.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish is distinctly more astringent with an oaky spiciness and a special note reminiscent of wet driftwood.

conclusion.marcel: A true delight that impresses with its surprisingly complex aroma profile and characterful finish – simply delicious and full of drinking pleasure. For all lovers seeking something special, this Glenglassaugh is a little treasure chest, even though the bottles are rare. Hopefully, more releases from The Cask Wizard will follow soon, because this one definitely leaves you wanting more!
conclusion.sascha: The Glenglassaugh 8 Years from The Cask Wizard demonstrates an impressive balance between sweet fruit aromas and a tart, spicy structure in the finish. Despite its young age, the complexity of the port and Amarone cask finish shines through, with the wet driftwood note on the finish adding an interesting, though not universally appealing, touch. Overall, a solid cask strength bottling with potential that rightly puts the spotlight on the Glenglassaugh distillery.

faq.question: How does the Amarone cask finish influence the flavor of this Glenglassaugh whisky?
faq.answer: The Amarone cask finish imparts additional fruity and spicy notes reminiscent of cherries, berries, and a subtle wine sweetness. This combination complements the caramel and nutty aromas, making the whisky more complex and multi-layered.

faq.question: Why is this Glenglassaugh 8 Years 2013/2022 bottled only at cask strength?
faq.answer: Bottling at cask strength (47.3% ABV) allows the whisky to be enjoyed in its purest form and full flavor intensity. Additionally, it was bottled without coloring and without chill filtration to preserve the natural aromas and characteristics as best as possible.

faq.question: What significance does the limited number of only 63 bottles have for collectors and enthusiasts?
faq.answer: The limited release of just 63 bottles makes this whisky particularly rare and sought after. For collectors, it represents a unique value, while enthusiasts receive an exclusive dram likely drawn from individual cask portions, showcasing a distinctive character.

item.name: The Balvenie 15 Years Madeira Cask
item.description: The Balvenie 15 Years Madeira Cask is a Travel Exclusive bottling from the distillery located in Speyside, Scotland. Not much is really known about the bottling - there was no press release or anything like that. The bottles just turned up. Most of the time, the single malt was matured in ex-bourbon casks before it was decanted into Madeira casks from the Portuguese sunny island of the same name for the finish.

tasting_notes.nose: We immediately smell a wild mix of nuts. Sweetish and with a slight gummy bear aroma, a combination of various berries follows, like those found in the frozen berry mix, i.e. cherries, currants, raspberries. A gentle vanilla-cinnamon-floral-honey jumble lies over everything. Very very pleasant.
tasting_notes.palate: The Speysider single malt opens just as nutty as the nose. More nuts follow with malty intense oak spice, a little bitter orange marmalade paired with ginger and a pinch of pepper. Later, a fruitiness of stone fruit comes through - we're thinking flat peaches - which integrates beautifully with yet more oak and cinnamon. All in all, a good sweet and sour combination.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the scotch is medium long, slightly spicy, slightly dusty and of course nutty.

conclusion.marcel: A genuine Speyside delight with plenty of nuttiness and a fruity explosion of berries – perfect for those who enjoy it sweet and spicy. Sure, the oak spice puts on the brakes a bit, but that doesn’t make it any less likeable. For a travel release, it’s a solid companion that’s enjoyable without being overwhelming.
conclusion.sascha: Structurally, the Balvenie 15 Year Madeira Cask shows a balanced interplay between nutty notes and fruity accents, although the dominant oak spice somewhat overpowers the flavor. The medium-length spiciness on the finish adds complexity, yet it lacks a clear focus to truly stand out. A decent, but not exceptional, travel exclusive single malt from Speyside.

faq.question: How does the Madeira cask finish influence the flavor of The Balvenie 15 Year Madeira Cask?
faq.answer: The Madeira cask finish imparts a fruity sweetness with notes of berries and stone fruit, along with a subtle spicy element that complements the whisky’s nutty and vanilla profile.

faq.question: Why is The Balvenie 15 Year Madeira Cask available as a Travel Exclusive?
faq.answer: This bottling was produced exclusively for the travel retail market and is therefore typically only available in duty-free shops at airports or on cruises, which limits its availability.

faq.question: What role does maturation in bourbon casks play before the Madeira finish?
faq.answer: The extended maturation in bourbon casks provides the whisky’s characteristic vanilla and oak spice, which is then enhanced by the subsequent Madeira finish with fruity and spicy aromas.

item.name: Jack Daniel's Single Barrel Select
item.description: Everyone knows the supermarket Jack Daniel's Old No. 7, but if you look a little further you will find a number of exciting bottlings from the same distillery. Besides the Single Barrel Select, which we have in the glass today, there are other Single Barrel bottlings, Bonded, Triple Mash and many more.The Single Barrel Select is the entry into the Jack Daniel's distillery bottlings that whisky lovers can take seriously and also enjoy neat.

tasting_notes.nose: If anyone has ever wondered whether whiskey can smell intensely of banana. The answer is clearly YES! And that is sweet, ripe bananas. Somewhere in there are a few other sweet fruity notes, a good portion of oak, and a hint of herbs. And banana. More banana. Banana anyone?
tasting_notes.palate: Banana? Yes, here too, and not too little. In addition, we taste a lot of vanilla, a little caramel, as well as the spicy oak that we could already perceive in the nose. And banana! Maybe a little leather and a little lactritz.
tasting_notes.finish: Slightly dry and yet fruity sweet, the Jack Daniel's closes with a medium-long finish.

conclusion.marcel: If you love banana notes in whisky, you’ll have a lot of fun here – it’s almost a fruit bomb! Still, I miss a bit of depth and character to truly celebrate this Single Barrel. For a quick sip on the go, though, it’s perfectly fine.
conclusion.sascha: This bottling shows a decent balance between sweet fruit notes and spicy oak, though the dominant banana aromas won’t be to everyone’s taste. Structurally solid, but it lacks the complexity and narrative arc to be considered a true Single Barrel classic.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Jack Daniel's Single Barrel Select from the classic Old No. 7?
faq.answer: The Single Barrel Select is bottled from individual, handpicked barrels, giving each bottle a unique flavor nuance. It also has a higher alcohol content of 45% and offers more intense aromas—especially banana, vanilla, and oak—compared to the milder Old No. 7.

faq.question: Why does the Jack Daniel's Single Barrel Select taste so strongly of banana?
faq.answer: The banana notes come from maturation in new American white oak barrels and the special yeast used during fermentation. This combination produces sweet, fruity ester compounds that define the characteristic banana aroma.

faq.question: What is the best way to enjoy Jack Daniel's Single Barrel Select?
faq.answer: The Single Barrel Select is excellent enjoyed neat to fully experience its complex aromas. Adding a drop of water can open up further nuances. Ice or mixers might mask the delicate fruit and wood notes.

item.name: Bruichladdich The Classic Laddie
item.description: Islay? Obviously peated Single Malt. At least that was the expectation when we first heard about Bruichladdich The Classic Laddie. It turned out to be just the opposite. After we had entered the batch code of the bottle on the Bruichladdich homepage - thanks to transparency - we received a list of detailed information about the casks used, the barley processed and the year of bottling. Even though the whisky comes without an age statement, this can be roughly calculated.

tasting_notes.nose: The number of different casks involved is immediately noticeable. The Scotch releases aromas of sugar syrup and caramel paired with vanilla, honey and white chocolate. Freshly mown grass, further floral notes and spicy oak notes resonate. Here and there, dark berries can be sensed.
tasting_notes.palate: Spicy, vanilla, sweet with a bit of nuttiness. The three-stage development of the Classic Laddie is interesting to observe. In the first stage, vanilla, brown sugar and honey spread out. The second stage moves into Brazil nuts and walnuts, lightly salted with a little crispbread and crunchy cereals. Finally, in the third stage, there is a bit of bitterness that we can't quite put our finger on - perhaps red wine.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish also develops in the throat. Beginning in the sweetish area, it turns into a spicy-soft combination to finally flash lightly fruity nuances.

conclusion.marcel: The Classic Laddie surprises with its elegant sweetness and multifaceted character – not your typical Islay punch, but a genuine pleasure for those who appreciate harmony and honesty. Its transparency and fair price make it the perfect everyday companion that leaves you wanting more.
conclusion.sascha: A well-balanced, versatile whisky that shines through its transparent cask selection and clear structure. Despite lacking an age statement, it impresses with a successful balance of sweetness, spice, and subtle bitterness – technically flawless, though without any major surprises.

faq.question: Why is Bruichladdich The Classic Laddie not smoky despite its Islay origin?
faq.answer: Bruichladdich The Classic Laddie uses unpeated barley, which is why it shows hardly any smoke or peat notes. Unlike many other Islay whiskies, the focus here is on fruity, floral, and sweet aromas.

faq.question: How can the age of Bruichladdich The Classic Laddie be estimated without an age statement?
faq.answer: Although there is no age statement, by entering the batch code on Bruichladdich’s website, you can find details about the casks used and the bottling year, which allows for an approximate age determination.

faq.question: What flavor development characterizes Bruichladdich The Classic Laddie?
faq.answer: The whisky exhibits a three-stage flavor development: first sweet notes of vanilla and honey, then nutty and slightly salty aromas reminiscent of crunchy muesli, followed by a subtle bitterness that can evoke red wine.

item.name: Glen Keith 21 Years
item.description: The Glen Keith 21 Years is a single malt Scotch whisky that is matured exclusively in oak casks. It is produced by the Glen Keith Distillery, which has been located in the Speyside region of Scotland since 1957.The single malt, which has been matured for more than two decades, comes in the typical packaging style of the distillery.

tasting_notes.nose: On opening the bottle, the aroma of Glen Keith 21 Years is intense and fruity, with strong notes of peach and apricot. The whisky has a velvety smooth smell with sweet vanillin and marzipan notes. There is also a slight oak note that adds depth and complexity to the aroma.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, the Scotch has a rich and creamy mouthfeel. The predominant flavour is marzipan, with hints of green apple and other light fruit notes. There is also a slight toffee flavour as well as a honey sweetness.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long and sweet, with a strong presence of oak. The oak flavours add depth and complexity to the whisky and balance the sweetness and fruitiness of the other flavours.

conclusion.marcel: The Glen Keith 21 is a true whisky for the pleasure-seeker: soft, fruity, and with an enticing sweetness that leaves you wanting more. It wraps you in marzipan and peach without ever becoming overly complicated – perfect for relaxed evenings with character!
conclusion.sascha: Technically clean and well-balanced, the Glen Keith 21 presents itself as a balanced Speyside whisky with a clear oak structure and a harmonious blend of fruit and vanilla notes. While it lacks a bit of complexity, its solid maturation and coherent mouthfeel make it a reliable choice for lovers of refined single malts.

faq.question: How does the 21-year maturation in oak casks influence the taste of the Glen Keith 21 years?
faq.answer: The long maturation in oak casks imparts a complex aroma profile with intense notes of vanillin, marzipan, and a pronounced oak character. This gives the whisky depth, balance, and a long-lasting, sweet finish.

faq.question: What characteristic aromas can one expect in the Glen Keith 21 years?
faq.answer: The Glen Keith 21 years presents fruity notes of peach and apricot on the nose, complemented by sweet hints of vanillin and marzipan. On the palate, marzipan, green apple, toffee, and a subtle honeyed sweetness dominate.

faq.question: How does the Glen Keith 21 years differ from younger Glen Keith releases?
faq.answer: Compared to younger expressions, the 21-years Glen Keith shows a noticeably more complex and deeper aroma profile, mainly due to the extended cask maturation. The oak notes are more pronounced, leading to a richer mouthfeel and a longer, sweet finish.

item.name: Macduff 20 Years - The Observatory
item.description: The Observatory 20 years is from the signature series of the bottler of the same name. Belonging to the Bacardi Group, this Single Grain Scotch whisky was created by the master blender of the Aberfeldy distillery Stephanie Macleod.It is believed that the single grain was distilled at the MacDuff distillery in the Scottish Highlands near the lighthouse in Banff Bay. The whisky was stored for 20 full years in ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks. The long storage period is said to make the whisky particularly soft and pleasantly complex.

tasting_notes.nose: A very sweet and vanilla note greets the nose. Later, lemon curd and spicy oak follow in the background. Surprisingly, we also perceive a sharp ethanol note. Slightly sour aromas support the composition, which is reminiscent of a sponge cake roll.
tasting_notes.palate: Foam sugar and marshmallows dominate the taste. Rounded off by a present oaky component, the Single Grain is very delicate. The whole thing is sweetened by a fair amount of forest honey.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish is vanilla, spicy and slightly bitter.

conclusion.marcel: The Macduff 20 Year pleasantly surprises with its sweet vanilla note and playful marshmallow flavor – a delight that’s fun to drink, though it lacks a bit of depth. For under 20 euros, it’s a charming companion offering straightforward drinking pleasure without being overly complex.
conclusion.sascha: A well-structured single grain with a clear balance between sweet vanilla tones, spicy oak, and a slight bitterness on the finish. The 20 years of maturation are evident, yet in terms of complexity and smoothness, the Macduff 20 Year could have shown a bit more character.

faq.question: What distinguishes a single grain whisky like the Macduff 20 Year from single malt whiskies?
faq.answer: Single grain whiskies are typically made from various grains alongside barley and are distilled in continuous stills, resulting in a lighter and often softer character. In contrast, single malt whisky is made exclusively from malted barley and distilled in pot stills, producing more intense aromas.

faq.question: How does maturation in ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks influence the flavor of this whisky?
faq.answer: The combination of ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks imparts a balanced complexity: bourbon casks contribute vanilla and sweet notes, while sherry casks add spicy, fruity, and nutty aromas. Together, they create the soft and layered flavor profile of the Macduff 20 Year.

faq.question: Why is there a sharp ethanol note on the nose of the Macduff 20 Year despite its 20 years of aging?
faq.answer: Despite the long maturation, single grain whiskies bottled at 40% ABV can sometimes exhibit a slight ethanol sharpness, especially if not bottled at a higher strength. This sharpness often reflects the distillation method and the grain mix used.

item.name: Speyburn 18 Years
item.description: The Speyburn 18 year is currently the oldest standard bottling from Speyburn, located in the Speyside region of Scotland. As one of the smallest distilleries in Scotland, the bottlings fly under the radar for many. That should change.This year, the Speyburn distillery celebrates its 125th anniversary. Instead of an anniversary bottling, the website features some cocktail recipes specifically for Speyburn whisky. We just hope that no one sinks the multi-award-winning 18-years single malt in a cocktail.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, Speyburn 18 presents strong aromas of raisins, leather, and sugar, as well as dried plums and nutty notes. The whisky has a full-bodied and dark character, with an intense oak aroma that balances out the sweeter notes. There is also a hint of citrus and dark chocolate, giving it a slightly similar profile to Schwarzwälder Kirsch without the cream.
tasting_notes.palate: It is not as sweet as one might expect based on its nose. Instead, it has an intense sherry aroma and a clear oak spice that dominates the flavor profile. There are also subtle citrus notes present, which add a touch of freshness and balance out the sweetness of the sherry and the spiciness of the oak.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long and slightly bitter, with a lasting sweetness. The bitterness adds depth and complexity, while the sweetness adds a touch of balance and roundness.

conclusion.marcel: The Speyburn 18 is a charming companion for connoisseurs who have a penchant for classic sherry notes – full-bodied, intense, and with a touch of sweet depth that leaves you wanting more. While it lacks that certain something to become a true classic, it brings plenty of enjoyment as a reliable everyday whisky and adds character to the glass.
conclusion.sascha: With its balanced interplay of intense oak spice, subtle citrus hints, and a long-lasting, slightly bitter finish, the Speyburn 18 presents itself as a technically well-structured single malt. Despite a lack of innovation, it impresses with a complex flavor profile and solid maturity, appealing especially to lovers of traditional Speyside sherry whiskies.

faq.question: How does the Speyburn 18 Years differ from younger releases from the distillery?
faq.answer: The Speyburn 18 Years is distinguished by its full-bodied, dark character with intense aromas of sherry, oak, and dried fruits, which are usually less pronounced in younger releases. The extended maturation adds complexity, depth, and a long-lasting, slightly bitter finish.

faq.question: Why shouldn’t you use the Speyburn 18 Years in cocktails?
faq.answer: The Speyburn 18 Years is a multi-award-winning single malt with complex aromas such as raisins, leather, and dark chocolate. These delicate nuances often get lost in cocktails, so it’s better enjoyed neat or with a few drops of water to fully experience its flavor profile.

faq.question: How does the Speyside location influence the flavor notes of the Speyburn 18 Years?
faq.answer: The Speyside region is known for rather fruity and sweet whiskies with elegant sherry and oak aromas. The Speyburn 18 Years showcases this typical balance with a combination of sweet raisin and plum notes alongside a pronounced oak spiciness characteristic of the region.

item.name: Patrisbrunna X-Mas Port (2016) Double Cask
item.description: KÖ13 in Paderborn is actually known for its large selection of food and delicatessen. The restaurant also sells a small number and especially some of its own single malts. Today we have a whisky from Paderborn in our glass.The whisky from Paderborn was first matured for two years in an ex-bourbon cask and for another two years in an ex-port wine cask. We are impressed by the deep, strong red colour.

tasting_notes.nose: A winey-sweet scent of red fruits, such as currants and blackberries, is in the air. Accompanied by malt and spicy oak, we perceive something that reminds us of cherry nectar.
tasting_notes.palate: The single malt occupies the tongue, but is less full-bodied than the nose would lead one to expect. A good portion of tannins accompanies a sour-bitter fruit note. Interestingly, there is still a malty sweetness lying on top of everything.
tasting_notes.finish: The short finish is slightly oaky-spicy and reminiscent of tobacco leaves.

conclusion.marcel: This deep red beauty from Paderborn delivers genuine drinking pleasure with its fruity aroma and malty sweetness, making it perfect for the Christmas season – a dessert whisky that lifts the spirits, even though it still has some room to grow in its fullness.
conclusion.sascha: With a clear structure and a harmonious interplay of tannins and malty sweetness, this four-years Port Cask matured single malt from Germany shows solid balance, yet the finish feels a bit short and could benefit from greater complexity.

faq.question: How do the two different cask types influence the flavor of the Patrisbrunna X-Mas Port 2016 Double Cask?
faq.answer: The whisky spent its first two years maturing in an ex-bourbon cask, which imparts malty and vanilla notes. The subsequent two-year aging in an ex-port wine cask brings fruity, tannin-rich aromas like currants and blackberries, as well as a vivid red color.

faq.question: Why does the Patrisbrunna X-Mas Port 2016 Double Cask have such a complex aroma despite its young age of four years?
faq.answer: The combination of two different cask types and careful maturation in an independent bottling allows for a multi-layered aromatic profile. The ex-port wine cask especially contributes to the fruity acidity and tannin structure, qualities often found only in older whiskies.

faq.question: What special characteristics should one pay attention to when tasting this German single malt?
faq.answer: Besides the intense red color, the balance between sweet malt notes and the sour-bitter fruity acidity stands out. The whisky is less full-bodied than expected, with tannins dominating alongside a spicy, slightly tobacco-like finish.

item.name: Aultmore 13 Years - 82 Chapters to Newcastle
item.description: In addition to the three distillery standards - 12 years, 18 years and 21 years - Aultmore from Scotland's Speyside has a number of bottlings from independent bottlers, such as the Aultmore 13 Years Red Wine Barrique from 82 Chapters to Newcastle's Creatures of Cask Strengt series.Matured for 13 years from a single red wine barrel, this single malt is striking for its intense colour. Among bottlers, both red wine casks and rum casks are considered to be the cask types with the greatest risks as far as the quality of the final result is concerned. Minor spoiler: Everything worked out here.

tasting_notes.nose: Unexpectedly, we first perceive pear, closely followed by a combination of cherries, currants, dates and plums. The heavy aroma is accompanied by Christmas spices such as cinnamon, nutmeg and a hint of clove. The composition is rounded off by a certain oak spiciness.
tasting_notes.palate: Whew! This is sweet. If we had expected a slightly sour base, we are surprised by a decent fruit sweetness with a combination of sugar syrup and wild berries, which, however, later turns into a sour-grapey and very mouthwatering note. The oily single malt also impresses in the mouth with Christmas spices, dates and plums. The 57.4% abv. are very well incorporated, yet the Scotch could do with a few drops of water to open up the flavours further. All in all harmonious, multi-layered and pleasant, even if surprisingly sweet.
tasting_notes.finish: Long and dry, the Aultmore from the red wine cask remains with wild berries, dark chocolate and an underlying oak spiciness.

conclusion.marcel: Awesome. This bottling is perfect, especially during the Christmas season. The profile of the Aultmore distillery weaves beautifully with the flavours from the red wine cask to create an all-round harmonious bottling, of which sadly there are only 257 bottles. Must have. There is nothing more to say here.
conclusion.sascha: Awesome. This bottling is perfect, especially during the Christmas season. The profile of the Aultmore distillery weaves beautifully with the flavours from the red wine cask to create an all-round harmonious bottling, of which sadly there are only 257 bottles. Must have. There is nothing more to say here.

item.name: Patrisbrunna Original Paderborner Single Malt (2016)
item.description: KÖ13 in Paderborn is actually known for its delicious food. However, the restaurant also sells luxury condiments and, in particular, its own single malt. Today we have a whisky from Paderborn in our glass.Locality and transparency are at the forefront of Patrisbrunna. Local barley and spring water are brewed with an original wort of 15.2° and distilled four times. It was bottled after about 5 years of ageing.

tasting_notes.nose: In the opening we find spicy floral notes with a hint of mown hay. There is also some solvent. Behind this, roasted aromas that almost make us think of freshly grilled food. With a little water added, the aroma opens up to blossom honey and sweet vanilla.
tasting_notes.palate: At first, we perceive intense vanilla sweetness. The malt is full-bodied, warming and soft. Malty, cereal-like aromas remind us of crunchy breakfast cereals. However, we also find youthful spiciness, which was to be expected. With water, the taste becomes much rounder. We find roasted grain and a good portion of oak again.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is short with a light spicy oak kick and a slight tingle on the tongue.

conclusion.marcel: Patrisbrunna is a true local hero with plenty of character and a charming spiciness that leaves you wanting more – even though the youthful sharpness could still be tamed a bit. For fans of down-to-earth, handcrafted single malts from Germany, it’s a rewarding experience with genuine drinking pleasure.
conclusion.sascha: The Patrisbrunna Original impresses with its balanced interplay between malty sweetness and spicy oak, although the high strength and youthful sharpness slightly dominate the overall impression. Despite its still evident youthfulness, the cask maturation reveals a clear regional signature and a solid structure.

faq.question: What makes the Patrisbrunna Original Paderborner Single Malt 2016 special compared to other German whiskies?
faq.answer: Patrisbrunna is made with locally sourced barley and spring water from Paderborn, distilled four times, and aged for about five years. This combination of regional ingredients, quadruple distillation, and a relatively high alcohol content of 58% gives it a distinctive spiciness and complexity.

faq.question: How does the fourfold distillation influence the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: The fourfold distillation produces a particularly pure yet intense spirit. This process develops spicy and floral notes as well as roasted aromas reminiscent of grilled food, while keeping the whisky soft and full-bodied.

faq.question: What is the best way to enjoy Patrisbrunna Single Malt to fully appreciate its aromas?
faq.answer: The whisky can be enjoyed neat to experience its vanilla sweetness and malty aromas. Adding a few drops of water opens up the bouquet to reveal floral honey and sweet vanilla, making the flavor rounder with more oak and roasted notes.

item.name: Ardbeg Scorch Committee Release
item.description: Ardbeg's Committee Release 2021 was the Scorch. The malt was stored in extremely burnt out ex-bourbon barrels. The associated story tells of a dragon that was resident in Ardbeg's Warehouse 3.As usual for Ardbeg, this Committee Release Malt is only available for purchase directly from Ardbeg on Ardbeg Day. Probably, very few bottles actually make it to the tasting - most will probably stand locked in collector's cabinets.

tasting_notes.nose: The opening is, as expected, biting. Intense BBQ and campfire aromas. A certain meaty, tar-like quality is rounded out by sweet vanilla notes. For us, the malt is (unusually for Ardbeg) slightly medicinal.
tasting_notes.palate: The taste is - after smelling - surprisingly sweet. Vanilla and caramel nestle with intense oak and the taste of burnt toast. Dryness and pepper spice complement the sweetness on the palate.
tasting_notes.finish: The intense Scorch stays in the mouth for a long time. Intensely woody with smoke and ash, it becomes a bit rubbery at the back.

conclusion.marcel: The Ardbeg Scorch surprises with its smoky intensity and the sweet counterbalance that makes the drinking experience exciting. Still, the high price feels more like that of a collector’s piece than pure drinking pleasure – a whisky you admire more than you regularly enjoy.
conclusion.sascha: A well-structured, complex Islay malt with a balanced interplay between smoky notes and sweet vanilla aromas. Nevertheless, the quality of the flavor doesn’t fully justify the inflated market price, making it more of a limited collector’s item than an everyday dram.

faq.question: What makes the Ardbeg Scorch Committee Release 2021 special compared to other Ardbeg bottlings?
faq.answer: The Ardbeg Scorch was matured in heavily charred ex-bourbon casks, which impart its distinctive smoky and woody aromas. This cask treatment is unusual for Ardbeg and creates the intense BBQ and campfire notes that set it apart from other releases.

faq.question: Why is the Ardbeg Scorch Committee Release so hard to obtain?
faq.answer: Scorch is an exclusive Committee Release available only on Ardbeg Day directly at the distillery. The bottle count is very limited, so many bottles remain in collectors’ cabinets, with only a few available for tasting.

faq.question: What flavor profiles can you expect from the Ardbeg Scorch despite its strong smokiness?
faq.answer: Alongside intense smoky and tarry notes, the Ardbeg Scorch surprisingly reveals sweet hints of vanilla and caramel. On the palate, dry pepper spice and oak complement the sweet nuances, while the finish is woody, smoky, and slightly rubbery.

item.name: Lochlea Fallow Edition (First Crop)
item.description: The Lochlea distillery plans four special bottlings a year. The Fallow Edition follows the Harvest Edition and is intended to embody an autumnal-winter profile.The single malt Scotch whisky was matured 100% exclusively in Oloroso sherry butts. There are a number of whiskies on the market that have also undergone such ageing - will the Lochlea distillery manage to stand out once again?

tasting_notes.nose: Crystal clear: Ripe dark fruits, leather, dark chocolate. We expected this and got it. Pleasantly, raspberries, strawberries and cinnamon still resonate. Somewhere, after some time in the glass, we find a few wine aromas and some burnt wood. Beautifully defined profile with exciting secondary aromas.
tasting_notes.palate: Fresh and at the same time heavy-oily, the Fallow Edition lays in the mouth. As in the nose, we have a red berry mix, some tannins and oak spice. Not as dry as we would have expected, the Lowland single malt qualifies as an excellent sherry bomb.
tasting_notes.finish: In the warming, medium-long finish, we find the dark chocolate from the nose again.

conclusion.marcel: For a young Lowland whisky, the Fallow Edition is a real delight – the blend of dark chocolate and berries almost evokes a winter mulled wine with a kick. Sure, it’s not the most complex sherry bomb, but the fresh fruitiness and smooth oiliness make for endless drinking pleasure. If you’re into autumnal flavors, this one’s right up your alley.
conclusion.sascha: The Lochlea Fallow Edition presents a balanced aromatic interplay of Oloroso sherry, dark fruits, and oak spice, which is remarkably well-structured for a three-years single malt. Despite its youth, the profile is well-defined and harmonious, with a finish that remains pleasantly warming. A convincing example of the quality of young Lowland sherry casks, it once again raises the bar for the distillery.

faq.question: How does maturation in 100% Oloroso sherry casks influence the taste of the Lochlea Fallow Edition?
faq.answer: Exclusive aging in Oloroso sherry casks imparts the whisky with intense aromas of ripe dark fruits, dark chocolate, and leather. This cask choice creates a full-bodied, sweet-spicy profile featuring notes of red berries, cinnamon, and a pleasant oak spice.

faq.question: What sets the Lochlea Fallow Edition apart from other Lowland single malts?
faq.answer: The Lochlea Fallow Edition stands out through its combination of a fresh, heavy-oily texture and intense sherry cask maturation. Despite its young age of three years, it offers a complex flavor profile with clearly defined secondary notes like raspberries, wine, and lightly charred wood, distinguishing it from many other Lowland whiskies.

faq.question: Which season and drinking occasion suit the Lochlea Fallow Edition best?
faq.answer: With its autumnal-winter character and warming, medium-length finish, the Lochlea Fallow Edition is especially well-suited for cooler months. It’s ideal for relaxed enjoyment by the fireside or as a companion to hearty autumnal dishes.

item.name: Glenturret 30 Years (1987/2018) Xtra Old Particular - Douglas Laing
item.description: The Glenturret 30 years was bottled as part of the XOP (Xtra Old Particular) by the independent bottler Douglas Laing. The XOP bottlings come from the rarest and best stocks of the bottler and are always bottled from a single cask at natural cask strength. Unfiltered and uncoloured - just as natural as a whisky has to be.As befits a unique single malt, the packaging is correspondingly elaborate. In addition to the handwritten bottle number (40 of 229), the bottle comes in a magnetically closing, high quality wooden packaging.

image.alt: Glenturret 1987/2018 30 Years Xtra Old Particular - Douglas Laing

tasting_notes.nose: Delicate and multifaceted, we smell spicy oak aromas, nutty notes and old grey bread. Behind that lies a velvety softness, something buttery and vanilla-floral characteristics.
tasting_notes.palate: Just as buttery as the nose, notes reminiscent of oatmeal with honey in orange juice spread out on the palate. Further back, precisely integrated spicy oak notes, some apple juice and a malty sweetness come to full effect. The individual aromas are clearly distributed and are suitably transported by alcohol.
tasting_notes.finish: A long-lasting finish with spicy oak, which later turns into a delightful sweetness, rounds off the pleasure in style.

conclusion.marcel: This 30-years Glenturret from the Xtra Old Particular range is like a cozy breakfast – rich, versatile, and simply enjoyable. Every sip reveals new nuances that are fun and make you want more. A true experience for connoisseurs that you won’t forget anytime soon!
conclusion.sascha: The Glenturret 1987/2018 impresses with its balanced structure and clear harmony between spicy oak, malty sweetness, and delicate fruitiness. The precisely integrated aromas and the long finish demonstrate the quality of a carefully matured Highland whisky at natural cask strength. A technically convincing single malt with plenty of character.

faq.question: What makes Douglas Laing’s Xtra Old Particular (XOP) series special?
faq.answer: The Xtra Old Particular series consists of single cask bottlings from particularly rare and high-quality stocks, bottled at natural cask strength, unfiltered and without coloring. Each bottle is hand-numbered and presented in a premium wooden box.

faq.question: How does the long maturation of 30 years influence the taste of the Glenturret 1987/2018?
faq.answer: The 30-year aging imparts complex aromas of spicy oak, nuts, and malty sweetness that harmonize with floral and vanilla notes. The extended maturation also provides an exceptionally smooth mouthfeel and a long-lasting, balanced finish.

faq.question: Why is the Glenturret 1987/2018 non-chillfiltered and non-colored?
faq.answer: Douglas Laing deliberately avoids chill filtration and color additives in the XOP series to keep the whisky as natural as possible. This preserves all the subtle aromas and the characteristic texture, maintaining the authentic character of the single malt.

item.name: Wild Turkey Rare Breed
item.description: Since 1991, the Rare Breed has been part of the core range of the Wild Turkey distillery. The Barrel Proof Kentucky Straight Bourbon is a blend of 6, 8 and 12 years whiskeys, and gets bottled uncut.The mashbill contains 75% corn, 13% rye and 12% malted barley - which leads us to expect a sweetish, slightly spicy whiskey. Like all bourbons, the Wild Turkey must also mature in a new, freshly burned out Virgin Oak barrel. The barrel was burned out to Char 4 level, also called Alligator Char. Char 4 is the highest char level.

tasting_notes.nose: As usual for a bourbon, the Rare Breed opens with a slight smell of solvents which fades quickly. This is followed by a multi-layered aroma explosion of popcorn, blackberries, fruity cherry, caramel, sugar syrup (maple syrup), and a good portion of oak. Very well woven and integrated variety of flavors overwhelms at the first moment. At the second nosing after the first sip, additional citrus notes, black tea and even more intense caramel are perceptible.
tasting_notes.palate: Whoa. We've rarely experienced such sweet stickiness and chewy viscosity in a whiskey and also whiskey. Once the taste buds get used to the sweetness, rye and oak spice make the second impact. The third, very dry round is characterized by leather, tobacco and dried tangerine.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is long, warming, dry and chewy - in a positive sense.

conclusion.marcel: This Rare Breed shows just how much fun a bourbon with over 58% alcohol can be without being harsh – sweet, viscous, and packed with plenty of character. If you enjoy intense aromas and a warming viscosity, you’ll find a genuine drinking pleasure here, though it might be a challenge for bourbon newcomers.
conclusion.sascha: The Wild Turkey Rare Breed impresses with its complex aroma profile and a remarkable balance between sweet vanilla, spicy rye, and robust oak. Its thick, syrupy texture and long, dry-warming finish make it a technically very well-crafted barrel proof bourbon that appeals to experienced connoisseurs.

faq.question: Why is Wild Turkey Rare Breed bottled without dilution?
faq.answer: Wild Turkey Rare Breed is bottled at barrel proof, meaning it comes straight from the barrel without any dilution. This preserves the full flavor and original alcohol strength of 58.4%, allowing for a more intense and authentic bourbon experience.

faq.question: What role does the Char 4 level of barrel charring play in the flavor?
faq.answer: The Char 4 level, also known as Alligator Char, is the deepest charring of a virgin oak barrel. It creates a deeply caramelized wood surface that imparts intense vanilla, caramel, and roasted notes to the bourbon while simultaneously emphasizing the oak spice.

faq.question: How does the mash bill of 75% corn, 13% rye, and 12% malted barley influence the whisky’s flavor?
faq.answer: The high corn content provides the classic sweetness typical of bourbon, while the rye contributes a subtle spiciness and bite. The malted barley supports fermentation and adds additional malty notes. Together, they create a balanced profile with sweet, spicy, and fruity aromas.

item.name: Wild Turkey 101
item.description: The Kentucky Straight Bourbon Wild Turkey 101 from the distillery of the same name in Lawrenceburg, Kentucky is a cornerstone of American bourbon culture. Without an age statement on the label, it is known to be bottled as a blend of 6-8 years bourbons. We take a closer look at the US classic.The mashbill is composed of 75% corn, 13% rye and 12% malted barley - which leads us to expect a sweetish, slightly spicy whiskey. Like all bourbons, Wild Turkey has to mature in new, freshly burnt-out Virgin Oak casks. 101 stands for American proof alcohol, which translates as 50.5% ABV.

tasting_notes.nose: The whiskey with the turkey on the lable opens up with intense aromas of brown sugar and caramel. Behind the slightly pungent smelling alcohol we perceive vanilla, light spiciness, cola and tobacco leaves. The mind pictures a caramel pudding with brittle and a vanilla coke to go with it.
tasting_notes.palate: In the first moment, the whiskey opens with a neat sugar-popcorn-syrup combination, closely followed by a massively spicy, very powerful kick. The oak and rye are clearly evident here. A liquorice and herb note floats along - somehow the composition reminds us of liquorice sticks with caramel.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is surprisingly short, tingles and is furry dry on the back of the tongue.

conclusion.marcel: Wild Turkey 101 is simply fun – whether neat or in a cocktail, this robust bourbon always brings good cheer to the glass with its sweet and spicy notes. For anyone looking for an uncomplicated yet characterful whiskey, it’s the perfect choice.
conclusion.sascha: A technically well-structured bourbon with a clear balance between sweetness, spice, and oak, which holds its own even in mixed drinks thanks to its 50.5% ABV. Wild Turkey 101 impresses as a versatile classic with distinctive aromatics, though the finish could linger a bit longer.

faq.question: Why doesn’t Wild Turkey 101 have an age statement on the bottle?
faq.answer: Wild Turkey 101 is a blend of bourbons aged between 6 and 8 years. The distillery deliberately omits a precise age statement to ensure flavor consistency across different batches.

faq.question: How does the high rye content in Wild Turkey 101 affect its flavor?
faq.answer: With 13% rye in the mash bill, Wild Turkey 101 imparts a distinctive spiciness and sharpness that balances the sweetness of the corn and delivers the signature peppery notes.

faq.question: What does the number 101 on the Wild Turkey bottle mean?
faq.answer: The number 101 refers to the American proof value, which corresponds to an alcohol content of 50.5% ABV. This makes Wild Turkey 101 a bold bourbon with intense aromatics.

item.name: Redbreast 12 Years
item.description: The Readbreast 12 from the Midleton distillery in County Cork in Ireland is a single pot still. This means that not only malted but also a proportion of unmalted barley is used in the mash. Malting barley produces enzymes that enable the yeast cultures to convert sugar into alcohol.Like all the other whiskeys in the Redbreast series, the 12 years was also triple distilled in copper stills. The Single Pot Still was matured in Spanish sherry casks and American bourbon barrels.

tasting_notes.nose: After a very fruity-mild opening, spicy-rich aromas push to the fore. Ripe pears and semolina pudding with marzipan can be noticed in the Redbreast. The sherry influence is embodied by freshly baked plum cake with crumble.
tasting_notes.palate: The 12 years comes across as soft as butter. Still spicy and fruity, a slight note of fruit wine resonates. Roasted aromas and dried berries are clearly to be found in the background. In the middle part there are cereal notes, which in combination with the dried berries make us think of crunchy muesli.
tasting_notes.finish: Long-lasting, intense with strong oak spice on the palate, the Irish whiskey lingers.

conclusion.marcel: A true delight, whose fruity sweetness and spicy sherry notes immediately invite you to take the next sip. The Redbreast 12 shows character without overwhelming – perfect for anyone who wants to enjoy their Irish whiskey with a bit of fun.
conclusion.sascha: Balanced and well-structured, the Redbreast 12 impresses with a harmonious blend of sherry and bourbon cask influences, along with a distinct oak spiciness in the finish. A technically precise Single Pot Still that scores especially with its layered aromas and smooth texture.

faq.question: What does 'Single Pot Still' mean in Redbreast 12 Years?
faq.answer: Single Pot Still whisky is made from a mash that contains both malted and unmalted barley. This traditional Irish method gives Redbreast 12 Years its characteristic spicy and full-bodied flavor.

faq.question: How does maturation in sherry casks and bourbon barrels influence the flavor?
faq.answer: Maturation in Spanish sherry casks brings out fruity and sweet notes like plum cake and marzipan, while American bourbon barrels contribute gentle vanilla and oak tones. The interplay of these casks gives the whisky its complex aroma profile.

faq.question: Why is triple distillation important for Redbreast 12 Years?
faq.answer: Triple distillation ensures an especially pure and smooth whisky by removing unwanted substances. This is typical for Irish whiskeys and contributes to the velvety texture and clarity of flavor found in Redbreast 12 Years.

item.name: Cardhu 12 Years
item.description: Until 1983, the distillery was called Cardow, named after a nearby village. Nowadays known as Cardhu (Gaelic: Black Rock), the distillery produces the significant share for the Johnnie Walker blends. Of course, there are also their own bottlings, of which we have the 12-years standard bottling in the glass today.The Cardhu 12 Single Malt Scotch Whisky belongs to the Classic Malts of Scotland and is supposed to represent the character of the Speyside region of Scotland.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, the whisky brings the classic notes of a Speysider: Vanilla, caramel, apple and honey are dominant, while some nutty flavours lie in the background.
tasting_notes.palate: We taste very old, very old oatmeal biscuits, which make us think of the Kinder Country commercial from the 90s. Woody, bitter notes resonate. Slowly and viscously, a little caramel can be perceived and, with a little imagination, hazelnuts can also be found. We don't find the fruitiness from the nose.
tasting_notes.finish: Slightly bitter, slightly woody and with something strange on the palate, the single malt finishes with some warming spiciness.

conclusion.marcel: For the price, the Cardhu 12 feels somewhat uninspired – the nose promises more than the palate delivers. Instead of an enjoyable dram, you get a confusing mix of flavors that remind you more of a blender than a Speyside. There are far more interesting alternatives out there, even within the standard range.
conclusion.sascha: The Cardhu 12 shows classic Speyside notes on the nose, but on the palate it lacks the expected harmony and depth; the flavors come across as unbalanced and somewhat diffuse. For a 12-years standard release, the structure feels rather flat, and the finish disappoints with an unpleasant bitterness. Those who value balance will find better single malts in this price segment.

faq.question: Why is Cardhu 12 years considered a typical Speyside whisky?
faq.answer: Cardhu 12 years is characterized by the classic traits of a Speyside whisky, such as notes of vanilla, caramel, and honey, along with a gentle fruitiness. These aromas reflect the region’s character, which is known for its smooth and well-balanced single malts.

faq.question: How does the 12-year age influence the taste of Cardhu whisky?
faq.answer: With 12 years of maturation, Cardhu develops a balanced complexity: the vanilla and caramel notes are well matured, while woody and slightly bitter hints add depth. The age provides a harmonious balance between fruitiness and spice.

faq.question: What role does Cardhu play in the Johnnie Walker blends?
faq.answer: Cardhu supplies a significant portion of the malt components for the Johnnie Walker blends. Its mild and sweet character contributes substantially to the balance and profile of these well-known blended whiskies.

item.name: Knockando 12 Years
item.description: Knockando 12 is considered the classic entry-level single malt, representative of Speyside, with a fruity, nutty flavour profile. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. Knockando mainly produces malts for blends. Nevertheless there are some own bottlings which are traded under the name of the distillery Knockando (Gaelic for: Little Black Hill).

tasting_notes.nose: As we would expect from a Speysider: nutty and fruity. We think of apple, pear and honey. A floral scent rounds off the bright, light fragrance.
tasting_notes.palate: Tastes like cinema. A very cereal aroma inevitably makes us think of popcorn and canned corn. Some vanilla and oak sneak up behind. This scotch is very delicate.
tasting_notes.finish: In the medium-long finish, the single malt becomes a tad bitter, but remains nutty and malty.

conclusion.marcel: Light and delicate like a mild summer evening, but a bit too tame for true whisky adventurers. A nice dram for beginners, scoring points with its fruity and nutty character – but those seeking more depth will likely be disappointed.
conclusion.sascha: A solid Speyside malt with balanced fruitiness and subtle vanilla notes, though it lacks a distinctive character and greater complexity. For the affordable price, the Knockando 12 Year offers a decent balance but remains somewhat one-dimensional overall.

faq.question: What makes Knockando 12 Year a typical Speyside whisky?
faq.answer: Knockando 12 Year is characterized by its fruity and nutty flavor profile, which is typical of Speyside whiskies. Notes of apple, pear, and honey, along with a lightly floral aroma, reflect the regional style.

faq.question: How does the 12-year age influence the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: With 12 years of maturation, Knockando develops a balanced combination of delicate malt notes, vanilla, and oak. The age provides a medium-length finish where mild bitterness blends with nutty and malty flavors.

faq.question: What is Knockando 12 Year best suited for: neat, with water, or in cocktails?
faq.answer: Knockando 12 Year reveals its fine aromas best when enjoyed neat or with a small splash of water to highlight the fruity and nutty notes. Due to its gentle character, it is less suited for robust cocktails.

item.name: Stork Club Straight Rye Whiskey
item.description: The rye for the rye whiskey from Stork Club is harvested in one of the largest rye-growing areas in Europe, namely in the German state of Brandenburg.The Rye whiskey matures in barrels made of American and German oak, which have been burnt out to different degrees. It is advertised as the perfect Rye for cocktails - is that true?

tasting_notes.nose: The first impression is tangy-grainy and ethanol-like. Once the nose has become accustomed to the sharpness, spicy-woody as well as sweet caramel flavours can be perceived.
tasting_notes.palate: Really buttery and creamy, the Stork Club Straight Rye Whisky tastes of caramel - so dominant that hardly any other notes are perceptible. After some time in the glass, both a slightly tart flavour and a certain bitterness emerge. However, it is not possible to identify this exactly.
tasting_notes.finish: In the finish, very spicy and with a punch The grain pushes strongly to the foreground. Even minutes after drinking, we have the taste of rye bread on the palate.

conclusion.marcel: The Stork Club Straight Rye Whiskey is a true original, surprising with its creamy caramel notes and spicy finish – not necessarily complex, but honest and full of character. For anyone looking for an uncomplicated, regional rye, it’s definitely enjoyable, whether neat or in a cocktail. A whiskey that scores more on personality than perfection.
conclusion.sascha: This German rye impresses with its clear structure and balanced use of American and German oak, even though the dominance of caramel limits its complexity. The finish reveals an interesting spiciness and grain emphasis, making the Stork Club a solid cocktail whiskey, but for lovers of subtle nuances it remains somewhat one-dimensional.

faq.question: Why is the Stork Club Straight Rye Whiskey recommended as ideal for cocktails?
faq.answer: The Stork Club Straight Rye Whiskey is characterized by its buttery-smooth, caramel flavor component and its spicy, robust finish. This balance makes it versatile and ensures it blends well with other ingredients in cocktails without being overshadowed.

faq.question: What special qualities does aging in American and German oak bring?
faq.answer: The combination of American and German oak, each charred to different degrees, gives the whiskey a complex wood note. American oak often contributes sweet, vanilla-like aromas, while German oak adds more bitter, spicier notes, making the flavor more layered.

faq.question: How does the rye-growing region of Brandenburg influence the character of this rye whiskey?
faq.answer: Since the rye comes from Brandenburg, one of Europe’s largest rye-growing regions, the grain is particularly aromatic and robust. This is reflected in the whiskey’s spicy, grain-forward profile, which is especially noticeable on the finish with flavors reminiscent of rye bread.

item.name: Glen Scotia Double Cask
item.description: You hear a lot of good things about distilleries from the Scottish coastal town of Campbeltown in recent years. It's hard to have a conversation about Glen Scotia without someone steering the topic towards Springbank, which everyone agrees is better in every way. Is that really true?Double Cask is an NAS Scotch with Glen Scotia single malt matured in first-fill ex-bourbon casks and aged for up to a year in Pedro Ximénez (PX) sherry casks. Up to one year - a wild time specification.

tasting_notes.nose: More intense than expected. Honey, vanilla, candied fruits, as you know them from the fair and a bunch of peach. If the whisky lingers a little in the glass, we perceive hints of leather (yes, exactly the section for leather shoes in the shoe shop), herbs and clear sherry notes. A floral note, as well as something woody, which is more reminiscent of a burnt match, can also be detected.
tasting_notes.palate: Dry - really dust-dry - a lot of spicy oak lies mouth-filling in the foreground. Left and right we find a bit of cinnamon, some peanuts, dried fruit (dates, apricots and peach) and a dominant vanilla.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish drags - like chewing gum - spicy-intense, as well as earthy-leathery and has a dominant note of Swiss herbal bonbons.

conclusion.marcel: This Glen Scotia Double Cask is a genuine surprise hit – packed full of character and drinkability that you often miss in some pricier malts. For less than half the price of a Springbank 10, it offers significantly more enjoyment and an exciting, spicy depth. Anyone looking for a delicious, straightforward Campbeltown whisky with plenty of personality should definitely snap this up.
conclusion.sascha: The Glen Scotia Double Cask demonstrates a remarkable balance between intense sherry notes and a spicy oak structure, making it highly competitive compared to the Springbank 10. The complex aroma profile featuring leather, herbs, and dried fruits is supported by a long-lasting, spicy finish, with the cask combination well harmonized. For the price, this Campbeltown whisky delivers a convincing interplay of structure and character that shouldn’t be underestimated.

faq.question: What does maturation in first-fill ex-bourbon and Pedro Ximénez sherry casks mean for the taste of the Glen Scotia Double Cask?
faq.answer: Maturation in first-fill ex-bourbon casks imparts notes of vanilla, honey, and spicy oak to the whisky, while aging in Pedro Ximénez sherry casks adds fruity and sweet aromas such as candied fruits, peach, and dried fruits. This combination creates a complex flavor profile balanced between dry and sweet elements.
