faq.question: How does Glen Scotia Double Cask differ from other Campbeltown whiskies, especially Springbank?
faq.answer: Glen Scotia Double Cask is a no-age-statement single malt that emphasizes sweeter and spicier notes through its combination of bourbon and sherry cask maturation. In contrast, Springbank is known for its more complex, often peatier and maritime aromas. Both represent the Campbeltown region but offer distinct flavor profiles and styles.

faq.question: What flavors can you expect on the finish of the Glen Scotia Double Cask?
faq.answer: The finish of the Glen Scotia Double Cask is long and intensely spicy with earthy, leathery notes. Characteristic hints of Swiss herbal candies add a fresh, slightly sweet herbal nuance to the finish.

item.name: The Balvenie 12 Years Doublewood
item.description: The Balvenie DoubleWood 12 years is the youngest bottling from Balvenie's Core Range with an age statement. This malt matures in ex-bourbon casks and receives a subsequent post-maturation in ex-Oloroso sherry casks.The Balvenie distillery is located on the Speyside in Dufftown. It dedicates itself to the traditional production of Single Malts and is one of the few distilleries that still dries their (on-site grown) barley on its own malting floors.

tasting_notes.nose: The 12 years Whisky opens with the sweetness of fresh blossom honey. The malt smells extremely juicy with malty vanilla notes. Behind the delicate sweetness, the bouquet is coated by a pleasant spicy sherry note.
tasting_notes.palate: Fittingly, it presents buttery soft and mild on the tongue. We perceive nutty notes and a hint of cinnamon. Likewise, an association of freshly caramelized crème brûlée imposes itself. Here, too, the sweet fresh aromas of sherry and the spiciness of European oak wrap the flavour.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish of the single malt is medium in length. As before, a wonderful marriage of fresh sweet flavors with spicy oak tones.

conclusion.marcel: A truly delicious Speysider that’s simply enjoyable with its sweet honey notes and spicy hints of sherry. The creamy texture and subtle caramel undertones invite relaxed sipping – this is exactly how a 12-years DoubleWood should taste. For whisky fans who appreciate something straightforward yet full of character, this one’s a real winner.
conclusion.sascha: The Balvenie 12 Year DoubleWood presents a well-balanced interplay of vanilla, nutty notes, and subtle oak spice that clearly defines the single malt’s structure. The finishing maturation in sherry casks integrates harmoniously without overpowering, lending the Scotch a medium-length, elegant finish. A solid Speyside malt with clean cask maturation and a balanced aromatic profile.

faq.question: What does the double wood maturation mean in The Balvenie 12 Year DoubleWood?
faq.answer: Double wood maturation refers to the whisky’s aging first in ex-bourbon barrels and then in ex-oloroso sherry casks. This combination imparts a balanced blend of sweet vanilla and honey notes alongside spicy sherry aromas.

faq.question: How does Balvenie’s traditional barley drying affect the flavor?
faq.answer: Balvenie still dries its barley on-site using traditional malting floors, allowing for gentle processing. This method contributes to a particularly malty and complex flavor profile, which is clearly noticeable in the DoubleWood 12 Year.

faq.question: Why does The Balvenie DoubleWood 12 Year have a medium-length finish?
faq.answer: The medium-length finish results from the combination of sweet honey and caramel flavors from the bourbon cask maturation with the spicy oak tones from the sherry casks. This harmonious marriage creates a balanced and lingering taste conclusion.

item.name: Clynelish 14 Years
item.description: For a long time, Clynelish produced almost exclusively for blends, among others for Johnnie Walker. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. Meanwhile, a 14 and a 16 years bottling are offered. We take a look at what the younger of the two has to offer.The distillery run under the name Clynelish is actually still comparatively young. Clynelish was founded by the Duke of Sutherland in 1819 and was to be replaced by a new building in 1967. For a short time, Clynelish A (1819) and Clynelish B (1967) existed in parallel, with Clynelish A being renamed Brora after a short break in operations and the newer Clynelish B now being called Clynelish.

tasting_notes.nose: "I smell spring and there's a horse stable in there" was Marcel's first statement. And indeed. We perceive strong hay-like and flowery smells. Definitely a floral environment. There is also a bit of sugar.
tasting_notes.palate: It tastes quite different from what it smells like. The path perceived in the nose through the flower meadow led directly to the beach. Oily-soft but still light, we taste sea salt, think of shells by the sea. Light citrus fruits can be found in the sand dune. Impressive and unexpected.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is unfortunately only a short sea breeze. A little sweetish, but still dominantly salty.

conclusion.marcel: This Clynelish 14 surprises with an unusual combination of floral freshness and salty sea air – a whisky that promises more adventure than you might expect at first glance. While I miss the classic scent of oak wood, that’s exactly what makes it exciting and lively. A true drinking pleasure for anyone eager to discover something new.
conclusion.sascha: The Clynelish 14 presents an interesting balance between floral notes and a surprisingly salty, maritime element that gives the palate complexity and structure. However, the finish feels a bit short and could benefit from more persistence, which slightly detracts from the otherwise precise harmony. Overall, a very solid Highland whisky with a distinctive profile and fine aromatic character.

faq.question: Why does the Clynelish 14-years whisky smell of hay and flowers but taste salty and maritime?
faq.answer: The Clynelish 14-years combines floral and hay-like notes on the nose, reminiscent of a spring meadow. On the palate, maritime aromas such as sea salt and a light citrus freshness dominate, which is due to the distillery’s proximity to the coast and the maturation in specific casks.

faq.question: How does the Clynelish 14-years differ from older releases like the 16-years version?
faq.answer: The 14-years Clynelish is somewhat fresher and lighter with more pronounced floral and salty notes, whereas the 16-years version is often fuller-bodied and more complex, with greater depth and a longer finish. The maturation period thus influences both the flavor and texture.

faq.question: What is the history behind the Clynelish distillery and its connection to Brora?
faq.answer: Clynelish was founded in 1819 and replaced by a new building in 1967. During a transitional period, two distilleries existed: Clynelish A (the older one) and Clynelish B (the new one). After a pause, Clynelish A was renamed Brora, becoming an independent distillery with its own character, while the newer distillery continued operating under the name Clynelish.

item.name: Scallywag Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing
item.description: The blended Scotch Malt Whisky is supposed to be cheeky, at least that's what the name and label promise. The Scallywag is now available in a wide variety of editions and styles. We have the classic in our glasses.What could be better than a dog with a monocle as a bottle label? The blend from Speyside is decorative in its own peculiar way. It is said to contain Mortlach, Macallan, Glenrothes and a few more.

image.alt: Scallywag Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing

tasting_notes.nose: The dominant sweetness on the nose reminds us of nothing other than vanilla pudding with icing, as strange as that may sound. In addition, we have an undertone of grain and malt, as well as sherry aromas.
tasting_notes.palate: In contrast to the nose, we have surprisingly little sweetness, but plenty of vanilla, cardamom and a little oak, which provides a slightly woody dryness. Green grassy notes are present as well as a hint of coked grass. Sherry flavours are in the background. The mouthfeel is slightly oily
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is light on oak tannins, a little cocoa and a bit of sherry tumbles at the end. A cheeky shot of black pepper follows at the very end.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that’s simply fun with its sweet vanilla notes and cheeky peppery finish – exactly what you’d expect from a Scallywag. Despite the slightly woody finish, it remains approachable and charming, perfect for sociable evenings without any fuss.
conclusion.sascha: The balance between sweet vanilla tones and subtle oak dryness is solid, although the sweetness present on the nose is more restrained on the palate. The complex interplay of cardamom, sherry, and pepper provides structure, even if the blend as a whole could benefit from a bit more depth.

faq.question: Which distilleries are included in the Scallywag Small Batch Release?
faq.answer: The blend consists of whiskies from various Speyside distilleries, including Mortlach, Macallan, and Glenrothes, complemented by other undisclosed malt whiskies.

faq.question: How does the taste of Scallywag differ from other Speyside whiskies?
faq.answer: Scallywag presents an unusual combination of sweet vanilla pudding notes on the nose and a rather spicy, slightly woody flavor with cardamom and black pepper on the finish, setting it apart from the typical fruity-floral Speyside style.

faq.question: Why does Scallywag have no age statement on the bottle?
faq.answer: The Scallywag Small Batch Release is a no-age-statement (NAS) blend because Douglas Laing, as an independent bottler, combines various younger and older whiskies to achieve a distinctive flavor profile.

item.name: Talisker Port Ruighe
item.description: Talisker Port Ruighe was named after the port in the small harbour town of Portree (Gaelic: Port Righ), which was very important for trade with the island.Traditionally, the Talisker distillery matures whisky in port wine casks, which have been imported from Portugal via the "Port of the King" since the 19th century. Port wine was also traded through this port. The Talisker Port Ruighe is meant to bring back a piece from the past.

tasting_notes.nose: Hello Talisker - the Singla Malt opens with the unmistakable fruity, spicy and maritime-smoky notes of a Talisker. We think of warm custard, dark fruits and plums by the campfire. A little more hearty, notes of smoked bacon and oak lie in the background. Simply a Talisker.
tasting_notes.palate: The taste is probably best described as red wine barrels on a pirate ship. Very spicy-sweet and full-bodied, we taste red fruits, sweet smoke, as well as caramel and a slightly peppery spiciness. Further back, we perceive strong woody aromas and some dry bread.
tasting_notes.finish: Baking cocoa, a voluminous smoke, woody flavours and the typical Talisker chilli kick form the long-lasting finish.

conclusion.marcel: A true delight for anyone who enjoys smoky and fruity flavors with a touch of madness – Talisker Port Ruighe brings the pirate’s life straight to the glass. The sweet, dark aromas from the port cask turn every sip into a little adventure that leaves you wanting more.
conclusion.sascha: Talisker Port Ruighe impresses with a balanced harmony of maritime smokiness and sweet port wine notes that perfectly round off its complex flavor profile. Despite the intense wood tones, the structure remains clean, and the finish is long-lasting with the signature chilli kick.

faq.question: Why is Talisker Port Ruighe matured in port wine casks, and how does this influence the flavor?
faq.answer: Talisker Port Ruighe is matured in port wine casks, a tradition of the distillery that traces back to the historic trade through the port of Portree. These casks impart fruity and sweet notes to the whisky, which blend with Talisker’s typical smoky character to create a complex flavor profile featuring red fruits, caramel, and a subtle spiciness.

faq.question: How does Talisker Port Ruighe differ from other Talisker expressions?
faq.answer: Talisker Port Ruighe stands out due to its maturation in port wine casks, giving it a pronounced fruity sweetness and a special depth. Compared to classic Talisker whiskies, it is somewhat milder and sweeter, while still retaining the characteristic maritime smokiness and peppery sharpness.

faq.question: What flavors can be especially detected in Talisker Port Ruighe?
faq.answer: In Talisker Port Ruighe, fruity notes like dark fruits and plums dominate, complemented by smoky and maritime elements. Additionally, there are sweet hints of vanilla and caramel, spicy touches of pepper, woody aromas, and a long-lasting, slightly sharp finish with dark cocoa and smoke.

item.name: Slipknot No.9 Whiskey
item.description: Often, the celebrities who give their names to spirits have nothing to do with it - this is not the case here. The drummer Shawn Crahan has blended the two varieties No. 9 and No. 9 Reserve. We have the No. 9 in the tasting.No. 9 was developed in collaboration with the Cedar Ridge distillery in Iowa and has become the distillery's best-seller nationwide. Cedar Ridge uses locally grown Iowa corn (51%) for their whiskeys, plus 35% malted rye and 14% malted barley.

tasting_notes.nose: The bourbon opens with a pleasant intense and vanilla-like oak aroma. Notes of cinnamon, butter and nut follow. We can't help but think of French rolls.
tasting_notes.palate: The bourbon also delivers on the taste what the nose promises. The intense and spicy taste of American oak paired with vanilla aromas is the dominant note. The voluminous and creamy mouthfeel is precisely elaborated by a hint of citrus and with fruity blood orange.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish is creamy and slightly spicy.

conclusion.marcel: Slipknot No.9 is a genuine surprise that proves well-known names aren’t always just marketing hype. Light, sweet, and with a touch of spice – this whiskey is simply fun and brings variety to the tasting circle. For fans of straightforward, American bourbon, it’s a clear delight.
conclusion.sascha: The balance between fruity blood orange, vanilla, and spicy American oak in Slipknot No.9 is well achieved, making it a convincing representative of the bourbon category. The creamy texture and medium-length finish provide structure, even though it still lacks a bit of depth and complexity. Overall, it’s a well-balanced whiskey with solid craftsmanship from Iowa.

faq.question: How does Slipknot No.9 Whiskey differ from other American bourbons?
faq.answer: Slipknot No.9 Whiskey stands out due to its high rye content (35%) alongside corn (51%) and malted barley (14%), giving it a spicier and more complex character than many typical bourbons with a higher corn ratio.

faq.question: What role did Shawn Crahan play in the creation of Slipknot No.9 Whiskey?
faq.answer: Shawn Crahan, drummer of the band Slipknot, was actively involved in developing the whiskey and collaborated with Cedar Ridge Distillery to blend the recipes for No.9 and No.9 Reserve.

faq.question: What flavor notes can be expected from Slipknot No.9 Whiskey?
faq.answer: The whiskey offers intense oak and vanilla aromas, complemented by hints of cinnamon, butter, and nuts. On the palate, it reveals a creamy texture with spicy wood notes, citrus, and fruity blood orange, rounded off by a medium-length, creamy-spicy finish.

item.name: St. Kilian Signature Edition Nine
item.description: The German distillery St. Kilian regularly releases its Signature Edition. Behind this 3-year matured single malt whisky is an exciting cask combination that can lead to an interesting result.The German Whisky from Upper Franconia was matured in bourbon barrels (62%), Sauternes barrels (27%) and casks made of Palatinate oak (11%). The Signature Edition is delivered in the usual pot-still bottle shape, which by now has a high recognition value.

tasting_notes.nose: Solvent dominates - clearly, the lion's share matured in bourbon barrels. Behind that, there are slight wooden aromas. After a good half hour in the glass, the fruity, citrusy and wine-like notes are released. We smell sultanas and dates, citrus, pineapple and pear.
tasting_notes.palate: The initial taste is "hella sweet". The taste is clearly dominated by fresh fruity pear. With its 55.3% ABV, a few drops of water do no harm at all. The pear, apple and light fruits are more clearly defined. New notes that make us think of vanilla ice cream with strawberries and cherry also shine out. The composition is accompanied by a subtle woody spiciness.
tasting_notes.finish: The Signature Edition lingers slightly dry on the palate and leaves a strawberry note.

conclusion.marcel: This St. Kilian Signature Edition Nine is a genuine flavor powerhouse that, despite its young age of just 3 years, impresses with plenty of character and sweet fruitiness. The combination of pear, vanilla ice cream, and a subtle hint of strawberry makes the drinking experience both exciting and enjoyable – although the price is a bit steep.
conclusion.sascha: The Signature Edition Nine demonstrates an impressively mature balance for a young German single malt, with clearly defined fruit aromas and a structured wood note. Nevertheless, the price feels somewhat ambitious in relation to the cask maturation, which slightly clouds the overall rating.

faq.question: What types of casks were used to mature the St. Kilian Signature Edition Nine?
faq.answer: The whisky was matured in a combination of bourbon barrels (62%), Sauternes casks (27%), and Palatinate oak barrels (11%). This blend significantly influences the aroma and flavor complexity.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 55.3% affect the enjoyment of the whisky?
faq.answer: At 55.3% ABV, the whisky is powerful and intense. Adding a few drops of water can help to highlight the aromas of pear, apple, and vanilla ice cream more clearly and make the flavor more approachable.

faq.question: What aromas can typically be expected in the St. Kilian Signature Edition Nine?
faq.answer: Typical aromas include fruity notes such as pear, apple, citrus fruits, as well as raisins and dates. The profile is complemented by woody spice and a strawberry-like note on the finish.

item.name: Bulleit Bourbon 10 Years
item.description: Under the name Bulleit Diageo distributes a bourbon brand whose exact origin was unclear for a long time. With the 10-years bottling they offer one of the few core range Bourbons with an age statement.Until 2013, Bulleit was (allegedly) distilled by Four Roses for Diageo. Then in 2017, a Bulleit distillery was built in Kentucky. In between, there was also a Bulleit visitor center at the former Stitzel-Weller Distiller. What is no secret is the recipe for Bulleit: 68% corn, 28% rye and 4% malted barley.

tasting_notes.nose: The bourbon presents itself strongly on the nose. With vanilla and a mixture of sweet caramel and brittle chips. Behind this are intense spicy oak notes and a hint of chocolate.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip is interestingly complex. Crisp sweetness, intense rye spice and pronounced dryness at the same time. In addition, there are toasty aromas - like burnt crumbs from the bottom of your toaster. Again we perceive brittle chips and chocolate. A hint of clove in the background.
tasting_notes.finish: Bulleit 10 lingers surprisingly long. The finish is soft and at the same time tangy and sweet.

conclusion.marcel: Bulleit 10 Year is a genuine taste experience that’s simply fun with its sweet caramel notes and lively rye spice. For this price, you get a bourbon with character that tells a little story with every sip – definitely worth grabbing!
conclusion.sascha: The Bulleit Bourbon 10 Year impresses with a balanced harmony between sweet vanilla and caramel aromas and the distinctive rye spiciness that structures the body. Its complex flavor profile and long, harmoniously dry finish make it a very successful representative of its price segment.

faq.question: What sets the Bulleit Bourbon 10 Year apart from younger Bulleit releases?
faq.answer: The Bulleit Bourbon 10 Year is one of the few bourbons in the core range with an age statement, which provides greater complexity and depth. The extended maturation results in more intense oak notes, pronounced dryness, and a longer finish compared to younger expressions.

faq.question: How does the high rye content in Bulleit Bourbon 10 Year influence its flavor?
faq.answer: With 28% rye in the mash bill, Bulleit Bourbon 10 Year delivers a noticeable spiciness that manifests as peppery and slightly sharp notes. This rye spice balances the sweet vanilla and caramel flavors and contributes to the characteristic dryness and complexity on the palate.

faq.question: Why has there been uncertainty about the original origin of Bulleit Bourbon?
faq.answer: The exact origin of Bulleit Bourbon was unclear for a long time because until 2013, the bottling was reportedly distilled by Four Roses for Diageo. It wasn’t until 2017 that Diageo opened its own Bulleit distillery in Kentucky. Additionally, a visitor center existed at the former Stitzel-Weller Distillery, which further blurred the provenance.

item.name: Ben Bracken Highland Peated
item.description: The discounter LIDL has several single malts in its range, including the Ben Bracken Highland Peated, which is described as powerful and intense. We took a closer look at the whisky.LIDL does not reveal which distillery is behind Ben Bracken. All we know is that we have a whisky from the Highlands at a bargain price in our glass. The packaging is brown, which distinguishes it from the other whiskies in the series. There is also a Highland whisky, an Islay Scotch and a bottling from Speyside.

tasting_notes.nose: Peated and smoky are hardly found here. Earthy and maritime-salty aromas are present. Apple, vanilla and caramel resonate, but are rather weak.
tasting_notes.palate: This scotch tastes extraordinarily thin and sweet. You have to search again to find the promised peat-smoke. We detect a maritime saltiness and a slightly peppery aroma. All in all, not unpleasant.
tasting_notes.finish: A trace of smoke and maritime saltiness lingers for a medium length.

conclusion.marcel: Those expecting a smoky flavor explosion will be disappointed – the Ben Bracken Highland Peated is more of a delicate companion with a slight salty note and sweet hints. For the low price, it’s certainly worth enjoying now and then, as long as you don’t expect too much.
conclusion.sascha: The promised intensity and smoke fall well short with the Ben Bracken Highland Peated; the whisky shows a weak structure with a thin texture and unbalanced sweetness. It may be acceptable in the low-budget segment, but true fans of peaty, smoky Highland whiskies should keep looking.

faq.question: Why is the Ben Bracken Highland Peated hardly smoky despite its name?
faq.answer: Although 'Peated' is in the name, the Ben Bracken Highland Peated only displays very subtle smoke and peat notes. Instead, earthy, maritime, and slightly salty aromas dominate, while the smoke remains more in the background.

faq.question: Which distillery is behind the Ben Bracken Highland Peated?
faq.answer: The exact distillery is not disclosed by LIDL. The whisky is sold as an independent bottling by Clydesdale and originates from the Scottish Highlands.

faq.question: How does the Ben Bracken Highland Peated differ in taste from other Islay whiskies?
faq.answer: Compared to typical Islay whiskies, the Ben Bracken Highland Peated is significantly less smoky and peaty. It comes across as thin, cloyingly sweet with maritime and slightly peppery notes, rather than the intense smokiness associated with Islay.

item.name: Glen Elgin 12 Years
item.description: Glen Elgin, one of Scotland's lesser-known distilleries, has been distilling whisky since around 1900. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. After being closed several times, the distillery has been producing its own bottlings since its reopening in 1998, in addition to a lion's share which is processed in blends.Apart from special releases and anniversary bottlings, the 12-years single malt is the only permanently available bottling of the distillery. A reason to try it.

tasting_notes.nose: The Scotch opens with hazelnuts and almonds before a fruity sweetness spreads. Notes of toffee caramel and marzipan round off the composition.
tasting_notes.palate: Plum and toffee are the base notes we taste. Accents of malty sweetness, marzipan and spicy oak shape the overall picture.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish is very dry but still pleasant. Dark chocolate with a touch of oak.

conclusion.marcel: A wonderfully approachable Speyside whisky that delights with its nutty sweetness and dry finish, offering genuine drinking pleasure. Glen Elgin 12 shows character without any fuss – ideal for those who appreciate uncomplicated enjoyment with a hint of spice.
conclusion.sascha: The Glen Elgin 12 presents itself as a balanced single malt with a clear structure between sweet and spicy notes. The combination of malty depth and a dry finish makes it a solid representative of Speyside, though it could benefit from a bit more aromatic complexity.

faq.question: How does Glen Elgin 12 years differ from other Speyside whiskies?
faq.answer: Glen Elgin 12 years is distinguished by its nutty aromas of hazelnut and almond, accompanied by a fruity sweetness featuring toffee caramel and marzipan. Compared to other Speyside whiskies, its finish is dry and slightly bitter, with notes of dark chocolate and oak.

faq.question: Why is Glen Elgin 12 years the only permanently available bottling from the distillery?
faq.answer: The Glen Elgin distillery primarily produces whisky for blends. Aside from occasional special releases and anniversary editions, the 12-years single malt is the only regularly available single cask bottling, which makes it special and ensures consistent quality.

faq.question: How should Glen Elgin 12 years be best enjoyed to fully experience its aromas?
faq.answer: Glen Elgin 12 years reveals its complex aromas best at room temperature. A glass without ice allows you to detect hazelnut, almonds, toffee, and marzipan. Optionally, a few drops of water can be added to highlight the spice and oak notes.

item.name: Suntory Hibiki Harmony
item.description: From the Yamazaki distillery, pioneer of Japanese whisky, there are some high-priced bottlings. Named after the region, the Suntory Yamazaki distillery is both the best known among Japanese whisky distilleries and number one in production volume and exports.The blended Suntory Hibiki Harmony comes in a wonderful bottle. The 24 indentations each represent an hour of the day and each month of the Japanese lunar calendar. The bottle label is made of handmade paper and radiates an especially high quality. We are curious about the content.

tasting_notes.nose: The malt-grain mix reminds us on the nose of parfumed, slightly burnt fried potatoes with Provencial herbs. Soft and floral, the whisky transitions into an herbaceous note reminiscent of thyme and rosemary. Subtle hints of oak can be detected.
tasting_notes.palate: The flavours in the mouth are predominantly sweet. Opening with honey and overly sweet orange, the Japanese whisky tingles slightly spicy-woody on the back of the tongue. In the middle part, we taste apricot and a bit of milk roll. All in all, very round and balanced, but not a taste explosion.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is very soft, surprisingly short and has hints of light oak.

conclusion.marcel: This bottle not only looks stylish, it’s also fun to drink – light, sweet, and pleasantly smooth. However, the Hibiki Harmony is nothing more than a nice everyday whisky; it simply lacks that certain something that really knocks you off your feet. Given the price, we expected more character.
conclusion.sascha: The Suntory Hibiki Harmony offers a clean, balanced structure with clear sweetness and subtle herbal notes, but it remains rather one-dimensional and short on the finish. Technically solid but without complex depth or innovative accents, it feels somewhat overrated in the premium segment. Ideal for connoisseurs who appreciate balance but don’t expect a flavor explosion.

faq.question: What does the name “Hibiki Harmony” mean and how is it reflected in the whisky?
faq.answer: “Hibiki” means “sound” or “resonance” in Japanese and symbolizes the harmony of different aromas. The blend combines malt and grain whiskies, creating a balanced, gentle flavor profile that reflects the balance and diversity of Japanese whisky craftsmanship.

faq.question: Why does the Suntory Hibiki Harmony bottle have 24 indentations?
faq.answer: The 24 indentations on the bottle represent the 24 hours of the day as well as the months of the Japanese lunar calendar. This design connects Japanese tradition with the contents and emphasizes the appreciation of time and nature in the production process.

faq.question: How does the Hibiki Harmony differ in taste from other Japanese whiskies from the Yamazaki distillery?
faq.answer: The Hibiki Harmony is a blended whisky that, through the combination of malt and grain whiskies, offers a softer, sweeter, and more balanced note than typical single malts from the distillery. It features floral and herbal aromas with mild spice, without the intensity of a pure malt whisky.

item.name: Ben Bracken Speyside
item.description: The discounter LIDL has several single malts in its range, including the Ben Bracken Speyside, which is described as light and fruity. We took a closer look at the whisky.LIDL does not reveal which distillery is behind Ben Bracken. All we know is that we have a whisky from the Scottish Speyside at a bargain price in our glass. The packaging is dark red, which distinguishes it from the other whiskies in the series. There is also a Highland peated, a Highland unpeated and an Islay Scotch.

tasting_notes.nose: The Speysider opens remarkably voluminous, smelling of beeswax candles, pear and apple and especially without strong alcoholic sharpness. In the background a maltiness and dried straw. The smell reminds a little of hay bales.
tasting_notes.palate: The beeswax also continues in the taste. At the same time creamy and syrupy, but still tasting of mealy apples, the aromas fan out further. In the background, hay again and, with a little imagination, a ripe banana.
tasting_notes.finish: An indefinable and unfortunately disgusting note in the finish ruins the medium-long and otherwise nut-cake tasting finish.

conclusion.marcel: The Ben Bracken Speyside surprises with its creamy honey note and a fruity touch that immediately invites you to keep drinking – until the finish unfortunately ruins it all. For a discount store whisky, it’s quite decent, but the odd finish takes the wind out of the sails. If you’re looking for uncomplicated enjoyment, it’s worth a look, but not a must.
conclusion.sascha: The Ben Bracken Speyside offers an appealing balance of waxy and fruity aromas at a moderate 40% ABV, but shows weaknesses in the finish quality, which is marred by an indefinable note that clouds the overall impression. The lack of an age statement and the moderate aromatic depth suggest this is a relatively young whisky that, despite some good elements in flavor, doesn’t rise above the average. For beginners on a budget, it’s worth an optional try, but for connoisseurs, it’s not very exciting.

faq.question: Which distillery is behind the Ben Bracken Speyside whisky?
faq.answer: The producer of Ben Bracken Speyside is not officially disclosed by LIDL. The whisky comes from the Speyside region and is distributed by the independent bottler Clydesdale.

faq.question: How can the taste of Ben Bracken Speyside be described?
faq.answer: The whisky shows aromas of beeswax, pear, apple, and maltiness with a hint of hay and dried straw. On the palate, the beeswax note continues, complemented by mealy apples, creamy and syrupy textures, and a subtle banana. The finish is medium-length with nutty notes but is accompanied by an indefinable, unpleasant note.

faq.question: How is Ben Bracken Speyside positioned compared to other LIDL single malts?
faq.answer: Ben Bracken Speyside is the fruity and lighter representative among LIDL’s single malts. It differs from other variants like the Highland Peated, Highland Unpeated, and the Islay Scotch by its brighter, fruitier aroma profile and its packaging in dark red.

item.name: North British 15 Years (2003) Mossburn No. 24
item.description: In addition to all the single malts, every now and then there is a single grain Scotch whisky that wants to be tasted. Mossburn, a relatively unknown brand in Germany, bottles Whiskys from all regions of Scotland.This bottling No. 24 is from a grain distillery in Edinburgh - North British. This distillery produces primarily for blends and is the main component of many of the world's best-known brands of blended Scotch whisky. Reason enough to try the raw material.

tasting_notes.nose: Hay-like and smelling of fresh straw, we can't help but think of a mown meadow. Notes of fresh, juicy grass and a flower meadow are also recognisable. Dominant is a sugary sweetness of white chocolate that coats the other aromas.
tasting_notes.palate: The 15-years single grain whisky opens really delicately. Matured in American hogsheads, it brings vanilla, lots - really lots - of sugar and a little oak. Almost sugar syrup. A little deeper lies a slightly peppery spice that sets a nice accent.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish coats the palate with a crust of sugar. A small acidic touch is also noticeable.

conclusion.marcel: For those who have had their fill of classic single malts and are craving something sweet with character, the North British 15 Year offers a surprisingly charming alternative. Its vanilla sweetness and a hint of pepper invite you to take another sip — perfect for relaxed moments of enjoyment away from the mainstream.
conclusion.sascha: Structurally, the North British 15 Year shows a balanced harmony between sweet vanilla notes and a light spiciness typical of American hogsheads. Despite its pleasant mildness, it lacks the complexity needed to rise above the good average.

faq.question: What distinguishes a single grain whisky like the North British 15 Year from a single malt?
faq.answer: Single grain whisky is made from various grains, often including corn or wheat alongside barley, and is distilled in column stills. In contrast, single malt whisky consists exclusively of malted barley and is distilled in pot stills. The North British 15 Year is a single grain from the Lowlands region, giving it a lighter, often sweeter and milder character.

faq.question: How does maturation in American hogsheads influence the flavor of this whisky?
faq.answer: American hogsheads are casks made from former bourbon barrels, usually American white oak, which impart vanilla and sugary notes to the whisky. In the North British 15 Year, this cask type contributes pronounced vanilla sweetness, delicate oak aromas, and a syrupy texture that shape the tasting experience.

faq.question: Why is the North British 15 Year mainly used in blends, and why is the single bottling worthwhile?
faq.answer: The North British distillery primarily produces grain whisky for blended Scotch, as it provides a neutral base with sweet and mild flavors. The single bottling offers whisky enthusiasts the chance to experience the raw spirit unadulterated and to discover the subtle nuances of this grain whisky from Edinburgh that often get lost in blends.

item.name: Oban 14 Years
item.description: The Oban distillery is one of the oldest, one of the smaller and one of the few distilleries in Scotland that produces whisky close to the coast. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. The Oban 14 is, apart from a special release and a limited edition, the only Oban with an age statement.As you would expect with an age of almost a quarter of a millenium, the distillery has had several owners and has had to be foreclosed on twice. For a time it even belonged to the Patterson brothers, the perpetrators of the eponymous catastrophic collapse of the whisky industry in the late 1800s.

tasting_notes.nose: The Scotch opens with fruity notes of mango and peach. This is followed by a little spicy notes of tropical wood. A salty aroma and a hint of smoke resonate. Unexpectedly interesting.
tasting_notes.palate: The first sip of this whisky from the western Highlands reminds us of apricot, fruit preserves, rapeseed honey and white chocolate. Really sweet, you could say. The sweetness is then displaced by a salty-mineral and at the same time citrusy-sour blast of flavours. We think of apple cider with lemon curd.
tasting_notes.finish: Dry and salty with light oak notes, the single malt lingers for some time before disappearing without a trace.

conclusion.marcel: Oban 14 Years is like a leisurely stroll along the coast – not too rough, not too sweet, but just the right amount of maritime charm with a creamy drinkability that keeps inviting you back for another sip. A whisky that proudly claims its place between the Highlands and Islay and simply brings enjoyment.
conclusion.sascha: The Oban 14 presents itself as a balanced single malt with a successful interplay of fruity sweetness and salty minerality, structurally well balanced. Despite minor imperfections, the whisky feels solid thanks to its clearly defined aromas and dry finish, showcasing the typical coastal influence in its profile.

faq.question: How does the Oban 14 Years differ from other Highland whiskies?
faq.answer: Oban 14 Years is unique because it is distilled close to the coast, resulting in a salty-mineral note typical of maritime whiskies. Additionally, it combines fruity sweetness with a lightly smoky and spicy complexity, setting it apart from many other Highland whiskies.

faq.question: Why is the Oban 14 Years the only Oban with an age statement?
faq.answer: Besides special releases and limited editions, Oban mainly offers whiskies without an age statement. The 14-years Oban is the only regular single malt from the distillery with an age statement, making it a consistent and representative expression of the distillery.

faq.question: What flavors can be expected from the Oban 14 Years on the palate?
faq.answer: On the palate, Oban 14 Years shows a combination of sweet notes like apricot, rapeseed honey, and white chocolate, complemented by a salty-mineral and lemony-citrus freshness. The finish is dry, salty, and slightly woody, emphasizing its maritime character.

item.name: Lagavulin 11 Years Offerman Edition
item.description: In a sitcom, Nick Offerman plays the director of a parks department who also idolises whisky from the Lagavulin distillery. At the same time, Nick Offerman is the one who is supposed to introduce Americans to the peaty Islay malt that is so popular in Europe.The special aspect of this limited and meanwhile highly sought-after bottling among collectors is a four-month finish in Guinness casks. The goal is to enhance the Scotch with the spicy stout-beer flavours. Sad fact: Instead of being made in Ireland, Guinness barrels come from Maryland in the USA and are transported to Scotland by ship. A horrible eco-footprint.

tasting_notes.nose: The 11-years bottling opens malty-sweet and with aromas of smoked paprika, cinnamon, pear and mint. Of course, we also find the typical Lagavulin charred oak wood.
tasting_notes.palate: Peaty-sweet and massively dry, the Lagavulin tastes less heavy than the nose would have us expect. We are encountered with a dryness that makes us think of baking cocoa and coffee powder. Characterised by cereal aromas, the Scotch is very soft and well balanced - perhaps too soft for a Lagavulin? We perceive the Guinness cask with roasted malt aromas. Unfortunately, only in nuances.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is dry and long with a fair amount of peat smoke, as we would expect from a Lagavulin.

conclusion.marcel: The Lagavulin 11 Year Offerman Edition is an intriguing play with Guinness casks, primarily impressing with its malty-smoky nose. On the palate, it reveals itself to be surprisingly soft and restrained, which might disappoint fans of the classic Islay style. For collectors and enthusiasts seeking character with a touch of humor, it remains a worthwhile dram – even if another Lagavulin offers more power at a better price.
conclusion.sascha: This limited Lagavulin release convinces with a balanced interplay between peaty smokiness and subtle Guinness notes, though the latter are only marginally pronounced. The texture is soft and well integrated, giving the whisky an easy drinkability but at the same time lacking a bit in complexity. Overall, an interesting, if not outstanding, variant for connoisseurs who value harmonious balance and a long finish.

faq.question: What makes the Guinness cask finish in the Lagavulin 11 Year Offerman Edition special?
faq.answer: The four-month finish in Guinness casks from Maryland imparts subtle spicy stout beer aromas that complement Lagavulin’s typical peaty character. This combination is unusual, as Guinness casks are not commonly used for Scotch whisky.

faq.question: How does the taste of the Lagavulin 11 Year Offerman Edition differ from classic Lagavulin releases?
faq.answer: Compared to classic Lagavulin whiskies, this edition feels somewhat softer and less heavy due to the Guinness cask finish. Alongside the typical peat smoke, notes of roasted malt, cocoa, and coffee grounds dominate, giving the whisky an additional dry and spicy edge.

faq.question: Why is the ecological footprint of the Lagavulin 11 Year Offerman Edition considered problematic?
faq.answer: The Guinness casks are not sourced from Ireland but are produced in the USA (Maryland) and then shipped to Scotland. This long transport route generates unnecessary emissions, which is problematic from an ecological standpoint.

item.name: Roslagswhisky EKO Sherry Oloroso 2x30 (2015)-(2022)
item.description: Personally handed over by Richard Jansson, the whisky from the small distillery Norrtelje in Sweden was previously produced entirely organically. Organically grown barley from Östergötland is malted at Viking Malt in Halmstad. The unsmoked malt is mashed with yeast and water from the farm's own well and gently distilled in the distillery's copper pot stills. A sign of quality?When we see that this whisky was aged for seven years in 30-litre ex-Oloroso casks, the question inevitably arises: Why is the content of the bottle golden yellow?The question remains unanswered - perhaps also because everything is somehow not round, what we have here in the glass.

tasting_notes.nose: We smell juniper, apple, pear and an aroma we can't place. If we hadn't known it was a single malt, we would probably have guessed grappa or a fruit brandy. In general, it has to be said that the whisky doesn't smell much. We still detect a bit of freshly cut wood and a musty spiciness.
tasting_notes.palate: The whisky from Sweden is working in the mouth. At first like a wet towel, afterwards we taste significant wood glue. We also find the freshly cut wood from the nose again. Besides the alcoholic spiciness, subliminal sherry aromas come through after a while. Now and then, some fruity notes of apple and grape flash through.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is very long and massive with intense red fruits and brown sugar.

conclusion.marcel: Unfortunately, this Roslags whisky comes across more like a piece of wood to chew on than a pleasure – the finish unpleasantly dominates the flavor. Despite the lovingly crafted organic production, it lacks the charm of a well-balanced single malt, and the price is simply excessive.
conclusion.sascha: The seven-year maturation in ex-Oloroso casks leaves an unbalanced impression with dominant wood notes and a lack of aromatic depth. While the whisky does show some structure, the disproportion between the cask character and the fruity components results in a disharmonious overall profile.

faq.question: Why is the Roslags whisky golden yellow and not darker despite seven years of aging in Oloroso casks?
faq.answer: Although the whisky matured for seven years in small 30-liter ex-Oloroso casks, it displays an unusually light, golden yellow color. This can be due to the cask size, the quality of the casks, or the malt used. The exact cause remains unclear, as sherry casks typically impart a darker color to whisky.

faq.question: How does the organic production affect the flavor of the Roslags whisky EKO Sherry Oloroso?
faq.answer: The use of organically grown barley and estate water provides a natural foundation that makes the whisky taste clean and unadulterated. Nevertheless, the whisky exhibits rather unusual aromas that are less typical of classic single malts, which can be attributed to the combination of raw materials and distillation.

faq.question: What special characteristics arise from aging this whisky in 30-liter casks?
faq.answer: Small 30-liter casks offer a larger surface area relative to their volume, which intensifies maturation. This can lead to stronger wood flavors and faster cask influence. In this whisky’s case, intense wood notes and a pronounced sherry character are evident despite the comparatively light color.

item.name: Lochlea Harvest Edition (First Crop)
item.description: The Lochlea distillery plans four special bottlings a year. The Harvest Edition follows the Lochlea Sowing Edition and is intended to embody a fruity, autumnal profile.A trio of casks, namely port, first-fill Oloroso sherry and first-fill bourbon lend the whisky its golden colour. We find the package design in the style of the distillery's other releases, as well as the bottle design, outstanding.

tasting_notes.nose: The Harvest Edition opens with apple juice. Damn apple juice. Aromas of fresh pear, maple syrup, malty sweetness and some grain and hay in the background unfold. Maybe a little nutty and kind of like a horse stable. Candy strawberries and red wine notes are also in the mix.
tasting_notes.palate: Also: apple juice. This is followed by fresh, sugared strawberries on milk chocolate. Underneath, a few dry grasses, nutty notes and a pile of a mixture of butter biscuits and oat biscuits.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is very strong vinous and distinctly oaky spicy.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that simply delights with its fruity apple charm and sweet strawberry-chocolate notes. Sure, it’s not perfectly balanced yet, but that’s exactly what makes it so likeable and genuine – an autumnal treat for relaxed evenings.
conclusion.sascha: The Lochlea Harvest Edition displays a solid structure with pronounced fruity sweetness and a complex cask combination that nicely highlights the Lowlands character. Although the aromatic profile isn’t fully refined yet, the harmonious overall concept convinces in this young three-years whisky.

faq.question: How do the types of casks used influence the taste of the Lochlea Harvest Edition?
faq.answer: The combination of port wine, first-fill Oloroso sherry, and first-fill bourbon casks gives the whisky a complex aroma profile with fruity notes, malty sweetness, and a pronounced oak character on the finish.

faq.question: What distinguishes the Lochlea Harvest Edition from the Sowing Edition?
faq.answer: The Harvest Edition is characterized by a fruity, autumnal flavor profile, whereas the Sowing Edition emphasizes different seasonal aromas. Both are part of the distillery’s annual special releases, each with its own distinct character.

faq.question: How old is the Lochlea Harvest Edition and what is its alcohol content?
faq.answer: The Lochlea Harvest Edition is 3 years old and has an alcohol content of 46% ABV.

item.name: Talisker 10 Years
item.description: Everyone knows it or has at least seen it in the spirits section of a well-stocked supermarket. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. Regular offers invite you to buy the Talisker 10 and we like to buy it and contribute to it being the best-selling whisky of the Talisker distillery.On the bottle in the new design, the Isle of Skye off the west coast of Scotland can be found in overdramatic images. The look runs like a red leitmotif through the other bottlings of the distillery, such as the magnificent Talisker 18 Years.

tasting_notes.nose: What do you expect with the rugged coastline of the Isle of Sky? Exactly - salt, seawater, rough seas. The sea air is accompanied by light cold smoke and iodine. Pleasant citrus aromas and an underlying fruitiness - we're thinking pear - round off the profile of this classic.
tasting_notes.palate: "It's kind of everything," says Marcel. There is no lack of different impressions in the taste. There are malty-sweet and vanilla aromas, as well as salty and peppery-sharp moments. We also recognise the pear from the nose. Leathery hints and accompanying peat smoke are also present.
tasting_notes.finish: Spicy, peppery and with a tart saltiness, the 10-years Scotch does not burn unpleasantly on the palate. We think here of the fiery burn of a chilli.

conclusion.marcel: A true all-rounder with character that’s fun to drink while remaining surprisingly complex. At under 30 euros, it’s an absolute recommendation that enriches any home bar – Talisker 10 is simply a pleasure with a rugged soul and a touch of humor in the finish.
conclusion.sascha: The Talisker 10 impresses with its balanced harmony of smoky spice, salty freshness, and a subtle fruity note. Technically precise, with a clear structure and a long-lasting, peppery finish, it offers excellent value for money in the maritime single malt category.

faq.question: What makes Talisker 10 years old special compared to other single malts?
faq.answer: Talisker 10 years old is distinguished by its maritime character, originating from the Isle of Skye. Typical are salty, iodine-rich aromas combined with light peat smoke and a complex flavor mix of malt, vanilla, pepper, and fruity nuances like pear.

faq.question: How should Talisker 10 years old best be enjoyed to experience all its aromas?
faq.answer: To experience the full range of aromas, it’s recommended to drink Talisker 10 years old neat or with a few drops of water. The water opens up the aromas and softens the alcohol, while ice can mute the flavor.

faq.question: Why does Talisker 10 years old have such a distinct salty and peppery note?
faq.answer: The salty note comes from the distillery’s proximity to the rugged coast of the Isle of Skye, where the sea air influences maturation. The peppery sharpness is typical of Talisker and arises from the special combination of peat smoke and the yeasts used during distillation.

item.name: Caol Ila 12 Years
item.description: Caol Ila is one of the ten largest distilleries in Scotland and by far the largest on the island of Islay. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. Nevertheless, a permanently available series of standard bottlings has only been on the market since the early 2000s. We taste the classic.Actually, a large part of Caol Ila’s production ends up in blends, such as the Black Bottle or Johnnie Walker. The own bottlings have become more and more popular in recent years. With good reason?

tasting_notes.nose: The 12-years single malt opens with a cold medicinal smoke. Campfires and burnt-out coals come to mind. Light citrus notes follow, as well as mineral notes. The scotch smells very clean. Not intense - rather restrained.
tasting_notes.palate: This Islay malt opens almost creamy and surprisingly sweet. Later, the smokiness takes over. Very pleasant notes of sea asparagus and oatmeal are on the tongue. Light, not intrusive and maritime, all parts of the palate are reached.
tasting_notes.finish: Phenolic citrus fruits and a slightly spicy oak note remain. The smoke lingers in the medium-long finish.

conclusion.marcel: Anyone looking for an uncomplicated Islay malt with character will definitely find it here. The Caol Ila 12 Year charmingly plays with smoke and sweetness without overwhelming the palate – a genuine pleasure for any occasion and a welcome invitation into the peaty world of the island.
conclusion.sascha: Caol Ila 12 Year impresses with its balanced interplay between smoky and maritime notes, combined with a creamy texture and a clearly defined finish. A technically solid Islay single malt that appeals to both newcomers and seasoned enthusiasts, without offering complex depth.

faq.question: Why is Caol Ila 12 Year relatively restrained in smokiness despite its Islay origin?
faq.answer: Caol Ila 12 Year exhibits moderate smokiness because the distillery maintains a balanced style. Compared to other Islay whiskies like Laphroaig or Ardbeg, its phenol content is lower, resulting in a cleaner, less intense smoke profile.

faq.question: What role does the maritime influence play in Caol Ila 12 Year?
faq.answer: The maritime influence is especially evident in the mineral and salty notes as well as the flavor of sea asparagus. The proximity to Islay’s coastline shapes the whisky’s aroma and taste, imparting a light, fresh sea breeze character.

faq.question: How does Caol Ila 12 Year differ from the blends in which it is used?
faq.answer: While Caol Ila 12 Year as a single malt offers a clear, slightly sweet, and maritime character with moderate smokiness, the whisky is often used in blends like Johnnie Walker or Black Bottle to contribute smoke and peat notes and enhance complexity without being dominant.

item.name: Royal Lochnagar 12 Years
item.description: Royal Lochnagar is one of the smallest distilleries in Scotland. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. In addition to the 12-years single malt, the distillery's two pot stills also produce a large amount which is used in the Johnnie Walker Blue Label.Royal Whisky? The story told is that shortly after the opening of the distillery, Queen Victoria and Prince Albert were invited by the owner at the time. The British royal family had just bought nearby Balmoral Castle (1848). Later, the distillery became a purveyor to the court and was allowed to bear the suffix Royal, which was even confirmed again in 2021 by a Royal Warrant of Appointment.

tasting_notes.nose: We perceive sweet brown sugar, wood chips and citrus fruits. Light smoke lies unobtrusively, but nevertheless well recognisable in the background. A very exciting note of liquorice resonates.
tasting_notes.palate: The Scotch opens sweet, then dry and with minimal smoke. Licorice notes can also be perceived here. All in all, the composition reminds us of suede care products. The flavours are very defined, slightly oily and wonderfully rounded.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish flatters the palate, but is still strong and brings along minimal bitterness.

conclusion.marcel: Royal Lochnagar 12 delivers a pleasantly smooth and rounded drinking experience that also appeals to whisky newcomers – a charming Highlander without sharp edges, but with a sweet, lightly smoky character that’s enjoyable without being overwhelming.
conclusion.sascha: This 12-years Highland malt impresses with a balanced profile featuring clearly defined licorice and citrus notes, while the subtle smokiness and minimal bitter finish provide a harmonious structure – straightforward yet solid complexity at a medium level.

faq.question: Why does Royal Lochnagar carry the "Royal" designation in its name?
faq.answer: Royal Lochnagar earned the "Royal" designation because shortly after its opening, the distillery hosted Queen Victoria and Prince Albert. At that time, the royal family had purchased the nearby Balmoral Castle. Since then, Royal Lochnagar has been an official supplier to the royal household, a status confirmed by a Royal Warrant of Appointment in 2021.

faq.question: How does the small size of the distillery influence the taste of Royal Lochnagar 12 years?
faq.answer: As one of Scotland’s smallest distilleries, Royal Lochnagar produces its whisky using only two pot stills, allowing precise control over the distillation process. The result is a characterful single malt with balanced sweetness, gentle smokiness, and well-defined notes such as licorice and citrus.

faq.question: What role does Royal Lochnagar play in Johnnie Walker Blue Label?
faq.answer: A significant portion of the whisky produced at the Royal Lochnagar distillery is used as a component in the Johnnie Walker Blue Label blend. This means that Royal Lochnagar’s distinctive flavor plays an important role in this premium blended Scotch.

item.name: Cragganmore 12 Years
item.description: Cragganmore focuses on the production of single malt whisky for blends. Some of the whisky from the Speysider distillery can be found, for example, in the Johnnie Walker Green Label and the Johnnie Walker Blue Label. So are the distillery's own bottlings to be enjoyed at all?The Cragganmore 12 belongs to the Classic Malts of Scotland. The distillery regularly bottles a Distillers Edition of the same. Unless a special release or a rare 25-years bottling is released, we have to be satisfied with the first two.

tasting_notes.nose: We get a strong smell of honey and butterscotch in the nose. After a while, we notice malty cereal notes in the middle part, while floral notes opening up a little further back.
tasting_notes.palate: Our first impression here is semolina pudding with pear puree - very intense. The Scotch is malty sweet with a good portion of vanilla. Characterised by pleasant oak, we have a discreet wood smoke, which is rounded off by cereal notes.
tasting_notes.finish: We would describe the sweet-bitter, slightly woody-spicy finish as a short trip rather than a long journey.

conclusion.marcel: The Cragganmore 12 is a charming everyday companion with its sweet, malty profile and subtle smoke that never becomes intrusive. For a whisky mostly used in blends, it surprises with pleasant drinkability and a hint of pear pudding – not spectacular, but likeable. A solid introduction to the world of Speyside that could certainly show a bit more personality.
conclusion.sascha: With a balanced interplay of sweet vanilla notes, malty-grainy structure, and a delicate touch of oak, the Cragganmore 12 presents itself as a technically clean Speyside malt. The finish is rather short and lightly woody-spicy, which makes the whisky clearly structured but somewhat unremarkable – ideal for fans of classic, straightforward single malts who aren’t looking for complex dram experiences.

faq.question: Why is Cragganmore 12 often used in blended whiskies?
faq.answer: Cragganmore 12 provides a balanced base with its malty, honey, and butter toffee notes, adding complexity and flavor diversity to blends like Johnnie Walker Green and Blue Label.

faq.question: How does Cragganmore 12 differ from other Speyside whiskies?
faq.answer: Cragganmore 12 stands out through its combination of malty sweetness, floral notes, and a subtle wood smoke, making it somewhat more complex and smoky than many other Speyside whiskies, which tend to be lighter and fruitier.

faq.question: What makes the finish of Cragganmore 12 special?
faq.answer: The finish is sweet-bitter and lightly woody-spicy, rather short and crisp, making it a pleasant experience without feeling too long or overwhelming.

item.name: Glenkinchie 12 Years
item.description: Glenkinchie 12 is one of only three bottlings offered by the distillery. Besides this, only the Distillers Edition and a variant of the 12-years in cask strength are available. The latter can only be purchased at the distillery itself.Glenkichie, also called the Edinburgh Malt, is produced in the immediate vicinity of the Scottish capital. The 12-years bottling is the only Lowlands representative among the Classic Malts. Some of the distillery's buildings are under the protection of historical buildings.

tasting_notes.nose: On the nose, the malt meets us with floral freshness. A malty sweetness goes hand in hand with the scent of freshly grated lemon peel. The aroma on the nose is completed by the scent of hay and mown grass.
tasting_notes.palate: On the palate, Glenkinchie 12 continues to present a strong, malty sweetness. This is joined by very appropriately slightly bitter spiciness. It distances itself somewhat from the lemon aroma of the nose. We continue to think of freshly mown Scottish greenery, complemented by notes of apples which were not quite finished ripening. The fresh sweet flavors do not overwhelm the palate.
tasting_notes.finish: Finally, the scotch finishes briefly and succinctly. With slightly bitter oak, in keeping with the previous flavor not obtrusively intense.

conclusion.marcel: A pleasantly light malt that, with its floral freshness and malty sweetness, leaves you wanting more – perfect for relaxed evenings without any fuss. The Glenkinchie 12 is like a friendly companion who charmingly wins over even newcomers without being overbearing.
conclusion.sascha: The Glenkinchie 12 impresses with a balanced interplay between malty sweetness and a subtly bitter spiciness typical of a Lowland malt. Its structure remains straightforward, with the short finish and restrained citrus notes making the character feel more light-footed than complex.

faq.question: What makes the Glenkinchie 12 Year old special among Lowland whiskies?
faq.answer: The Glenkinchie 12 Year is the only representative of the Lowland region among the Classic Malts. Its floral freshness and the combination of malty sweetness with lemon and hay notes clearly set it apart from other Lowland whiskies.

faq.question: How does maturation influence the flavors of the Glenkinchie 12 Year old?
faq.answer: The 12-year aging in oak casks creates a balanced harmony between malty sweetness and gentle bitterness. The cask imparts subtle woody notes in the finish without overshadowing the fresh, floral aromas.

faq.question: Why is Glenkinchie 12 Year often called the 'Edinburgh Malt'?
faq.answer: Glenkinchie is located very close to Scotland’s capital, Edinburgh. Because of this geographical proximity, the whisky is often referred to as the 'Edinburgh Malt,' highlighting its regional identity.

item.name: Rock Island Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing
item.description: This blend of the famous independent bottler Douglas Laing offers us a composition of the characteristic and absolutely unmistakable aromas of the Hebrides.Douglas Laing provides us with independently bottled blends. Founded back in the 1940s, Douglas Laing distributes a variety of different series in which single malts are blended and thematically ordered. Within the Remarkable Regional Malts, Rock Island represents the Scottish islands. Fun Fact: Originally distributed as Rock Oyster, the bottling was renamed to Rock Island, as oysters had a negative connotation for many customers.

image.alt: Rock Island Small Batch Release - Douglas Laing

tasting_notes.nose: At first, we are met by a pleasant portion of peat smoke. Slighty hidden there is a wonderful sweetness. Malt and vanilla combine with fresh citrus notes. The classic maritime aroma of the Hebrides.
tasting_notes.palate: Like a proper gulp of seawater, Rock Island lies on the palate with an interesting saltiness. Spices and smoke provide a mouth-filling aroma. Citrus freshness and vanilla take a back seat, but are clearly perceptible.
tasting_notes.finish: As expected, the spice and smoke provide a lasting finish. The typical maritime aroma of the Scottish islands remains.

conclusion.marcel: Rock Island is like a stroll along a windy beach – pleasantly smoky with a sweet, malty embrace. Not a groundbreaking dram, but certainly charming and easy-drinking for a relaxed evening by the sea.
conclusion.sascha: The Rock Island Small Batch impresses with a harmonious balance between peaty smoke, maritime saltiness, and subtle citrus notes. Despite lacking sharp edges, the blend offers a solid structure that is typical of the Hebrides region.

faq.question: What makes the Rock Island Small Batch Release from Douglas Laing special compared to other Hebridean whiskies?
faq.answer: Rock Island combines typical Hebridean aromas with a strong maritime character, shaped by peat smoke, salty sea air, and spicy notes. The blend comes from Douglas Laing, an independent bottler known for their thematically curated regional malts.

faq.question: Why was the whisky originally called Rock Oyster and then renamed?
faq.answer: The whisky was originally named Rock Oyster, but due to many customers’ negative associations with oysters, Douglas Laing changed the name to Rock Island to place greater emphasis on the Scottish islands and their distinctive flavors.

faq.question: How does the 46.8% alcohol content influence the tasting experience of this whisky?
faq.answer: At 46.8% ABV, Rock Island has enough strength to clearly convey the complex aromas of peat smoke, spice, and maritime notes without being too harsh. The alcohol level delivers an intense yet balanced flavor and a lasting finish.

item.name: The Singleton Of Dufftown 12 Years
item.description: The Singleton of Dufftown 12 year is one of the few bottlings with an age statement of more than 10 years that can be purchased for under 30€. As part of the Classic Malts Selection, it becomes even more interesting when viewed in the context of the full lineup. It is part of the core range (12, 15 and 18 years) of the Dufftown distillery.The three distilleries Dufftown, Glen Ord, and Glendullan produce whiskies under the label The Singleton. While The Singleton of Glen Ord is intended for the Asian market, The Singleton of Glendullan targets the North American market and The Singleton of Dufftown belongs to the European market.

tasting_notes.nose: As expected, the "Luscious Nectar" smells malty-sweet, fruity, slightly honeyed perhaps and very soft. Other aromas we detect are nuts and latte, as well as green apple. Really pleasant.
tasting_notes.palate: Thin, delicate and oily, the whisky floats in the mouth and tingles a little on the tongue. The fruitiness is pronounced here, as well. The apple in particular stands out. Orange toffee, roasted nuts and brown sugar round off the less complex but quaffable single malt. Every now and then, the alcohol bites a little, despite the low 40% abv.
tasting_notes.finish: The finish is rather short and combines sweet cane sugar syrup with slightly more dominant oaky bitterness.

conclusion.marcel: The Singleton Of Dufftown 12 Years is a relaxed companion for summer evenings and beginners who don’t want any fuss. Its subtle sweetness and soft mouthfeel simply invite you to enjoy — an easy sipper that even perks up with a small fruit garnish. Not spectacular, but friendly and straightforward.
conclusion.sascha: A solid Speyside whisky with balanced fruitiness and gentle maltiness, though it falls a bit short in complexity and finish length. The Singleton Of Dufftown 12 Years impresses with its clear structure and pleasant balance, but remains a rather light representative of its category without much depth. Recommended for beginners seeking an accessible and harmonious whisky.

faq.question: What distinguishes The Singleton of Dufftown 12 Years from other single malts from Speyside?
faq.answer: The Singleton of Dufftown 12 Years is characterized by its mild maltiness and fruity notes, featuring green apple, orange toffee, and roasted nuts. Compared to other Speyside whiskies, it is less complex but very approachable and soft in taste.

faq.question: Why does The Singleton of Dufftown 12 Years sometimes have a slight burning sensation on the tongue despite its 40% alcohol content?
faq.answer: Although the alcohol content is relatively low at 40%, the slight tingling or burning can come from the whisky’s thin and oily-delicate texture, which can make the alcohol seem a bit more concentrated, especially given its less complex profile.

faq.question: For which market is The Singleton of Dufftown 12 Years primarily produced, and how does this differ from the other Singleton expressions?
faq.answer: The Singleton of Dufftown 12 Years is mainly produced for the European market. In contrast, The Singleton of Glen Ord is aimed at the Asian market, and The Singleton of Glendullan is intended for the North American market, which is also reflected in their flavor profiles and marketing.

item.name: Kavalan King Car 40th Anniversary Edition (2012/2020)
item.description: To celebrate their 40th birthday the King Car Group, the owner of the Kavalan Distillery, bottled as a Single Cask Limited Edition Whisky matured in exclusive red wine casks. The bottling has no age statement.The bottle comes in a 1.5 litre size and has a cap studded with Swarovski crystals, which is shaped like a crown to emphasise the exclusivity. The whole thing is packed in a high-quality wooden box. The presentation is actually quite impressive.

tasting_notes.nose: The Single Malt Whisky from Taiwan opens extremely powerful and intense. Strawberries, blueberries and cinnamon are the dominant aromas. Dark grapes, blood orange and notes of marzipan follow. In the background, there is a lasting peppery note. We also perceive other spices, but are unable to classify them exactly.
tasting_notes.palate: Very fruity-sweet, similar to good toffee fudges, the taste spreads very voluminously in the mouth. Oily and at the same time dry, we taste delicately bitter oak, orange zest and clearly perceive the wine character of the Whisky. Here, as well, the composition is accompanied by hints of a peppery note in the background and rounds off the flavour profile.
tasting_notes.finish: The Taiwanese Single Malt finishes long and sweet into a very pleasant bitter oak note.

conclusion.marcel: A whisky that truly delights with its intense fruitiness and elegant spice – almost a celebration in a glass. The lavish presentation perfectly matches the character, even if it lacks the final polish to elevate it to absolute world-class status. For connoisseurs seeking a powerful yet charming tasting experience, this comes highly recommended.
conclusion.sascha: This limited Kavalan edition impresses with a balanced interplay between intense fruit and complex spice notes, while the maturation in red wine casks adds structure to the whisky. Despite the opulent presentation, it falls just short on depth and precision to warrant a top rating. Overall, a technically solid and harmoniously composed spirit with potential.

faq.question: What special characteristics does the red wine cask maturation bring to the Kavalan King Car 40th Anniversary Edition?
faq.answer: Maturation in exclusive red wine casks imparts pronounced fruity notes such as dark grapes and blood orange, along with a distinctive wine character that enriches the flavor profile with sweet and spicy elements.

faq.question: Why does the Kavalan King Car 40th Anniversary Edition have no age statement (No Age Statement)?
faq.answer: The bottling deliberately omits an age statement to focus on the quality and character of the individual cask, which is highlighted through the limited single cask release.

faq.question: How does the high alcohol content of 56.3% affect the taste and drinking experience of this whisky?
faq.answer: The high alcohol content delivers an intense and powerful aroma, enhances spicy notes like pepper, and provides a full-bodied mouthfeel. At the same time, it can be softened with a little water to release additional aromas.

item.name: Ben Bracken Highland
item.description: The discounter LIDL has some single malts in its range, including the Ben Bracken Highland, which is described as light and elegant. We took a closer look at the Whisky.LIDL does not reveal which distillery is behind Ben Bracken. All we know is that we have a Whisky from the Highlands at a bargain price in our glass. The packaging is blue, which distinguishes it from the other Whiskys in the series. There is also an Islay Whisky, an Highland peated and a bottling from Speyside.

tasting_notes.nose: We smell glue and strong alcoholic pungency. Minimally, we can perceive caramel and sweet fruits, light malt. However, the notes are all very weak, while the alcohol is so strong that it clears the nose better than Airwaves chewing gum does.
tasting_notes.palate: Not at all as dramatic as we expected after that awful smell. Faint and thin, aromas of pear, apple and Elderberry slowly begin to spread. Unfortunately, the alcoholic pungency prevails here as well.
tasting_notes.finish: The very short finish makes us think of rotten walnuts. There is also a bit of black coffee.

conclusion.marcel: For the small price, you can’t expect a flavor explosion here – the Ben Bracken Highland is more of a tired companion, offering a sharp alcoholic bite and a thin fruit profile that hardly brings any joy to the glass. More of a last resort than a pleasure, it’s quickly forgotten.
conclusion.sascha: Structurally, the Ben Bracken Highland feels unbalanced and dominated by a harsh alcohol sharpness, while aromatically it barely delivers any depth or complexity. The finish, with its slightly unpleasant notes, reinforces the impression of a budget but disappointing Highland whisky.

faq.question: Why is the distillery behind Ben Bracken Highland not known?
faq.answer: Ben Bracken Highland is marketed as a whisky from an independent bottler called Clydesdale. The actual producer or distillery is not disclosed by LIDL, which is common practice for some discount whiskies to keep the origin confidential.

faq.question: How does the 40% alcohol content affect the taste of this whisky?
faq.answer: At 40% ABV, the Ben Bracken Highland shows a pronounced alcoholic sharpness despite its moderate strength, which is clearly noticeable both on the nose and the palate, partially overshadowing the subtle fruit and malt aromas.

faq.question: What flavor impressions can be expected from Ben Bracken Highland despite its low age?
faq.answer: Although the whisky carries no age statement, light notes of pear, apple, and elderflower appear on the palate. The finish is short and reminiscent of musty walnuts and black coffee, accompanied by a lingering alcoholic sharpness.

item.name: Blended Malt Speyside - Berry Bros. & Rudd
item.description: The family business Berry Bros & Rudd is considered to be Britain's oldest wine and spirits merchant and is also said to have stocked the Titanic with spirits. Today, the company is known among whisky lovers for its excellent blends.We have a blended malt from the Classic Range by Berry Bros & Rudd in our glass. The Classic Range includes Whiskys from the different regions of Scotland, in this case from Speyside. Of course, the company does not reveal which distilleries the different Whiskys come from.

image.alt: Berry Bros. & Rudd - Blended Malt Speyside

tasting_notes.nose: Very fruity, but not very sweet with a little vanilla. We perceive honeydew melon and mango. A little banana and oaky notes resonate. NicNacs are hiding far in the back. Yes. Really.
tasting_notes.palate: The blended malt opens very vanilla-fruity and a little candle waxy. Banana chips and orange aromas quickly follow. On top is an interesting aroma that reminds us of apple pie with vanilla ice cream.
tasting_notes.finish: The medium-long finish is a bit oaky and spicy.

conclusion.marcel: This Speyside blend offers plenty of drinking pleasure with its fruity lightness and charming vanilla notes. For a no-age-statement whisky, it surprises with a pleasant smoothness and a hint of apple pie – perfect for enjoyable evenings without any fuss. A solid companion that’s fun to drink and worth the price.
conclusion.sascha: A well-balanced blended malt from Speyside that impresses with its clearly defined fruitiness and subtle oak notes. The balance between vanilla and lightly spicy aromas works well, although the medium-length finish could benefit from a bit more depth. An attractively priced whisky with good complexity, suitable for both beginners and connoisseurs.

faq.question: What does Blended Malt mean for this Berry Bros. & Rudd Speyside whisky?
faq.answer: A blended malt consists exclusively of mixed single malts from different distilleries, without any addition of grain whisky. For this whisky, Berry Bros. & Rudd combines various Speyside malts that are not disclosed.

faq.question: How does the Speyside region influence the flavor of this whisky?
faq.answer: Speyside whiskies are known for their fruity and often vanilla aromas. This blended malt displays typical notes such as honeydew melon, mango, banana, and vanilla, which are characteristic of the region.

faq.question: Why does Berry Bros. & Rudd not reveal the distilleries used?
faq.answer: As an independent bottler, Berry Bros. & Rudd keeps the composition a secret to protect their selection and blending expertise. This way, the focus remains on the tasting experience rather than on individual distilleries.

item.name: Ardbeg An Oa
item.description: Named after the Oa Peninsula on the Isle of Islay, Ardbeg An Oa was the first addition to the standard range ten years after the Ardbeg Corryvreckan, having previously focused entirely on limited bottlings.Once again without an age statement, Ardbeg presents us with a Whisky that is even available in a limited barbecue smoker edition. A very good idea, we think, because this way you get the desired smoky flavour even without spices on the barbecue peppers.

tasting_notes.nose: Tobacco smoke, albeit slightly fruity. In the background we find a lot of subtle aromas - could be an orange or a peach. The creamy caramel that follows is joined by pine resin and wood shavings.
tasting_notes.palate: At first, we think of grilled vegetables, black tea and tobacco. An interesting combination. Further on, smoked ham, oak and fruitiness are perceptible. The oily mouthfeel is characterised by the ashes of an extinguished campfire.
tasting_notes.finish: Long, as is usual for an Ardbeg, we have an intensely smoky and very smooth and balanced finish that leaves us tasting smoked ham again.

conclusion.marcel: Ardbeg An Oa is a true Islay classic for anyone who loves smoky adventures – perfect for barbecues, but not for the faint-hearted. The smoky ham and campfire vibe deliver a drinking experience full of character, even if afterwards you hardly want to enjoy anything else.
conclusion.sascha: With its balanced blend of smoke, fruitiness, and wood, Ardbeg An Oa presents a well-crafted structure that particularly appeals to smoke lovers. The long, gentle finish makes it a complex single malt that is intense yet remains harmonious.

faq.question: What does the designation "An Oa" mean for this Ardbeg whisky?
faq.answer: The name "An Oa" refers to the Oa peninsula on the Isle of Islay, known for its wild nature and smoky character, which is reflected in the whisky’s flavor.

faq.question: How does Ardbeg An Oa differ from other Ardbeg no-age-statement releases?
